layout.layout_pattern: The page sits inside a full-bleed dark shell with a centered content container (~1140–1200px max-width, about 70–80% of viewport width) and generous side margins of dark background. Below the global site header (logo + main nav, not part of this spec), the product page begins with a slim utility row combining the product-page-controller on the left and the global-search-form on the right. Beneath this, the product-detail-header behaves as the hero section, occupying most of the initial viewport height and visually anchoring the page. Below the hero, content follows a linear top-to-bottom stack, but within the main zone it breaks into a two-column layout: a wider left column for technical information (specifications, compatibility, documents) and a narrower right column for status and commerce actions (availability-and-leadtime-block, add-to-cart-form). At the bottom, the compatible-spare-parts-panel returns to a full-width section. A floating mini cart (cart-summary-mini) sits above all content at the bottom-right of the viewport.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page sits inside a full-bleed dark shell with a centered content container (~1140–1200px max-width, about 70–80% of viewport width) and generous side margins of dark background. Below the global site header (logo + main nav, not part of this spec), the product page begins with a slim utility row combining the product-page-controller on the left and the global-search-form on the right. Beneath this, the product-detail-header behaves as the hero section, occupying most of the initial viewport height and visually anchoring the page. Below the hero, content follows a linear top-to-bottom stack, but within the main zone it breaks into a two-column layout: a wider left column for technical information (specifications, compatibility, documents) and a narrower right column for status and commerce actions (availability-and-leadtime-block, add-to-cart-form). At the bottom, the compatible-spare-parts-panel returns to a full-width section. A floating mini cart (cart-summary-mini) sits above all content at the bottom-right of the viewport.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header/utility band (product-page-controller + global-search-form) uses roughly 10–15% of the viewport height on first load, with compact but readable vertical padding (~16–24px). The product-detail-header below it occupies the largest share of the above-the-fold content, about 50–60% of the viewport height on desktop, framed by 40–64px of top and side margin from the dark shell. Vertical gaps between major sections (hero to specs, specs to related parts) are 48–64px to maintain a premium, spacious feel. Within the two-column body, the left information column spans roughly 7–8 of 12 conceptual grid columns (about 60–65% width), while the right action column spans 4–5 columns (about 35–40% width). The floating cart-summary-mini is compact, roughly the size of a small card or pill, offset ~24–32px from the bottom and right edges of the viewport.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is low and deliberate. Each major element sits in its own clearly defined block with substantial breathing room around it. Text lines are short to medium length, with comfortable margins and padding inside cards and panels (around 16–24px). The product-detail-header is visually dominant yet uncluttered, focusing on a large product visual and a small set of key facts. Below, the specs and info panels present detailed information but are grouped into distinct, visually separated sections instead of a single dense wall of data. The right column actions (availability, add to cart) are compact but not crammed, emphasizing clarity over sheer data volume. The floating cart summary remains small and minimal so it does not disturb the main focus.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F/Z pattern consistent with the mockup. The eye first lands at the top-left on the product-page-controller (e.g., model name / breadcrumb), then travels horizontally to the top-right where the global-search-form sits, forming the upper bar of the F/Z. From there, the gaze drops diagonally into the center of the screen where the product-detail-header as the hero element dominates with imagery and key details. Within that hero, the eye moves horizontally across the product image and headline content. As the user scrolls, the left column (specifications, compatibility, documents) carries the primary reading flow downward, while the right column (availability, add-to-cart) stays in peripheral view, possibly behaving as a sticky action stack. Finally, the floating cart-summary-mini at the bottom-right acts as a persistent secondary attractor, visible at the end of the visual path to encourage cart review without competing with the hero or primary CTAs.

layout.overall_description: The product detail page appears as a dark, industrial canvas with a bright, precise content column floating in the center. At the very top of the content area, just beneath the global site header, a slim, horizontally aligned utility bar stretches across the full width of the central column: on the left, controls for the current product context; on the right, a tightly styled global search field. Below this band, the page opens up into a cinematic hero-like product-detail-header: a large, central product visual and bold model name fill most of the visible area, surrounded by generous dark margins that frame the content and reinforce a premium, machine-focused character. As you scroll, the layout resolves into a clear two-column structure, with detailed specifications, compatibility information, and documents cascading down the wider left side, while the right side stacks availability status and a compact yet prominent add-to-cart card that can remain in view. Near the bottom, the columns recombine into a full-width related parts grid, echoing the hero’s emphasis with a more modular, repeated pattern of cards. Throughout the experience, a small, fixed mini-cart hovers in the bottom-right corner of the viewport, subtly lit against the charcoal background and always ready to be used, echoing the floating utility button behavior from the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-page-controller sits at the top of the main content area, directly beneath the global site header and aligned to the left within the centered content container. It forms the left half of a slim horizontal utility bar that spans the full width of the content column. This bar is about 56–64px tall, with 16–20px of vertical padding and 24–32px of horizontal padding from the container edges. The controller’s elements (back arrow, breadcrumb, model code, or view toggles) are left-aligned and occupy roughly 60–65% of the bar’s width, leaving the remaining space on the right for the global-search-form. The bar is visually subtle—dark background slightly lighter than the page shell with minimal separators—to keep focus on the hero below while still being immediately accessible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky utility bar within the main content shell: once the user scrolls past the global site header, this bar pins to the top of the viewport, ensuring quick access to navigation controls and search while the rest of the page content scrolls beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global-search-form shares the same horizontal row as the product-page-controller at the top of the content area. It is right-aligned within the centered content container, occupying roughly the rightmost 4 grid columns (about 30–35% of the content width). The search input is a single, clean field with no heavy chrome, set against the dark background with a subtle border or glow, and has comfortable internal padding (~12–16px vertical, 16–24px horizontal). There is a small horizontal gap (~16–24px) between the right edge of the product-page-controller’s content and the left edge of the search form. On large screens, the search form aligns vertically centered within this utility bar; on narrower screens, it may wrap below the controller content, spanning close to the full width while still padded by the container’s side margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives within the same sticky utility bar as the product-page-controller, staying visible at the top of the viewport as the user scrolls, without overlapping or obscuring the hero content below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-detail-header is positioned directly below the sticky utility bar, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 32–48px to give it a standalone, hero-like presence. It spans nearly the full width of the centered content container (roughly 90–100% of the container width), but is still framed by the dark side margins of the page, which take up about 10–15% of the overall viewport width on each side. Vertically, it occupies about 50–60% of the initial viewport height on desktop, creating a cinematic feel. Internally, the header is organized as a two-part layout: on the left, a large product image or render takes up around 60–65% of the header width, centered vertically with 24–32px padding around it; on the right, the remaining 35–40% of the width contains the product name, model number, a concise tagline or key specs, and a high-level price or category label. This right-hand text block is vertically aligned with the image and kept low-density, with distinct spacing between each text element (12–24px). The bottom of the header aligns horizontally with the top edge of the two-column body below, so the transition into specs and side actions feels cohesive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scroll flow: it anchors the top of the page content but scrolls naturally off-screen as the user moves down into the more detailed sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-specifications-panel appears immediately below the product-detail-header, separated by a vertical margin of about 48–64px. It forms the top section of the left column in the two-column body layout. This panel occupies roughly 7–8 of the 12 conceptual grid columns within the centered content container, or about 60–65% of the content width, starting flush from the left edge of the container. The panel is card-like but minimal: a section title at the top left, followed by tabbed content or structured tables of specifications, all aligned left with consistent 24px inner padding on all sides. The right column (containing availability and add-to-cart) begins at the same vertical line as the top of this panel, creating a clean horizontal alignment across the page. The specs panel can extend vertically as needed; as it grows longer with detailed data, it remains the primary scrolling content area that the user reads down the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element that scrolls normally; acts as the main vertical content stream while right-column actions may remain partially in view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability-and-leadtime-block is placed at the top of the right-hand column in the two-column body, horizontally aligned with the top edge of the product-specifications-panel. Within the centered content container, this right column begins after a consistent gutter of roughly 32–40px from the left column, and spans about 4–5 grid columns (roughly 35–40% of the content width). The availability block sits as the uppermost card in this column, with a compact height (approximately 120–180px depending on content) and comfortable internal padding of 16–24px. It displays stock status, lead times, and possibly location tags, arranged in a vertical stack with clear hierarchy: status label at the top, lead time in the middle, additional notes below. There is a moderate vertical gap (~24px) beneath this block before the add-to-cart-form starts, making the two blocks feel like a cohesive stack but not cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of a right-column action stack; on larger screens this block, together with the add-to-cart-form below it, can be configured to behave as a sticky group that remains visible within the viewport as the user scrolls the taller left column content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The compatibility-info-panel is placed directly below the product-specifications-panel in the left column, separated by a clear vertical gap of about 32–48px. It maintains the same left alignment and width as the specs panel, spanning roughly 7–8 grid columns. The panel reads as a secondary information card: a smaller section title at the top, followed by grouped entries indicating compatible machine models, series, or configurations. Content is arranged in a low-density list or pill-based layout with significant spacing between groups (8–16px), ensuring it feels lighter than the dense specifications above. This panel’s bottom edge aligns visually with the mid-section of the right column, reinforcing the vertical rhythm of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the vertical flow of the left column, scrolling naturally with the rest of the technical information.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The documents-list appears further down the left column, positioned directly beneath the compatibility-info-panel with a vertical separation of approximately 32–48px. It continues to use the same left-column width (about 60–65% of the content container) and aligns flush with the left edge of the specs and compatibility panels above. The section starts with a heading such as 'Documentation' followed by a vertically stacked list of downloadable documents (PDFs, manuals, drawings). Each document row spans the panel width with ample vertical padding (~12–16px per row) and a clear, high-contrast label. The list is bounded within a subtle card or divided by thin separators, avoiding visual heaviness. The total height depends on the number of documents, but consistent spacing maintains an airy, ordered feel even for longer lists.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static component that scrolls with the rest of the left column, forming the lower portion of the primary information stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The compatible-spare-parts-panel is positioned below the main two-column area, acting as a full-width section within the centered content container. It begins after a more generous vertical gap of 48–64px beneath the lowest content in either column (typically below the documents-list on the left and the add-to-cart-form on the right), visually signaling a new stage in the page. This panel spans nearly the full width of the container (about 90–100%), ignoring the previous left/right column division. Inside, compatible spare parts are arranged in a responsive grid of cards: for example, three or four columns across on large screens with equal card widths and consistent gutters (~24–32px) between them. Each card has moderate padding (16–20px) and a fixed height where possible to create a stable, industrial-feeling grid. The section title runs along the top left of the panel with plenty of breathing room above and below, echoing the hero’s typography-led style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width section at the bottom of the main content stack, forming a visually distinct band of related content before the page footer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart-form is placed directly beneath the availability-and-leadtime-block in the right-hand column, separated by a vertical spacing of around 24px so they read as a logical pair. It shares the same column width (roughly 35–40% of the content container) and aligns with the right margin of the container. The form is a compact but visually strong card, slightly taller than the availability block, wrapping quantity controls, price details, and a primary 'Add to Cart' button. Internal padding is generous (around 16–24px), with vertical spacing of at least 8–12px between form fields and the CTA. The primary button stretches close to the card’s full width, with enough surrounding whitespace to stand out against the dark theme. The top edge of this form is comfortably within the upper half of the viewport on first scroll, ensuring quick access without overwhelming the hero section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Forms the core of the right-column action stack; on larger screens, this card (often combined logically with the availability block above) can be configured to stick within the viewport while the user scrolls through long technical content on the left, always keeping the purchase action within easy reach.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-summary-mini floats above the main content as a small, unobtrusive overlay in the bottom-right corner of the viewport. It is positioned with a consistent inset of about 24–32px from the right edge and 24–32px from the bottom edge of the screen, regardless of how far the user has scrolled. Visually, it resembles a compact card or pill, approximately 220–280px wide and 56–80px tall, with subtle elevation or a soft glow to separate it from the dark background. Inside, minimal information is displayed—cart icon, item count, and a short total or a 'View Cart' label—arranged horizontally with about 8px spacing between icon and text. Its size and placement echo the floating utility button pattern from the mockup, making it noticeable but not intrusive. It does not align to the content container; instead, it anchors to the viewport for consistent access.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport as a floating element that remains visible at all times while the user scrolls. It overlays the content but is sized and positioned to avoid covering critical actions or text, and can collapse or expand on interaction if needed.

name: Shopping Cart
title: Shopping Cart
description: Displays the current shopping cart with machines and spare parts, allows quantity updates and item removal, shows order totals, and lets users request a sales follow-up or proceed to other pages.

core_features: Retrieve and display the current cart with all line items and calculated totals using getCartDetails.
Allow inline quantity changes for each cart item and update totals via updateCartItemQuantity.
Allow removal of individual items from the cart with removeCartItem.
Allow clearing all items from the cart at once using clearCart.
Show aggregated pricing information (subtotal, tax, total, currency) based on getCartDetails.totals.
Provide navigation from each cart item to its product detail page (product.html) using productId and itemType URL parameters.
Provide an option to request a sales follow-up/quote for the current cart contents using requestCartSalesFollowUp.
Provide a direct navigation path to the contact sales page (contact.html?topic=sales).
Provide access to global search across machines, spare parts, documents, and case studies using globalSearch from the header search bar.
user_workflows: View cart contents: 1) User navigates to cart.html. 2) On page load, the cart-page-loader component calls getCartDetails with no parameters. 3) The returned Cart, CartItem list, and totals object are bound to the cart-items-table and cart-summary-panel components. 4) The user sees all items, their quantities, line totals, and the aggregated totals.

Change item quantity: 1) In cart-items-table, user edits the quantity field for a line item. 2) onChangeQuantity handler validates the input and invokes updateCartItemQuantity with cartItemId and the new quantity. 3) On success, the response’s updated items and totals replace the current cart state in the page. 4) cart-items-table and cart-summary-panel re-render with the new quantities and totals. 5) cart-feedback-banner shows the message from the API response.

Remove an item: 1) In cart-items-table, user clicks a 'Remove' control for a specific cart item. 2) onClickRemoveItem handler optionally confirms the action, then calls removeCartItem with cartItemId. 3) On success, the updated Cart, CartItem list, and totals from the response replace the current state. 4) The removed line disappears, totals update, and cart-feedback-banner shows the response message. 5) If the last item is removed, cart-items-table shows an empty-state view and cart-summary-panel adjusts to zero totals.

Clear the entire cart: 1) In cart-actions-toolbar or cart-summary-panel, user clicks 'Clear cart'. 2) onClickClearCart handler optionally asks for confirmation, then calls clearCart with no parameters. 3) On success, components reset their bound Cart and CartItem data to reflect an empty cart (based on a subsequent call to getCartDetails or inferred empty state), and totals are reset. 4) cart-feedback-banner displays the success or error message from clearCart.

Request sales follow-up / quote for current cart: 1) User scrolls to the sales-followup-form (or lands on cart.html?mode=quote to focus the form). 2) User fills in name, email, and optionally company, phone, and comments. 3) On form submit, onSubmit handler validates required fields and calls requestCartSalesFollowUp with the form values. 4) On success, the component shows the response message (via cart-feedback-banner and/or inline form feedback). 5) Optionally, the form fields (except contact details, if desired) can be cleared while keeping the cart unchanged.

Navigate from cart to product details: 1) In cart-items-table, user clicks on an item name. 2) onClickItemName handler reads the CartItem.productId and CartItem.itemType and navigates to product.html?id={productId}&itemType={itemType}. 3) User views detailed product information on product.html and can later return to cart.html via site navigation.

Contact sales via contact page: 1) In cart-actions-toolbar or cart-summary-panel, user clicks 'Contact Sales'. 2) onClickContactSales navigates to contact.html?topic=sales. 3) The contact page loads with the sales topic preselected; the user can reference their cart separately if needed.

Use global search from the cart page: 1) User enters a query into the global search bar in the header. 2) onSubmitSearch in header-global-search calls globalSearch with query and selected entityTypes. 3) Search results are displayed either in an overlay or on a dedicated search results page (outside this spec), without affecting the current cart state. 4) User can navigate back to cart.html at any point.
interactions: Page load triggers getCartDetails to initialize cart data and totals.
Editing a quantity input in the cart-items-table triggers updateCartItemQuantity when the input is confirmed (e.g., blur or enter), refreshing items and totals.
Clicking a 'Remove' control on a cart row triggers removeCartItem for that cartItemId and refreshes the cart.
Clicking a 'Clear cart' control triggers clearCart and resets all cart-related components after success.
Submitting the sales-followup-form triggers requestCartSalesFollowUp with the user’s contact details and comments, then displays the returned message.
Clicking a cart item’s name triggers navigation to product.html with id and itemType taken from the corresponding CartItem.
Clicking a 'Contact Sales' link triggers navigation to contact.html?topic=sales.
Typing into and submitting the header-global-search form triggers globalSearch with the query and selected entityTypes, without mutating cart data.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is derived from the getCartDetails response and is not encoded in URL parameters. URL parameters are used for navigation context and optional UI modes, not for data storage. The cart.html page can accept an optional 'mode' query parameter (e.g., cart.html?mode=quote) that is read by cart-page-loader or sales-followup-form on initialization. When mode='quote', the page can automatically focus or scroll to the sales-followup-form and emphasize quote-related actions, while still loading cart data solely via getCartDetails. Navigation from cart to product details uses product.html?id={productId}&itemType={itemType}, where productId and itemType come from the selected CartItem. Navigation to the contact page uses contact.html?topic=sales. No cart content is stored in URL parameters or localStorage; cart identity and contents are managed entirely by the backend accessed via getCartDetails and mutation interfaces.

components.functionality: Initializes and refreshes cart data on page load or when explicitly requested by other components. Calls getCartDetails to retrieve the current Cart, the list of CartItem entries, and the totals object, then exposes this data to child components via a shared state or props.

Displays all CartItem entries returned by getCartDetails as editable line items. For each item, shows name, quantity, unit price, and lineTotal. Allows users to change quantities and remove individual items. Clicking the item name navigates to the corresponding product detail page.

Shows aggregated pricing information for the current cart based on the totals object from getCartDetails, including subtotal, tax, total, and currency. Optionally presents derived shipping or lead time indications if such information is provided in the cart item data. Provides primary actions such as 'Proceed to checkout' (navigation to a checkout page, e.g., checkout.html?cartId={cart.id}, without calling new interfaces) and a 'Contact Sales' link that routes to contact.html?topic=sales.

Provides high-level cart actions like clearing all items. When the user triggers 'Clear cart', it confirms the action if needed, calls clearCart, and then requests a data refresh from cart-page-loader so that all cart-related components reflect the emptied cart.

Collects user contact details and comments to request a sales follow-up or quote for the current cart contents. On submission, validates required fields (such as name and email) and calls requestCartSalesFollowUp with name, email, company, phone, and comments. Displays the response message and can coordinate with cart-feedback-banner to show global feedback. Respects the optional mode URL parameter (e.g., if mode='quote', it may auto-focus the form on load).

Displays non-persistent status messages for cart operations, such as quantity updates, item removals, cart clearing, and sales follow-up requests. It receives 'message' and 'success' flags from the responses of updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem, clearCart, and requestCartSalesFollowUp and renders them as informational or error notifications.

Implements the global search bar available in the site header when on the cart page. Accepts a text query and optional entity type filters, calls globalSearch with these parameters, and routes the user to a search results view or updates a global search overlay. This interaction does not modify cart state.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered fixed-width grid (approximately 1140–1200px max-width) on a full-bleed dark background, conceptually compatible with a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: a fixed-height dark header bar on top, then a centered main content column within a full-bleed dark background. Inside the main column, stack elements vertically but allow a two-column split for the cart core: the cart items table acts as the hero-like central block on the left, while the cart summary and follow-up form form a right-hand side column. Utility elements (feedback banner, actions toolbar, loader) float above or sit just above/below the main stack, similar to the floating button pattern in the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: a fixed-height dark header bar on top, then a centered main content column within a full-bleed dark background. Inside the main column, stack elements vertically but allow a two-column split for the cart core: the cart items table acts as the hero-like central block on the left, while the cart summary and follow-up form form a right-hand side column. Utility elements (feedback banner, actions toolbar, loader) float above or sit just above/below the main stack, similar to the floating button pattern in the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Use a max-width content container of ~1140–1200px centered on a dark shell. The header occupies roughly 10–15% of viewport height. Below it, the central cart content (cart items + summary) dominates the visible area, with 10–15% dark margin left and right of the container. Within the container, allocate about 65–70% width to the cart items table (left) and 30–35% to the cart summary + follow-up column (right). Maintain generous vertical spacing (48–64px) between major blocks: feedback banner, cart core, and any lower sections.
layout_strategies.content_density: Preserve a spacious, low-density feel. Each main block (banner, table, summary, follow-up) reads as a distinct card-like section with 24–32px inner padding. Table rows are not cramped; allow 12–16px vertical padding per row. Avoid clutter: header search is compact, actions toolbar is minimal, and the form uses clear vertical stacking with sufficient breathing room around fields and labels.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Follow an F/Z-pattern: the eye starts at the saturated yellow logo top-left, scans along the header navigation to the right where the global search resides, then drops diagonally down to the large cart items table in the left-center. From there, the gaze shifts horizontally to the right-hand cart summary panel and follow-up form. Finally, attention is drawn to the bottom-right cart actions toolbar, mirroring the floating utility button behavior from the mockup. When active, the loader overlays the entire screen and temporarily becomes the visual focus.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a full-bleed charcoal background with a fixed-height, dark industrial header bar at the top. The header spans the full viewport width but is visually anchored by a centered content grid: the yellow stencil-style logo on the far left, navigation links horizontally next to it, a compact global search input right-aligned within the same row, and a language selector at the far right. Below the header, the main cart content is centered in a 1140–1200px-wide column, surrounded by generous dark margins on both sides. If present, a slim but wide feedback banner appears first, stretching nearly the full content width with generous top and bottom breathing space. Beneath that, the main hero-like area of the page is the cart: on the left, a broad, card-like cart items table occupies about two-thirds of the content width and extends vertically, while on the right, a taller but narrower vertical stack of the cart summary panel and the sales follow-up form occupies the remaining third. All cards share a slightly lighter charcoal surface with soft radius and 24–32px internal padding, maintaining a clean, typography-led look. At the bottom-right of the viewport, slightly inset from the edges, a compact, floating cart actions toolbar hovers over the dark background and remains visible as users scroll, echoing the mockup’s floating utility button. When the cart is loading or updating, a semi-opaque dark overlay with a centered industrial loader covers this composition, maintaining the minimal, premium aesthetic while focusing attention on system status.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-page-loader appears as a full-viewport overlay on top of all other elements. It covers both the header and main content with a semi-transparent charcoal scrim, slightly darkening the underlying UI while still hinting at its structure. Centered both horizontally and vertically in the viewport is a compact loading indicator—either a mechanical, circular spinner or a minimal industrial-styled progress element—approximately 64–96px wide, with a small status label below it. The loader’s content is centrally aligned and not stretched, preserving ample empty space around it so it feels deliberate and calm rather than busy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned fixed to the viewport (top: 0, left: 0, width: 100%, height: 100%), sitting above all other layers with a high z-index. It appears only during loading states, blocking interaction with underlying components while preserving their layout in the background.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-items-table is the main hero-like block in the content area. It is placed in the left portion of the centered content container, starting roughly 48–64px below the feedback banner (or below the header if no banner is present). It spans about 65–70% of the container width, aligned to the left, with the right edge lining up with the left edge of the cart summary panel. The table is contained within a slightly lighter charcoal card, with 24–32px internal padding on all sides, and a subtle separation from the background (via shadow or subtle border). Column headers sit at the top of the card, with each row laid out in a spacious manner: product image on the far left, name and specs next, then quantity controls and price columns to the right. Vertical spacing between rows uses at least 12–16px padding, ensuring a low-density, premium feel. The bottom of the table aligns roughly with or slightly above the bottom edge of the cart summary panel, depending on the number of items, allowing the right column to visually anchor the right side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow, participating in a two-column layout (left column). It scrolls with the page; only its vertical position changes as the user scrolls the document.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-summary-panel sits to the right of the cart-items-table, within the same centered content container, forming the right column of the cart area. It begins aligned at the same top baseline as the cart-items-table, approximately 48–64px below the feedback banner or header. The panel occupies about 30–35% of the container width and is separated from the table by a 32–40px horizontal gap. It appears as a vertical card with a slightly lighter charcoal background, 24–32px internal padding, and clear typographic hierarchy for subtotal, shipping, taxes, and grand total. The height of this panel is moderate; it should feel concise and readable at a glance, with each line item separated by 8–12px and major totals separated by 16–24px. Primary checkout or "Request sales follow-up" calls-to-action sit near the bottom of this panel, spanning nearly its full width with sufficient padding and vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the right column, the panel behaves as a sticky element on larger screens: it remains visible as the user scrolls down through a long items table. Implement this as position: sticky, aligned to the top of the viewport with a 96–120px offset to remain below the fixed header, ensuring the summary is persistently accessible without obstructing content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-actions-toolbar is a compact, horizontally arranged utility bar that anchors the interaction flow at the bottom-right of the viewport, echoing the floating button in the mockup. It appears as a dark, semi-elevated capsule or narrow bar with rounded corners, containing 2–4 key secondary actions such as "Continue shopping", "Save cart", or "Clear cart". The toolbar is inset approximately 24–32px from the right edge of the content area and 24–32px from the bottom edge of the viewport. It does not stretch full-width; instead it occupies only as much width as needed for its buttons, roughly 25–35% of the content width at most, leaving ample empty space around it. The toolbar visually hovers over the background, not attached directly to any specific card, and remains visually quiet so it doesn’t compete with the main cart summary or items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed relative to the viewport on desktop (position: fixed) and aligned to the bottom-right inside the max-width content bounds. It stays visible as the user scrolls. On smaller screens, it can dock just above the bottom edge as a full-width, low-height bar but still maintain a 8–16px inset for breathing room.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sales-followup-form is positioned directly below the cart-summary-panel in the right column of the central content container. It aligns with the summary panel’s left and right edges and is separated from it by a vertical gap of 32–40px. The form appears as another card with similar visual treatment—a slightly lighter charcoal background and 24–32px padding—but it is slightly taller and more vertically oriented. Form fields (contact info, company details, notes) are stacked vertically, each spanning the full width of the panel, with 12–16px vertical spacing between fields, maintaining a calm, spacious rhythm. The form’s title and short explanatory text sit at the top, with a modest gap above the first input. The primary submission button sits at the bottom, aligned left or spanning the full width of the form, visually subordinate to any main checkout CTA but clearly discoverable within the right-hand column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column beneath the cart-summary-panel. It scrolls with the page and does not stick; when the summary panel is sticky, the form may scroll out of view while the user moves through the table, preserving the summary’s priority.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-feedback-banner appears at the top of the main content area, directly below the header and spanning nearly the entire width of the centered content container (about 90–100% of the container width). It is a relatively low-height strip, with 16–24px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding, visually treated as a thin, colored band (e.g., subtle green or red accent) sitting against the dark background. The banner is centered along the same axis as the cart content and has a 48–64px gap above the cart-items-table and cart-summary-panel area. Its message text is left-aligned, with an optional icon on the far left and a close/dismiss icon aligned to the right. The surrounding dark shell provides generous breathing space above and below, ensuring the banner feels lightweight rather than intrusive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the main vertical flow, pushing the rest of the content downward when present. It does not remain sticky; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-global-search is integrated into the fixed top header bar, aligned to the right side of the centered header content row. Inside the ~1140–1200px header grid, the logo occupies the far left, navigation links sit in the middle, and the search input appears on the right, just to the left of the language selector. The search box is compact and horizontal, approximately 220–280px wide, with a low-profile dark input field, a faint outline or subtle underlined style, and a search icon aligned inside on the right or left. Vertically, it is centered within the header’s height, sharing the baseline with the navigation items, and there is adequate horizontal spacing (16–24px) between the search component and neighboring elements to preserve the clean, minimal feel. The search does not dominate; it feels like a precise utility within the industrial header bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in place within the fixed/sticky header (which remains at the top of the viewport). As the user scrolls, the header and its embedded search form stay visible, maintaining quick access to search across the site.

name: Machine Configurator Landing
title: Machine Configurator Landing
description: Allows users to choose a configurable CNC machine category and start a configuration, optionally using a preselected category from the URL, and supports global search across the catalog.

core_features: Fetch and display all configurable machine categories using getConfigurableMachineCategories, with filtering to only show categories that are currently active.
Allow users to select a machine category and start the configuration flow by navigating to build_machine.html with the selected categoryId.
Respect an optional categoryId URL parameter to preselect and highlight a specific configurable machine category on page load.
Provide a clear explanation of how the configuration and quote request process works for configurable machines.
Offer a global search input that calls globalSearch to display quick cross-entity results (machines, spare parts, documents, case studies) without leaving the page.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Browse and start a configuration:
1) User opens configurator.html without URL parameters.
2) Page initialization component calls getConfigurableMachineCategories.
3) Only categories with status equal to the active value are passed to the category list component.
4) User reviews category cards (each showing name, description, basePrice, currency, and machineType when available).
5) User clicks the primary action on a category card (e.g., a configure action).
6) The page navigates to build_machine.html with the selected category's id in the categoryId URL parameter.

Workflow 2 – Preselected category from another page:
1) User clicks a link like Build Your Machine on index.html or products.html that includes configurator.html?categoryId={someId}.
2) On load, page initialization reads the categoryId from the URL.
3) getConfigurableMachineCategories is called.
4) When data arrives, the component finds the category whose id matches the URL categoryId.
5) If a match is found and the category is active, it is marked as selected, highlighted in the category list, and its details are shown in the selected-category summary component.
6) If the user clicks the start configuration action from the selected-category summary or card, the page navigates to build_machine.html?categoryId={thatId}.

Workflow 3 – Global search from configurator page:
1) User types text into the search input in the header search component.
2) On submit or debounce, the component calls globalSearch with the current query and entityTypes set to include all supported entity types for this interface.
3) The component receives machineModels, spareParts, documents, and caseStudies arrays.
4) A compact results panel is shown on top of the configurator page content.
5) If the user clicks a result, the page navigates to the appropriate detail page (for example, a machine model detail or a document download page) using that entity's id or shareableUrl in the URL parameters; this navigation does not affect the current configurator logic but leaves the page.
6) If the user closes the search panel, the configurator landing state (including any selected category based on categoryId) remains unchanged.

Workflow 4 – Empty or inactive category handling:
1) Page calls getConfigurableMachineCategories.
2) If no categories are returned, or none have status equal to the active value, the empty-state component displays a functional message that no configurable categories are currently available.
3) The configuration start actions are disabled or hidden, preventing navigation to build_machine.html when no valid category is available.
interactions: On page load, the initialization logic reads the categoryId URL parameter, triggers getConfigurableMachineCategories, and then synchronizes the selected category state with the URL.

Clicking anywhere on a category card (except the primary action) sets that category as the current selection, updates the URL categoryId parameter via history manipulation, and updates the selected-category summary component.
Clicking the primary start configuration action on a category (either from the card or summary) triggers navigation to build_machine.html with categoryId set to the selected category's id.

Typing into the global search input updates an internal query state; submitting the form sends a globalSearch request and shows inline results; clicking a search result navigates away to the relevant detail page.

If the URL categoryId changes via browser navigation (back/forward), the page re-evaluates the selected category based on the updated URL and updates the highlighted card and summary panel accordingly.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary piece of page state is the selected machine category, which is synchronized with the categoryId URL parameter. On initial load, the page reads window.location.search for categoryId. After getConfigurableMachineCategories resolves, the page attempts to find a ConfigurableMachineCategory with id equal to this categoryId. If found and its status equals the active status value, that category becomes the selected category and is highlighted in the category list and shown in the summary component. If the categoryId parameter is missing, no category is preselected; the first selection will come from user interaction. If the categoryId parameter is present but does not match any returned active category, the page behaves as if there is no valid selection (no highlight or summary until the user selects a category). When the user clicks a category card to select it, the page updates the in-memory selectedCategoryId and also updates the URL query string (replacing or adding categoryId) using the History API, without reloading the page. This ensures that refreshing the page or using bookmarks preserves the selected category. Navigation to build_machine.html includes the selected categoryId in the URL query parameters by constructing build_machine.html?categoryId={selectedId}. The global search component manages its own transient state (current query string and latest results) in memory only and does not introduce additional URL parameters on configurator.html; search-related navigation is performed by constructing target URLs based on the clicked result, without changing the configurator-specific state logic.

components.functionality: Initializes the Machine Configurator Landing page by reading URL parameters, calling getConfigurableMachineCategories, filtering results, and coordinating selected category state.

Displays a list of configurable machine categories returned by getConfigurableMachineCategories, shows key fields (name, description, basePrice, currency, machineType), indicates which category is currently selected based on state, and exposes actions to select a category or start configuration.

Shows detailed information for the currently selected configurable machine category, including name, description, base price, currency, machineType, and any other available fields, and offers a primary action to start the configuration flow.
data_binding: ConfigurableMachineCategory (single item corresponding to selected categoryId)

components.functionality: Explains, in functional terms, how the configuration process works (choose a category, configure options on build_machine.html, submit a quote request there). This component does not call any interfaces and only displays static instructional content.

Displays a functional message and disables configuration navigation when getConfigurableMachineCategories returns no active categories; appears conditionally based on the results from the initialization logic.

Provides a search input that calls globalSearch with the user's query and displays quick results (machines, spare parts, documents, and case studies) in an overlay panel; clicking a result navigates to the appropriate detail page while leaving configurator state unchanged until navigation.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered fixed-width grid (approximately 1140–1200px max-width) on a full-bleed dark background, conceptually compatible with a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain a linear, top-to-bottom flow with a fixed-height dark header (logo and navigation) at the top, followed by a dominant hero section that visually echoes the CNC machine hero from the mockup. Within the hero, the main interactive element is the global search, centered over or just below the hero imagery. Below the hero, the page transitions into a stacked content zone: first the machine category list as the primary content band, then a right-aligned vertical strip for the selected-category summary and process explainer. The empty state occupies the same primary content space as the category list when applicable, so the overall structure remains consistent.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain a linear, top-to-bottom flow with a fixed-height dark header (logo and navigation) at the top, followed by a dominant hero section that visually echoes the CNC machine hero from the mockup. Within the hero, the main interactive element is the global search, centered over or just below the hero imagery. Below the hero, the page transitions into a stacked content zone: first the machine category list as the primary content band, then a right-aligned vertical strip for the selected-category summary and process explainer. The empty state occupies the same primary content space as the category list when applicable, so the overall structure remains consistent.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Use a max-width central content column of roughly 1140–1200px within a full-bleed charcoal background. The header occupies about 10–15% of the viewport height. The hero section immediately below occupies most of the visible viewport (roughly 60–70% height on first load) and about 70–80% of the content width, framed by dark margins of 10–15% on either side. Below the hero, the category and information panels share the same central column: the category list takes about two-thirds of the width, while the right-side informational column (selected-category summary + process explainer) uses the remaining one-third. Vertically, leave 48–64px gaps between major sections (hero to categories, categories to any subsequent sections) to preserve the spacious, premium feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep the layout low-density and typography-led. Each major block (search, category list, summary panel, explainer, empty state) sits in its own clear zone with generous padding (16–24px inside components) and large surrounding negative space. Avoid crowding: even in the two-column section, maintain 32–48px of gutter between columns and ample top/bottom breathing room so the page retains the calm, product-centric tone of the mockup. Text is concise and well spaced, with clear hierarchy and no ornamental borders; rely on subtle cards, lines, and spacing rather than heavy chrome.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve an F/Z-pattern: the eye first catches the bright yellow logo in the header’s top-left, moves horizontally through the navigation to the right, then drops diagonally down into the center of the page where the hero CNC image and the global search command attention. From the search, the eye naturally flows downward to the category list as the main interactive area. When a category is selected, attention is guided to the right-hand selected-category summary, then further down to the process explainer panel. A small floating utility (e.g., support/consent) in the bottom-right corner closes the Z-path as a tertiary attractor after the core configuration content has been absorbed.

layout.overall_description: The page sits within a full-bleed charcoal background with a fixed-height industrial header running across the top, logo at the far left in a saturated yellow stencil-like font, navigation links in the center, and a compact language selector aligned to the right. Beneath the header, the main content is centered in a max-width column, leaving wide dark margins on both sides. The initial viewport is dominated by a cinematic hero zone showcasing a CNC machine image, occupying roughly 70–80% of the content width and framed by generous top and side margins so the image feels like a premium, framed object. Overlaid near the center of this hero, a wide but shallow global search bar spans about 60% of the content width, becoming the immediate interactive focus without overpowering the image. Below the hero, after a tall 48–64px band of breathing room, the layout resolves into a two-column content band: on the left, a vertically scrolling list of machine categories occupies about two-thirds of the width; on the right, a narrower stack of informational panels—first the selected-category summary at the top and then the configurator process explainer below—share the remaining third. If no categories are available or no search match is found, a dedicated empty-state card appears centered in the left content area where the category list would normally be, maintaining alignment and rhythm. A small floating utility button in the lower-right corner of the viewport sits 24–32px away from both the right and bottom edges, unobtrusive yet clearly visible against the dark shell. Overall, the layout feels sparse, deliberate, and mechanical, directing attention from brand to hero to configuration actions in a controlled, cinematic sequence.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init component defines the main hero section immediately below the fixed-height header. It spans the full width of the central content area (approximately 70–80% of the total viewport width) and is horizontally centered, with equal dark margins on the left and right. Vertically, this hero zone occupies the majority of the above-the-fold space, roughly 60–70% of the viewport height, starting about 32–40px below the header to create a generous top margin. Within this region, a large CNC machine hero image is anchored centrally, with soft padding of roughly 40–64px between the image and the boundaries of the hero section to create a framed, gallery-like feel. All other primary components (notably the global search) visually anchor themselves relative to this hero, either as overlays or immediately beneath it in the vertical flow. On first load, this component ensures the user perceives a single, focused hero block before any detailed lists or panels below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as the primary hero container in the normal document flow, directly under the fixed header. Its height is responsive, scaling with viewport height while maintaining minimum padding around the hero image. It does not scroll independently but scrolls with the page as a whole.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search component is positioned within the hero region defined by the page-init component. It is horizontally centered and placed slightly below the vertical midpoint of the hero area so it sits over or immediately under the focal area of the CNC hero image without obscuring critical details. The search bar itself spans about 60% of the central content width on large screens, leaving substantial dark or image space on either side; on smaller screens it can expand to roughly 80–90% width while remaining centered. There is generous vertical whitespace around it: at least 32–48px above and below before any other interactive elements appear, preserving the low-density aesthetic. The search field is a wide, flat input with industrial minimal styling, with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, and it may include a subtle icon aligned to the right or left, separated from the text by an 8px gap. Visually, it reads as the primary call to action within the hero, clearly distinct from any content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as a central overlay or foreground element within the hero, but still within normal flow for accessibility (no fixed or sticky behavior). On scroll, it moves up with the hero section; on smaller viewports, it maintains its centered position and proportional width while stacking above the hero image if necessary to preserve clarity.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category list appears in the main content section directly beneath the hero and the global search. It is aligned with the same central content column as the hero and begins after a vertical gap of approximately 48–64px to clearly separate it from the hero area. This component occupies roughly two-thirds of the central content width on desktop, positioned on the left side of a two-column layout. There is a visible gutter of about 32–40px to its right before the right-hand informational column begins. Internally, the category items are arranged in a vertically stacked list or compact grid, with each category row/card having 16–24px of padding and 16–24px vertical separation from the next, keeping the layout airy rather than dense. The overall height of the category list can extend below the fold, with smooth vertical scrolling; the right-hand summary and explainer panels align their top edges with the top of this list so the section feels like a unified band. When categories are loaded and available, this list is the dominant element in the post-hero section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the regular document flow. On larger screens, it forms the left column of a two-column layout; on smaller screens (e.g., tablet and below), it should stack above the right-hand panels and take 100% width, preserving generous vertical spacing (32–48px) between stacked sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The selected-category summary panel is positioned to the right of the category list, sharing the same baseline and forming the top portion of the right-hand column. This right column occupies about one-third of the central content width, with a 32–40px gutter separating it from the category list. The summary panel anchors at the top of this column, aligning its top edge with the first row of the category list to create a clear horizontal rhythm. It behaves like a compact card with 16–24px internal padding and a modest height, occupying roughly the upper half (or less, depending on content) of the right column. The content within is tightly curated: a title, a few key specs or configuration summary lines, and possibly a minimal thumbnail or icon, with 8–16px spacing between text elements. When no category is selected, the panel either collapses to a low-contrast placeholder state or remains hidden so that the process explainer can slide up into the available space. When a category is selected, this panel becomes visually active and slightly more luminous against the dark background, drawing the eye as the logical next step after interacting with the category list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Located in the normal flow as part of the right-hand column, potentially with a sticky-top behavior on tall pages so that it remains visible while scrolling through a longer category list. If sticky behavior is used, it should lock to the top of the viewport with about 24–32px offset from the header, maintaining the premium, controlled feel without feeling cramped.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The process explainer resides directly below the selected-category summary within the same right-hand column. It shares the same width (one-third of the central content area) and aligns flush beneath the summary, with a small vertical gap of about 16–24px between them. When the summary is absent (no category selected), the explainer panel moves up to occupy the top of the right column, aligning with the top of the category list. Visually, it behaves like a concise info card: 16–24px internal padding, clear step-by-step or short description text, and 8–16px spacing between lines or bullet points. It is designed to be taller than the summary, potentially occupying the remaining height of the right column above the fold, but still leaving enough negative space so as not to feel dense. The explainer helps users understand the configuration journey while they browse categories; it is clearly subordinate to the category list and summary but still prominent enough to be read quickly in the peripheral vision as users scan the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the normal flow in the right-hand column, optionally coupled with the summary in a shared sticky container so that both panels remain visible while the user scrolls the category list. On smaller screens, it stacks below the category list (and below the summary if present), with 32–48px vertical spacing between these blocks, occupying full width with the same spacious padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state panel occupies the same spatial footprint as the category list when no categories are available or when a search yields no results. It is centered within the left portion of the two-column layout, aligning with the same left and right edges as the category list to preserve the grid rhythm. Vertically, it sits in the main content region just beneath the hero, starting after the same 48–64px gap used for the category list. The card itself is moderately sized—approximately one-third to one-half of the viewport height—with 24px internal padding and ample negative space around a short message and a small illustrative icon or subtle graphic. The text is center-aligned within the card, with 8–16px spacing between the icon, heading, and explanatory copy. Surrounding this panel, maintain at least 40–64px of dark background before any other elements to reinforce the feeling of a calm, controlled state rather than an error. The right-hand column (summary and explainer) remains in place, visually aligning to the top edge of the empty-state card so the section still reads as a cohesive band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the category list in the left column: when the empty state is shown, the category list is hidden or visually suppressed, but the right-hand panels remain in normal flow. On smaller screens, the empty-state card takes full width and appears directly below the hero and search, with the right-hand panels stacking underneath it.

name: Machine Configurator Detail
title: Machine Configurator Detail
description: Page for configuring a CNC milling machine category, adjusting options with live pricing, and submitting a quote request for the selected configuration.

core_features: Load a configurable machine category and its option groups based on the required categoryId URL parameter using getConfiguratorCategoryDetails.
Initialize a default machine configuration by selecting options flagged as default within each option group, then calculate the initial price using calculateConfigurationPrice.
Allow users to change configuration options (single-choice, multi-choice, optional) per option group and recalculate pricing in real time using calculateConfigurationPrice.
Display a running price summary (base price, options total, final total) that updates whenever selected options change and compares the total to an optional target budget.
Allow users to set or adjust a target budget (if provided via URL or entered on the page) and highlight when the current configuration is above or below this target.
Enable users to refine their configuration (e.g., switch from higher-cost to lower-cost options) to meet or approach the target budget, with immediate feedback from the price summary.
Provide a 'Request a Quote' action that scrolls to or reveals a quote request form while preserving the current configuration state.
Collect user and project contact details in a quote request form and submit them alongside the current configuration using submitConfiguredMachineQuote.
Show a confirmation state on successful quote submission (without leaving the page) and lock or summarize the submitted configuration while still allowing further exploration if desired.
Provide access to global search (machines, spare parts, documents, case studies) via globalSearch from the page header.
user_workflows: Open configurator for a category: 1) User navigates from configurator.html or index.html and arrives on build_machine.html?categoryId=<id>. 2) On page load, the page reads categoryId from the URL. 3) The page calls getConfiguratorCategoryDetails with categoryId. 4) The page derives initial selectedOptionIds from options marked as default per group or from the selectedOptionIds URL parameter if present. 5) The page calls calculateConfigurationPrice with categoryId and the resolved selectedOptionIds. 6) The page renders the machine summary header, option groups, and price summary using the returned data.

Configure the machine and see live price: 1) User reviews each option group (e.g., spindle, tool changer, coolant, enclosure, accessories). 2) For each group, the user selects or deselects options according to the group’s selectionType (single_choice, multi_choice, optional). 3) On every change, the configuration component updates the in-page selectedOptionIds state and also updates the URL parameter reflecting the current configuration. 4) The page calls calculateConfigurationPrice with the new selectedOptionIds. 5) The price summary panel updates base price, total price, and other derived values. 6) If a target budget is active, the panel indicates whether the configuration is under or over this target.

Adjust configuration to meet a target budget: 1) User either lands with a targetBudget URL parameter or inputs a target budget into a dedicated control on the page. 2) The page writes or updates the targetBudget parameter in the URL. 3) The price summary compares totalPrice from calculateConfigurationPrice with targetBudget and displays a status indicator (e.g., under, near, or above target) and the price difference. 4) If the configuration is above target, the user can revisit option groups, change to lower-cost options, or remove accessories. 5) On each change, calculateConfigurationPrice is called and the budget comparison is refreshed, guiding users to a configuration that meets their budget.

Request a quote for the current configuration: 1) When satisfied with the configuration, the user clicks the 'Request a Quote' call-to-action in the price summary or at the bottom of the configurator. 2) The page scrolls to or expands the quote request form section, preserving the current selectedOptionIds and categoryId in memory and in the URL. 3) The user enters contact and project details (name, email, optional company, phone, comments). 4) On form submission, the page validates required fields client-side. 5) If valid, the page calls submitConfiguredMachineQuote with categoryId, selectedOptionIds, and the form fields. 6) If the response indicates success, the page shows a confirmation message detailing that the quote has been submitted and may add a quoteStatus parameter in the URL. 7) The configuration used for the quote is preserved in the UI, and users may still tweak options for exploration without affecting the already-submitted request.

Use global search from the configurator: 1) At any point, the user can type into the global search bar. 2) On submit, the page calls globalSearch with the query and appropriate entityTypes. 3) The page either shows a search dropdown/overlay with results or navigates to a dedicated search results page (per overall site design), passing the query via URL parameters. The configurator page itself does not modify configuration state based on search results.
interactions: On page initialization (onInit), read categoryId (required), selectedOptionIds (optional), targetBudget (optional), and quoteStatus (optional) from the URL query string and use them to determine initial page state.
On invalid or missing categoryId, show an error state component that prevents configuration and suggests returning to the configurator landing page (navigation behavior defined elsewhere).

On successful response from getConfiguratorCategoryDetails, populate the machine header with category details and build the list of option groups and options in their specified sortOrder, pre-selecting options marked as default where selectionType demands it.

When a user changes an option selection in a single_choice group, deselect any previously selected option in that group, update selectedOptionIds, write the updated set to the URL parameter, and trigger calculateConfigurationPrice.
When a user toggles options in a multi_choice or optional group, update selectedOptionIds appropriately, write the updated set to the URL parameter, and trigger calculateConfigurationPrice.

After each calculateConfigurationPrice call, update the price summary component (basePrice, totalPrice, currency, and list of selectedOptions) and update any budget comparison indicators using the current targetBudget (if defined).

When a user sets or edits a target budget in the budget control, validate the input, update the internal target budget state and the targetBudget URL parameter, then recompute budget comparison indicators without needing additional API calls.

When a user clicks the 'Request a Quote' action, scroll the viewport to the quote request form component using an in-page anchor or programmatic scrolling and optionally add or update a URL hash or parameter (e.g., form=quote) to reflect that the form is in focus.
On quote form field changes, update local form state but do not call any API until submission.

On quote form submission, validate required fields; if validation passes, call submitConfiguredMachineQuote with categoryId, selectedOptionIds, and the form data, and disable the submit button until the call completes.

On a successful submitConfiguredMachineQuote response (success=true), show a confirmation message, optionally lock the form fields or replace the form with a read-only summary, and update the URL to include a quoteStatus parameter indicating success; ensure that the configuration and price summary still reflect the submitted configuration.
On a failed submitConfiguredMachineQuote response (success=false) or network error, show an error message in the quote form component and allow the user to correct data and retry the submission.

On global search bar submission, call globalSearch with the user’s query and configured entityTypes; update a search results overlay component or navigate away as defined by the global layout behavior.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary required URL parameter is categoryId. On every page load, the configurator reads categoryId from the query string (e.g., build_machine.html?categoryId=<id>). If categoryId is absent or invalid, the page enters an error state and does not attempt to load configuration data. If categoryId is present, the page calls getConfiguratorCategoryDetails to retrieve the ConfigurableMachineCategory and its associated ConfigOptionGroups and ConfigOptions. Optional URL parameters are used to encode and restore configuration and workflow state without using localStorage. A selectedOptionIds parameter stores the list of currently selected ConfigOption ids (encoded as a query-safe representation such as a delimited string); if present, this set is used to initialize the configuration instead of or in combination with defaults. After resolving initial selectedOptionIds, the page calls calculateConfigurationPrice with categoryId and selectedOptionIds to initialize pricing. A targetBudget parameter (optional) represents the user's desired budget; when present or updated, the page uses it to compare with totalPrice from calculateConfigurationPrice and adjust budget indicators. A quoteStatus parameter (optional) can be added or updated when a quote request is successfully submitted to allow the page to display a confirmation message even after reload, while leaving the configuration intact. Additional optional parameters (such as a form=quote flag or a hash) may indicate that the quote form should be scrolled into view on load. All changes to selected options or budget are reflected both in in-memory component state and in the URL parameters so that reloading or sharing the URL restores the same configuration and budget context.

components.functionality: Provides an input field for users to search across machines, spare parts, documents, and case studies from the configurator page header.
event_handlers: onSubmit: Reads the user's query string and calls globalSearch with query and a preconfigured entityTypes array; handles the response by either updating an in-page search results overlay or navigating to a search results page using URL parameters.
onChange: Optionally debounces user input to provide suggestions by calling globalSearch with partial queries (implementation detail dependent on global UX).

components.functionality: Central controller that reads URL parameters, initializes page state, loads category details, derives the initial configuration, and triggers the first price calculation.
event_handlers: onInit: Reads categoryId (required), selectedOptionIds, targetBudget, and quoteStatus from the URL. If categoryId is present, calls getConfiguratorCategoryDetails with categoryId. On success, it determines initial selectedOptionIds by combining any options marked as isDefault within each ConfigOptionGroup with the selectedOptionIds URL parameter (URL values override defaults where applicable). It then triggers calculateConfigurationPrice with categoryId and the resolved selectedOptionIds and dispatches the results to the price-summary-panel and configuration-option-groups-list components. If categoryId is missing or the API returns an error, it sets an error state consumed by the configurator-error-state component.

onUrlChange: If the URL query parameters change due to internal navigation (e.g., back/forward in browser), re-reads relevant parameters and, if categoryId matches the current one, updates selectedOptionIds and targetBudget in in-page state and re-runs calculateConfigurationPrice.

components.functionality: Displays an error message and disables configuration when categoryId is missing, invalid, or getConfiguratorCategoryDetails fails.
event_handlers: onStateChange: Listens to configurator-initializer state; when an error flag is set, renders an appropriate error message and may present a link or button back to the configurator landing page (navigation URL defined elsewhere).

components.functionality: Shows high-level information about the selected configurable machine category, such as name, description, and base pricing, giving users context for the configuration they are about to build.
event_handlers: onCategoryLoaded: Receives category data from configurator-initializer after getConfiguratorCategoryDetails and renders textual details such as category name, description, and basePrice with currency; this component is read-only in terms of configuration.

components.functionality: Renders all configuration option groups (spindle, tool changer, coolant, enclosure, accessory, etc.) with their associated options and handles user selections according to each group's selectionType.
event_handlers: onCategoryDetailsLoaded: Renders a list of groups ordered by group.sortOrder and, for each group, displays its options with current selection state determined by selectedOptionIds from configurator-initializer.

onOptionSelect: When a user selects an option within a single_choice group, updates the selected state to that option only, modifies the shared selectedOptionIds state, updates the selectedOptionIds parameter in the URL, and triggers calculateConfigurationPrice via the pricing-controller component.

onOptionToggle: When a user toggles an option within a multi_choice or optional group, adds or removes the option id from selectedOptionIds, updates the URL parameter accordingly, and triggers calculateConfigurationPrice via the pricing-controller component.

onSelectionStateSync: Listens for updates from pricing-controller or configurator-initializer (e.g., after a back/forward navigation or URL change) and adjusts UI selection states to match the authoritative selectedOptionIds.

components.functionality: Coordinates all calls to calculateConfigurationPrice whenever the set of selected options changes or when the page is first initialized, and distributes pricing data to the price summary and any dependent components.
event_handlers: onConfigurationInit: Invoked after configurator-initializer has resolved initial selectedOptionIds; calls calculateConfigurationPrice with categoryId and selectedOptionIds and forwards the result to price-summary-panel.

onSelectedOptionsChange: Triggered by configuration-option-groups-list whenever selectedOptionIds change; calls calculateConfigurationPrice with the updated selectedOptionIds and updates the price-summary-panel with the new basePrice, totalPrice, currency, and selectedOptions.

onApiError: If calculateConfigurationPrice returns an error or fails, signals an error state to price-summary-panel so it can display a non-blocking pricing error while allowing configuration to continue.

components.functionality: Displays the current pricing details of the configuration, including base price, total price, and a summary of selected cost-impacting options, and compares the total to any active target budget.
event_handlers: onPriceUpdate: Receives basePrice, totalPrice, currency, and selectedOptions from pricing-controller and renders them; updates any indicators showing price changes relative to the initial configuration.
onBudgetChange: Listens for targetBudget updates from budget-target-control or URL changes; compares totalPrice to targetBudget and updates visual indicators for under/over/near target status.

onRequestQuoteClick: When the user clicks the 'Request a Quote' button within this panel, triggers a scroll or focus event to bring the quote-request-form into view and updates the URL to reflect that the quote form should be shown (e.g., adding a form=quote or similar parameter).

components.functionality: Allows users to view and adjust an optional target budget for their machine configuration; this budget is used by the price-summary-panel for guidance but does not constrain API calls.
event_handlers: onInit: Reads any existing targetBudget parameter from the URL and populates the budget input field with that value, if available.

onBudgetInputChange: When the user edits the budget input, validates the value (e.g., non-negative numeric), updates the in-page targetBudget state, updates the targetBudget parameter in the URL, and notifies the price-summary-panel of the new value for comparison with totalPrice.
onClearBudget: If the user clears the budget input, removes the targetBudget parameter from the URL and signals price-summary-panel to remove budget comparison indicators.

components.functionality: Collects user contact and project details for the current configuration and submits a quote request using submitConfiguredMachineQuote.
event_handlers: onInit: Uses categoryId and selectedOptionIds from configurator-initializer to know which configuration will be bound to the quote; if quoteStatus in the URL indicates a previously successful submission, initializes in a read-only or confirmation state rather than an empty form.
onFieldChange: Updates local form state as the user enters name, email, company, phone, and comments; performs basic validation feedback (e.g., required fields, email format) but does not call APIs.

onSubmit: Validates that required fields are present; if valid, calls submitConfiguredMachineQuote with categoryId, selectedOptionIds, and the form fields. While awaiting response, disables the submit button and may show a loading indicator.
