onSubmitSuccess: When submitConfiguredMachineQuote returns success=true, receives the quoteRequest and configuration objects. It displays a confirmation message including key configuration details (such as totalPrice and currency from configuration) and may lock the form fields or switch to a submitted state. It also updates the URL to include a quoteStatus parameter indicating success, so reloading the page shows the confirmation state.
onSubmitError: If submitConfiguredMachineQuote returns success=false or fails, shows an inline error message and re-enables the submit button so the user can retry or adjust their details.

components.functionality: Displays a standalone confirmation message after a successful quote submission, based on quoteStatus in the URL and the response from submitConfiguredMachineQuote.
event_handlers: onQuoteStatusChange: Listens to changes in quoteStatus from the URL or from quote-request-form. When quoteStatus indicates a successful submission, renders a confirmation message including reference information from quoteRequest (e.g., id and createdAt) and key pricing details from configuration totalPrice. If quoteStatus is not present or indicates no success, hides itself.

components.functionality: Keeps URL parameters in sync with the current configuration and workflow state (selectedOptionIds, targetBudget, quoteStatus, and optional form focus flags) so that the configurator state is shareable and recoverable on reload.
event_handlers: onSelectedOptionsChange: When notified by configuration-option-groups-list of a change to selectedOptionIds, updates the selectedOptionIds URL parameter using a deterministic encoding (e.g., sorted ids joined by a delimiter) without triggering a full page reload, and notifies pricing-controller.
onBudgetChange: When notified by budget-target-control of a new or cleared targetBudget, updates the targetBudget URL parameter and notifies price-summary-panel.
onQuoteStatusUpdate: When notified by quote-request-form of a successful submission, writes or updates quoteStatus in the URL so that the confirmation state persists across refreshes.

onPopState: Handles browser back/forward events by re-reading URL parameters and notifying configurator-initializer, configuration-option-groups-list, budget-target-control, and quote-confirmation-panel to synchronize their states.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered fixed-width grid (approximately 1140–1200px max-width) on a full-bleed dark background, conceptually compatible with a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top‑to‑bottom stack inside a full‑bleed dark shell, mirroring the mockup’s pattern: fixed-height dark header (logo + nav, outside this spec), then a slim global search band, then a hero-like machine summary block that visually replaces the original hero image. Below that, the configurator zone is structured as a 2‑column band: configuration options on the left and live pricing on the right. Beneath this band, a full-width quote-request section provides the final call to action. Message panels (error and confirmation) occupy the same central band as the hero or quote form, appearing inline and temporarily replacing the normal content. A small floating controller in the bottom-right corner echoes the mockup’s utility button pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top‑to‑bottom stack inside a full‑bleed dark shell, mirroring the mockup’s pattern: fixed-height dark header (logo + nav, outside this spec), then a slim global search band, then a hero-like machine summary block that visually replaces the original hero image. Below that, the configurator zone is structured as a 2‑column band: configuration options on the left and live pricing on the right. Beneath this band, a full-width quote-request section provides the final call to action. Message panels (error and confirmation) occupy the same central band as the hero or quote form, appearing inline and temporarily replacing the normal content. A small floating controller in the bottom-right corner echoes the mockup’s utility button pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Content is constrained to a centered column with a max width of about 1140–1200px, sitting on a full-bleed charcoal background. Left and right margins consume roughly 10–15% of the viewport each, leaving the content column to occupy about 70–80% of the viewport width. The header occupies ~10–15% of viewport height. The machine summary hero block fills most of the first viewport, with generous top and side margins, and a height of roughly 40–50% of the viewport on desktop. The configurator’s 2‑column band uses around 60–65% width for the left configuration options and 35–40% for the right pricing column. Vertical gaps between major sections are 48–64px, and between related components within a section 24–32px, following the 8px spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout remains deliberately low-density. Each major component (hero summary, config options, pricing summary, quote form) is housed in its own flat, lightly bordered card on the dark background with 24–32px inner padding and clear breathing room around it. Controls in forms and panels have 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. Content is grouped in logical blocks with minimal simultaneous choices on screen: configuration options are chunked into collapsible groups, pricing information is summarized succinctly, and the quote form is compact but airy. Large dark areas around the central column preserve the premium, cinematic feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The flow follows an F/Z-pattern adapted for a configurator: the eye first hits the bright yellow logo in the top-left (header), then moves horizontally across the navigation and over the global search bar. It then drops diagonally into the center of the page where the machine summary hero block sits. From here, the gaze moves right to the prominent price summary in the right column, then travels down the left column through the configuration options. Finally it crosses to the full-width quote section and ends near the primary submission action. A subtle floating controller at the bottom-right mirrors the mockup’s floating button, acting as a secondary attractor and giving quick access to configuration state utilities without disrupting the main flow.

layout.overall_description: Inside a full-width charcoal background, the page content sits in a centered, fixed‑width column that feels like a spotlighted stage for an industrial machine. The dark header band with the yellow stencil logo and navigation anchors the top. Directly beneath, a slim, centered search bar runs horizontally, echoing the navigation rhythm but with more breathing room above and below. A large, cinematic machine summary block then takes over most of the initial viewport: a wide, dark card with the machine name, model, key specs, and a strong visual or schematic of the CNC unit, framed by generous margins on all sides, effectively replacing the original hero image. Below this hero-like block, the configurator area splits into two vertical columns: on the left, a tall stack of configuration option groups, each in its own minimal card; on the right, a vertically aligned price summary panel that stays visible as you scroll, with live pricing, budget controls, and a compact pricing controller inside. The band is spacious, with plenty of dark background showing between columns and sections. Once the user scrolls past the configuration area, a full-width quote request section spans across the central column, with the form fields laid out in a clean grid and ample space around them. Message panels for errors or confirmations appear inline in the center, replacing either the hero or the quote band when relevant, maintaining the straightforward vertical narrative. At the bottom-right of the viewport, a small floating controller echoes the mockup’s utility button, subtly hovering above the dark background to give access to configuration state tools while staying out of the primary visual path.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search bar is positioned just below the fixed header, centered within the main content column. It spans roughly 60% of the content width, leaving generous dark margins on the left and right so it feels like a lightweight band rather than a full block. There is a vertical gap of about 32–40px between the bottom of the header and the top of the search bar, and another 32–40px between the bottom of the search bar and the top edge of the machine summary hero block. The field height is moderate (40–48px), with 16–24px horizontal padding inside the input and an 8px gap between any icon and text. On smaller screens, the bar expands to nearly 100% of the content width with reduced side margins but maintains similar vertical breathing room.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow, aligned to the top-center of the main content column; it scrolls away as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The configurator initializer itself is mostly invisible when idle, but when active (e.g., loading or reloading a configuration), it manifests as a centered overlay aligned to the middle of the viewport. A semi-transparent charcoal scrim covers the hero block, configuration columns, and quote section, dimming the background while keeping the broader layout faintly visible. In the exact center of the viewport, a compact rectangular loading panel (approximately 320–400px wide and 160–200px tall) appears, with 24px padding, a spinner or mechanical icon aligned left or centered, and concise status text below or beside it. The overlay respects the dark theme and keeps sufficient margin to the viewport edges (at least 48px) to avoid feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed as a fixed, full-viewport overlay with a high z-index, centered both vertically and horizontally; it temporarily blocks interaction with underlying content until initialization completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When an initialization or configuration error occurs, the configurator error state appears inline in the main content column, occupying the central position where the machine summary block would normally sit. It is a wide, horizontally centered message panel spanning about 60–70% of the content width, with ample dark margins on either side to preserve the spacious feel. The panel’s height is determined by its content but typically sits around 160–240px, with 24–32px padding on all sides and at least 32–48px of vertical space above and below to separate it from the search bar and any subsequent content. An icon or emphasis element aligns to the left, with error headline and explanatory copy stacked to the right or underneath. While this panel is visible, the primary configurator sections below (option groups, pricing panel, quote form) may be hidden or visually muted to keep the user’s focus on resolving the error.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the vertical flow, centered within the content column; it replaces or pushes down the machine summary header region while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The machine summary header acts as the hero of this page and sits directly below the global search bar (or below the error panel when present), centered within the content column. It is a wide, dark card spanning about 80% of the content width, leaving roughly 10% margin on each side within the central column, visually echoing the framed hero image from the mockup. Vertically, it occupies around 40–50% of the initial viewport height on desktop, with 32px padding on all sides. Inside, the layout divides into two internal columns: on the left (about 60% of the card width), the machine name in a large industrial display type, model designation, short descriptive text, and perhaps a couple of key specs; on the right (about 40%), a machine photo, render, or technical schematic that mimics the cinematic hero feel. The card has at least 48px of space below it before the configurator 2‑column band starts. On smaller screens, the internal columns stack, with the image or visual appearing above or below the text, maintaining the same outer margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main document flow; it anchors the top of the configurator content and scrolls offscreen as the user moves down to configuration details.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The configuration option groups list forms the left column of the configurator band, positioned directly beneath the machine summary header. The band starts about 48px below the bottom edge of the hero card. On desktop, the list occupies roughly 60–65% of the content width, aligned flush to the left edge of the central column, with a 24–32px horizontal gap separating it from the right-hand price summary column. Each option group appears as its own flat card or panel with 24px internal padding, a group title at the top, and the relevant controls below. The groups are stacked vertically with 24–32px gaps between them, giving a rhythm of discrete modules down the page. The left column can extend well below the fold, while the right column’s pricing panel remains pinned for context. On smaller screens, this list spans 100% of the content width and appears above the price summary panel, maintaining the same vertical spacing between groups.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content forming the left column of a 2‑column layout on larger screens; collapses to a full-width, single-column stack on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pricing controller sits at the top of the right-hand column, closely associated with the price summary panel. Visually, it appears as a slim horizontal control row directly above or integrated into the top edge of the price summary card, aligned to the right side of the configurator band. It spans about 60–80% of the width of the right column, leaving a small margin on the left and right within that column. Typical contents might include a currency selector, tax inclusion toggle, or pricing mode switch, arranged horizontally with 8px spacing between elements and 12–16px vertical padding. There is a 16–24px vertical gap between the bottom of this controller strip and the main body of the price summary content. On mobile, it sits just above the price summary panel, full-width, but still visually connected as part of the pricing block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; it scrolls together with the price summary panel but remains visually attached to it as a control header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price summary panel occupies the right column of the configurator band, aligned to the top of the first configuration option group on the left. On desktop, it spans about 35–40% of the content width and sits flush to the right edge of the central column, with a 24–32px horizontal gap separating it from the configuration options list. The panel is a vertically oriented dark card with 24–32px internal padding and slightly more visual weight than the option cards. The current total price appears near the top in larger type, followed by a concise list of key cost components and the embedded budget target control. The card’s height is sufficient to fit these elements without feeling cramped—roughly 260–400px depending on content—but it is allowed to extend taller if needed. There is clear space (24–32px) beneath the card before any following elements in the right column. On smaller screens, the panel becomes full-width and sits below the configuration options list with the same 48px vertical separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the right column on larger screens: when the user scrolls, the panel remains visible, pinned near the top of the viewport with an offset roughly equal to the header height (e.g., 80–96px), while the left column scrolls independently. On smaller screens, it behaves as a normal static block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget target control is placed inside the price summary panel, occupying a full-width subsection below the main price display and any high-level price breakdown. It appears as a clearly delineated sub-block—such as a slider, input field row, or stepped selector—spanning nearly 100% of the panel’s internal width with 16–24px horizontal padding on each side. Vertically, it has 16–24px internal padding and is separated from the elements above and below by 16–24px of whitespace, maintaining a clear breathing area within the price panel. This control may include a label aligned to the left, with the interactive element (slider/input) stretching horizontally and its numeric value aligned to the right, following the minimal, mechanical aesthetic. It does not extend beyond the edges of the price panel, preserving the card’s clear frame.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content nested within the price summary panel; it inherits the panel’s sticky behavior on desktop, remaining visible as part of the right column price card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote request form sits as a distinct full-width section beneath the configurator band. There is a generous vertical gap of about 64px between the bottom of the lowest configuration option group (or the bottom of the sticky price panel, whichever extends further) and the top of the quote form section. The form is placed in a wide, horizontally centered card spanning approximately 80% of the content width, mirroring the width and framing of the machine summary hero block above. Inside this card, 32px padding on all sides provides ample breathing room. On desktop, the form fields are arranged in a balanced 2‑column grid: contact and personal details on the left, company and project details on the right, with 24px horizontal spacing between the internal columns and 16–24px vertical spacing between rows. A prominent submit button is aligned either to the right or full-width at the bottom, with at least 24px of space above and below it. On smaller screens, the form collapses to a single column while preserving generous vertical spacing, and it still maintains the same outer margins to keep the premium, spacious feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, forming its own section in the vertical flow below the configurator band; it scrolls naturally and becomes the final major content block on the page before the footer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: After a successful quote submission, the quote confirmation panel appears in the same vertical slot as the quote request form, effectively replacing that card. It is a centered message panel spanning about 60–70% of the content width, with generous dark margins on both sides to give it a more focused, celebratory feel compared to the larger form area. The panel has 24–32px padding inside and may include a confirmation icon, a bold headline, supporting text, and a concise summary of next steps. There is at least 48px of vertical space above and below the panel to separate it from any adjacent content. If desired, the now-submitted configuration summary or a link back to the machine overview can be aligned beneath the main confirmation message, still within the same card. On smaller screens, this panel becomes nearly full-width but maintains comfortable padding and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow, occupying the same vertical space where the quote-request-form previously appeared; it either overlays the form in place or appears after the form collapses, without changing the global layout structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The configuration state encoder functions mostly behind the scenes, but when represented visually, it takes the form of a small, unobtrusive floating control that echoes the mockup’s bottom-right utility button. It appears as a compact circular or rounded-square icon button (approximately 40–48px in diameter/size), positioned in the bottom-right corner of the viewport, floating above the dark background and all primary content. It is offset from the right and bottom edges by about 24–32px, ensuring it feels deliberately placed and not cramped. The button may use a subtle mechanical icon (e.g., link, chip, or cog), with a minimal glow or border to distinguish it from the background. It does not compete visually with main CTAs but remains visible as the user scrolls through the machine summary, configurator band, and quote form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport in the bottom-right corner with a high but subordinate z-index (below full-screen overlays); it remains in place as the user scrolls and can expand into a small popover if interaction is required, without shifting the underlying layout.

name: Spare Parts Listing
title: Spare Parts
description: Spare parts catalog page that lets users filter by machine model, model year, part category, and availability, sort the results, and open spare part details from a list of compatible parts.

core_features: Load spare parts filter options (machine models, part categories, availability values, sort options) using getSparePartsFilterOptions and bind them to filter and sort controls.
Parse URL parameters (machineModelId, modelYear, partCategory, availability, sortBy, page) on page load to initialize the current filter, sort, and pagination state.
List spare parts using listSpareParts with server-side filtering, sorting, and pagination based on the current state derived from URL parameters.
Allow users to refine results by machine model, model year, part category, and availability; updating the URL parameters and reloading the list via listSpareParts.
Allow users to change the sorting order (e.g., by price or availability) via a sort control bound to sortOptions from getSparePartsFilterOptions and reflected in the sortBy URL parameter.
Provide pagination controls that use the page URL parameter to request the corresponding subset of spare parts from listSpareParts.

Provide a global search input (in the shared layout) that uses globalSearch to suggest spare parts; selecting a suggestion navigates to the spare part detail page (product.html) with appropriate URL parameters.
Enable navigation from spare part cards to the spare part detail page (product.html) with itemType=spare_part and the selected part id in the URL.
Enable navigation to the cart page (cart.html) from the header cart icon without altering the cart on this page.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Landing on the Spare Parts page with preselected machine and filters
1. User navigates from another page (such as a machine product page) to parts.html; the URL includes one or more of the parameters machineModelId, modelYear, partCategory, availability, sortBy, page.
2. On initialization, the page reads the current URL parameters and normalizes them to an internal state object (e.g., converting modelYear and page to numbers if valid).
3. The page calls getSparePartsFilterOptions and stores the returned machineModels, partCategories, availabilityOptions, and sortOptions.
4. The page validates the incoming machineModelId and sortBy against the loaded filter options; if they are invalid, they are removed from the state (and later from the URL).
5. The page constructs a filters object from the validated parameters (e.g., filters.machineModelId, filters.modelYear, filters.partCategory, filters.availability) and determines the effective sortBy value (from the URL if valid, otherwise a default from sortOptions).
6. The page calls listSpareParts with { filters, sortBy, page, pageSize }.
7. Once results are returned, the spare parts list component renders the part cards and the pagination controls.
8. The filter controls, sort control, and pagination controls are initialized to reflect the current state derived from the URL, so the user sees which filters are active.

Workflow 2: Changing filters (machine model, model year, category, availability)
1. The user changes a filter in the filter bar (for example, selects a different machine model from the machine model dropdown).
2. The filter bar emits an onFilterChange event with the updated filter values.
3. The URL-state manager component merges these new filter values into the current state, resets page to 1, and updates the URL parameters (using history.pushState or history.replaceState) so that machineModelId, modelYear, partCategory, and availability reflect the new filter state.
4. The URL-state manager then calls listSpareParts with the updated filters, current sortBy, page=1, and pageSize.
5. The spare parts list component re-renders based on the new results, and the pagination controls reset to the first page.
6. The active-filters summary component updates to show the new active filters, allowing the user to clear individual filters or all filters.

Workflow 3: Changing the sorting of spare parts
1. The user opens the sort dropdown (which is populated from sortOptions) and selects a different sort option.
2. The sort control emits an onSortChange event with the selected sortBy value.
3. The URL-state manager updates the internal state, sets sortBy to the selected value, resets page to 1, and updates the sortBy and page URL parameters accordingly.
4. The URL-state manager calls listSpareParts with the current filters, the new sortBy, page=1, and pageSize.
5. The spare parts list and pagination components refresh with the newly sorted results, and the selected option remains highlighted in the sort dropdown.

Workflow 4: Paging through spare parts
1. The user clicks a pagination control (e.g., a specific page number or next/previous button).
2. The pagination component emits an onPageChange event with the chosen page number.
3. The URL-state manager updates the internal state and sets the page URL parameter to the new page number without changing the filters or sortBy.
4. The URL-state manager calls listSpareParts with the current filters, current sortBy, the new page, and pageSize.
5. The spare parts list re-renders to show the items for the selected page, and the pagination component updates its current-page indicator.

Workflow 5: Opening a spare part detail from the listing
1. The user clicks on a spare part card (or a dedicated detail button within the card).
2. The spare parts list component emits an onItemClick event with the selected spare part id.
3. The page navigates to product.html with URL parameters itemType=spare_part and id set to the selected spare part id.
4. The product detail page is responsible for loading the spare part details and providing any add-to-cart functionality.

Workflow 6: Using global search to jump to a spare part
1. The user types into the global search input available in the page header.
2. As the user types, the global search component calls globalSearch with parameters query (the current text) and entityTypes containing the spare parts entity type identifier.
3. globalSearch returns an object that includes a spareParts array; the search component displays these results as suggestions.
4. The user selects a spare part from the suggestions list.
5. The search component navigates directly to product.html with itemType=spare_part and id set to the selected spare part id.
6. The spare parts listing page itself remains in the background or is replaced depending on the navigation behavior.

Workflow 7: Clearing filters via active-filters summary
1. The user reviews the active-filters summary component, which displays chips or labels representing currently active filters.
2. The user clicks an action to remove a single filter (for example, removing the partCategory filter).
3. The active-filters summary emits an onRemoveFilter event with the identifier of the filter to remove.
4. The URL-state manager updates the internal state to remove the specified filter, sets the corresponding URL parameter to empty or removes it entirely, resets page to 1, and updates the URL.
5. The URL-state manager calls listSpareParts with the updated filters, current sortBy, and page=1.
6. The spare parts list and filter controls update to reflect the removed filter.
7. If the user clicks a “clear all” action, the active-filters summary emits an onClearAll event, leading the URL-state manager to remove all filter-related URL parameters, reset sortBy to its default, set page=1, and re-fetch the list with no filters applied (other than the default sort).
interactions: Filter selection: When the user selects or changes a machine model, part category, availability option, or enters/changes a model year, the filter-bar component emits onFilterChange with the new filter set. The URL-state manager updates URL parameters (machineModelId, modelYear, partCategory, availability), resets page to 1, and triggers listSpareParts with the updated state.

Filter form apply: If filters are applied via a dedicated apply action (e.g., submit button), the filter-bar emits onSubmitFilters; this behaves the same as onFilterChange but only after the user confirms, enabling batched filter changes.

Clear individual filter: Clicking the remove action for a single active filter in the active-filters summary emits onRemoveFilter, which removes that filter from URL parameters and reloads listSpareParts.

Clear all filters: Clicking a clear-all action emits onClearAll, causing the URL-state manager to remove machineModelId, modelYear, partCategory, and availability from the URL, reset page to 1 and sortBy to its default, then call listSpareParts with an empty filters object.

Sort change: Selecting a new sort option in the sort dropdown emits onSortChange with the chosen sortBy value. The URL-state manager updates the sortBy URL parameter, resets page to 1, and re-calls listSpareParts.

Pagination click: Clicking a pagination control emits onPageChange with the requested page number. The URL-state manager updates the page URL parameter and re-calls listSpareParts with the same filters and sortBy but the new page.

Spare part card click: Clicking a spare part card or its detail button emits onItemClick with the spare part id. The page navigates to product.html with URL parameters itemType=spare_part and id set accordingly.

Add-to-cart intent from card (if present): If there is an add-to-cart button on the card, clicking it emits onAddToCartClick with the spare part id. Since no cart-modification interface is assigned to this page, this event navigates to product.html with itemType=spare_part and id set to the selected part so that the add-to-cart operation can be handled on the product detail page.

Global search input: Typing into the header search box triggers onInput events; after debounce, the global-search component calls globalSearch with the current text and entityTypes restricted to spare parts. Selecting a suggestion emits onSelectSuggestion with the spare part id and leads to navigation to product.html.
Header cart icon click: Clicking the cart icon in the header navigates directly to cart.html without changing any URL parameters on parts.html or modifying the cart contents from this page.

Browser back/forward navigation: When the user uses the browser’s back or forward buttons, a popstate event is captured by the URL-state manager. It re-parses the URL parameters, updates the filter, sort, and pagination state, synchronizes all UI controls, and calls listSpareParts to reflect the historical state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is entirely derived from and synchronized with URL parameters so that the Spare Parts listing is fully bookmarkable and sharable.

1) Incoming URL parameters
- machineModelId (string): If present, this is used to initialize filters.machineModelId. After getSparePartsFilterOptions resolves, the value is validated against the machineModels list; if not found, it is removed from the state and from the URL.
- modelYear (number): If present and a valid number, it is used to initialize filters.modelYear. Invalid numeric values (e.g., non-numeric strings) are ignored and removed from the URL when the state is next written.
- partCategory (string): If present, it initializes filters.partCategory. After getSparePartsFilterOptions, the value is validated against partCategories.value; invalid values are cleared from state and URL.
- availability (string): If present, it initializes filters.availability. After getSparePartsFilterOptions, the value is validated against availabilityOptions.value; invalid values are cleared from state and URL.
- sortBy (string): If present, it attempts to set the current sortBy. After getSparePartsFilterOptions, the value is validated against sortOptions.value; if invalid, sortBy falls back to a default (for example, the first sortOptions item), and the URL is updated to reflect the effective sortBy value.
- page (number): If present and a valid positive integer, it sets the current page. If invalid or missing, page defaults to 1. If the user triggers a filter or sort change, page is automatically reset to 1 and the URL updated.

2) State construction and data loading
- After parsing the URL, the URL-state manager builds an internal state object:
  - state.filters = { machineModelId?, modelYear?, partCategory?, availability? } (properties are only included if corresponding URL parameters are valid).
  - state.sortBy is derived from the sortBy parameter or defaulted from sortOptions.
  - state.page is derived from the page parameter or set to 1.
- Once getSparePartsFilterOptions data is available and the state is validated, the page calls listSpareParts with:
  - filters: state.filters
  - sortBy: state.sortBy
  - page: state.page
  - pageSize: a value determined by global application configuration or higher-level settings (not hardcoded in this page's logic).

3) URL updates on interactions
- On filter changes (including clear actions), the state.filters object is updated, state.page is set to 1, and the URLSearchParams are rewritten to reflect only the active filters and page=1. Parameters corresponding to inactive filters are removed rather than set to empty strings.
- On sort change, state.sortBy is updated and written to the sortBy URL parameter, state.page is reset to 1, and other parameters (filters) are preserved.
- On pagination, only the page parameter changes; filters and sortBy remain unchanged.
- The URL is updated using pushState or replaceState to avoid full page reloads while keeping browser history in sync.

4) Reacting to URL changes
- On initial load and on each popstate event (back/forward navigation), the URL-state manager re-parses URLSearchParams and reconstructs state.filters, state.sortBy, and state.page exactly as on first load.
- The filter-bar, sort control, active-filters summary, and pagination controls receive updated props or state based on this reconstructed state, ensuring visual and functional consistency with the URL.
- listSpareParts is called again whenever the parsed state differs from the current in-memory state, ensuring that the displayed list always matches the URL.

5) Consistency and validation
- The page never assumes fixed values for machine models, categories, or availability options. Instead, it relies entirely on the dynamic lists from getSparePartsFilterOptions and validates the URL parameters against those lists.
- If validation changes the effective state (for example, a removed machineModelId), the URL is normalized to remove or correct invalid parameters, so that future reloads or shared links reflect only valid, supported filters.

components.functionality: Parses URL parameters into internal filter, sort, and pagination state; validates this state against options from getSparePartsFilterOptions; calls listSpareParts with the current state; and updates the URL when the user changes filters, sorting, or pagination. Listens for browser history (popstate) events to restore previous states.

Displays controls for selecting machine model, entering model year, choosing part category, and selecting availability. Initializes its fields based on the current URL-derived state. Uses machineModels, partCategories, and availabilityOptions from getSparePartsFilterOptions. Emits filter change events when the user modifies any control.

Shows a summary of currently active filters (machine model, model year, part category, availability) derived from the URL-state manager. Provides actions to remove individual filters or clear all filters, and delegates these actions back to the URL-state manager.

Displays a dropdown populated with sortOptions from getSparePartsFilterOptions. The current selection reflects the sortBy value from the URL-derived state. When the user selects a different sort option, it emits an event so the URL-state manager can update the URL and re-call listSpareParts.

Renders a grid or list of spare part cards based on items returned by listSpareParts. Each card displays spare part information (such as name, price, availability) and exposes actions to open the detail page and optionally initiate an add-to-cart intent which is handled via navigation to the detail page.

Displays pagination based on the page, pageSize, and totalItems values returned by listSpareParts. When the user selects a different page, it emits an event that the URL-state manager uses to update the page URL parameter and reload the results.

Implements the global search input available in the header on this page. Calls globalSearch with the current text and entityTypes configured to include spare parts. Shows suggestions based on the returned spareParts array. Selecting a suggestion navigates directly to product.html with itemType=spare_part and id set to the selected spare part id.

On page load, calls getSparePartsFilterOptions and passes the results (machineModels, partCategories, availabilityOptions, sortOptions) to the filter-bar, sort-control, and URL-state manager. Ensures that listSpareParts is only called after filter options are available and URL parameters have been validated.

Displays an appropriate message when listSpareParts returns no items for the current filters or when the interfaces return errors. It listens to the results and error states from listSpareParts and the loaders, and shows or hides itself accordingly.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered fixed-width grid (approximately 1140–1200px max-width) on a full-bleed dark background, conceptually compatible with a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing dark header, all page content sits inside a centered, fixed-width column (approx. 1140–1200px max) on a full-bleed charcoal background. Components are stacked vertically in a clean, linear flow: global search at the top of the content area, followed by the filter bar, then a slim active-filters + sort row, and below that the main spare-parts list occupying most of the viewport height, with pagination at the bottom. State-related components (loaders, URL manager, empty/error state) overlay or replace the main results area without breaking the underlying vertical structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing dark header, all page content sits inside a centered, fixed-width column (approx. 1140–1200px max) on a full-bleed charcoal background. Components are stacked vertically in a clean, linear flow: global search at the top of the content area, followed by the filter bar, then a slim active-filters + sort row, and below that the main spare-parts list occupying most of the viewport height, with pagination at the bottom. State-related components (loaders, URL manager, empty/error state) overlay or replace the main results area without breaking the underlying vertical structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column occupies roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, leaving 10–15% dark margins on each side to echo the hero framing from the mockup. Vertical spacing between major bands (search, filters, results, pagination) follows the 48–64px section rhythm, while internal spacing between related controls (within the filter bar, between active filters and sort control) uses 16–24px. The spare-parts list is the dominant block, consuming the majority of vertical space below the filters, similar to how the hero image dominated the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout remains deliberately low-density and typography-led. Each functional band (search, filters, results) has generous breathing room above and below, and individual controls use 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. Instead of packing many elements into tight rows, filters are grouped into a single, clearly separated bar; the active filters and sort sit in a light, minimal row with ample whitespace; the list uses a regular grid or stacked cards with consistent, comfortable gaps between them.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mimics the mockup’s F/Z pattern: after the eye is drawn from the logo in the header, it lands on the global search near the top-left of the content column, then scans right across the filters band. From there it drops diagonally into the central spare-parts list, which acts as the new ‘hero’ area. The eye then moves horizontally across the list items. The sort control at the top-right of the results band and the pagination centered at the bottom of the list form secondary anchors that complete a Z-shaped scan path within the results area.

layout.overall_description: The spare parts listing page sits inside the same dark, industrial shell as the rest of the site: a full-bleed charcoal background, a fixed-height header with a yellow stencil logo on the left and navigation on the right, and below that a centered content column framed by generous dark margins. At the top of this content column, a wide, understated global search bar stretches across most of its width, visually replacing the hero headline while keeping the focus on functionality. A full-width filter bar sits just beneath, like a sleek control panel for the machines, with clearly separated dropdowns and inputs. Directly below it, a low-profile row shows active filter chips aligned left, and a compact sort dropdown aligned right, forming the top edge of the main results area. The spare-parts list itself becomes the visual centerpiece: a large, dark card-like area with either a grid or vertical stack of part entries, each spaced evenly in a comfortable rhythm and aligned with the column’s inner width. Pagination controls sit centered below the list, giving a clear end to the page content but still surrounded by ample negative space. When loading or when no parts are available, simple, centered states overlay or replace the results area without disturbing the overall calm, spacious structure. The entire layout feels like a modern control terminal: dark, minimal, and precise, with the main interaction zones clearly highlighted in the middle of the screen and nothing extraneous crowding the user’s attention.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is a non-visual controller that conceptually sits at the page root, just inside the content shell below the global header. It occupies no visible space and does not alter the layout grid. It monitors and updates the URL query parameters as users adjust filters, search, sorting, or pagination, ensuring those UI components reflect the URL state on load and on navigation. Spatially, it is aligned with the full content column but is completely transparent to the user, layered behind all visible components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered controller with no physical footprint; logically scoped to the full page content region but not visible or interactive.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search bar is the first visible component in the main content area, positioned directly below the header with a vertical offset of approximately 48–64px to maintain the airy separation seen between header and hero in the mockup. It is horizontally centered within the fixed-width content column, spanning roughly 8–10 of the 12 grid columns (about 70–80% of the content width), with equal dark margins on both sides. The input field itself is a single, wide row with subtle borders and 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, emphasizing clarity and ease of use. There is at least 24–32px of vertical whitespace below the search bar before the filter bar begins, ensuring it reads as a distinct, primary control rather than blending into the filters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element in the normal document flow, aligned with the central grid. On scroll, it scrolls away with the rest of the content (filters may become the sticky anchor instead).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar sits immediately below the global search bar, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the content column (all 12 grid columns), forming a strong horizontal band akin to a control console. Within this band, filter controls for machine model, model year, part category, and availability are arranged left-to-right in a single row on large screens, each occupying 2–3 columns with consistent spacing (16–24px gaps) between them. Each control is rendered as a compact, dark-surfaced input or dropdown with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, maintaining the minimal, industrial aesthetic. The top and bottom padding within the filter bar itself echo a card-like block, with about 16–24px of padding from the bar edges to the controls. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space below the bar before the active filters + sort row begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a block element in the flow, but on desktop can become sticky: when the user scrolls past its original position, the filter bar pins to the top of the viewport (just beneath the main header), maintaining its full width and allowing the spare-parts list to scroll beneath it. On smaller screens it may switch to non-sticky if necessary for usability.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active filters summary appears directly beneath the filter bar, forming the left side of a thin, low-profile row that spans the full content width. The row is separated from the filter bar by around 16–24px, reinforcing it as a secondary, informational layer. Active filters are shown as compact pills starting at the left edge of the content column, each with 8px inner spacing between icon/text and 8–16px gaps between pills. They occupy approximately the left 2/3 of the row space, wrapping to a second line if needed but keeping a clear left alignment. This row maintains a low visual density, with plenty of negative space above and below (about 8–12px) to keep it unobtrusive yet accessible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the normal document flow, sharing a horizontal row with the sort control. If the filter bar is sticky, this row may also become sticky in tandem, staying just below the filter bar as the user scrolls, or it may scroll away while the filter bar alone remains pinned, depending on implementation constraints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is positioned in the same horizontal row as the active filters summary, aligned to the far right edge of the content column. It is visually a compact dropdown or icon-labeled control with 8–12px vertical and 16px horizontal padding, sitting flush with the right grid edge. The control occupies about 2–3 grid columns on desktop. The row’s height is dictated by the sort control, with the active filters on the left naturally aligning vertically around its center. There is a small, consistent gap (16–24px) between this row and the top of the spare-parts list, visually marking the transition from ‘controls’ to ‘results’.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline-block or right-aligned flex item within the active-filters row. It may participate in a sticky behavior together with the filter bar, staying visible at the top of the viewport when the user scrolls down the results, creating a fixed control strip above the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The spare parts list occupies the main body of the page and acts as the functional equivalent of the hero image from the mockup. It begins approximately 16–24px below the active-filters/sort row and extends down the page, often filling the majority of the visible viewport height. Horizontally, it spans the full width of the content column (all 12 grid columns), but each item within the list is arranged in a tidy grid or vertical stack with consistent gaps (24–32px) between rows and 24px horizontal gutters between columns. On desktop, you can picture a 2–3 column card layout, where each card represents a spare part: each card uses 16–24px internal padding, follows the dark theme, and aligns its content (image, title, part number, compatibility summary, availability) in a clear vertical hierarchy. The list is visually grounded by aligning its left and right edges precisely with the search and filter elements above, so the entire block reads as a cohesive panel. Ample dark background surrounds the list above and below, preserving the spacious, premium feel. When content is long, the list scrolls beneath any sticky controls, maintaining a clear, anchored top frame of filters and sort.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable block in the normal flow, occupying the entire central column beneath the controls. It does not itself scroll independently; instead, the page scrolls as a whole while the list moves under a potentially sticky filter/sort strip.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are positioned directly below the spare-parts list, separated by approximately 32–48px of vertical space to clearly indicate reaching the end of the current results. They are horizontally centered within the content column, with the pagination component itself spanning around 4–6 grid columns (roughly 30–40% of the content width) and equal dark margins on both sides. Individual page buttons have 8–12px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding, arranged in a single, compact row with 8–12px gaps between them. The overall band has additional top and bottom breathing room (around 8–16px), but remains visually quieter than the list and filter area, acting as a subtle navigational footer to the results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element at the bottom of the main content flow. It scrolls with the page and does not stick or float. It respects any existing floating utility button in the bottom-right corner by keeping its main interactive cluster centered rather than pushed to the edges.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The initial data loader overlays the main content region (from just below the header to just above the footer) during the first load or major data refresh. Visually, it dims or subtly blurs the content area beneath with a semi-transparent charcoal veil, while a single loading indicator (e.g., a minimal circular spinner or progress bar) is centered both horizontally and vertically within the content column. This centered indicator sits roughly in the same vertical zone that the hero image would occupy in the original mockup, drawing the eye to the middle of the screen without adding clutter. The overlay respects the fixed-width content column horizontally, aligning its inner edges to that column while the dimming effect can gently extend to the full viewport background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A fixed-position or absolutely positioned overlay anchored to the main content container, covering search, filters, list, and pagination while allowing the global header to remain visible. It blocks interaction with underlying controls while active and scrolls with the page if the page is longer than the viewport, but the loader itself remains centered within the visible viewport area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-or-error state occupies the same visual footprint as the spare-parts list, effectively replacing the list content while preserving the overall layout. It begins at the same vertical position where the list would start (16–24px below the active-filters/sort row) and spans the full width of the content column. Inside this area, a single, clean message block is centered horizontally and placed slightly above the vertical midpoint of the list area, echoing the hero focus from the mockup. The message may include an icon, headline, and short explanatory text, stacked vertically with 8–16px spacing between elements, all contained within a subtle card with 24–32px padding. Surrounding the card is generous empty dark space, reinforcing the sense of calm and clarity. Below the message, a primary action (e.g., ‘Clear filters’ or ‘Retry’) is centered and separated from the text by 16–24px of space, keeping interaction simple and focused.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered block in place of the spare-parts list. It does not overlay the header, search, or filters; those stay visible so users can adjust them. When active, it scrolls with the rest of the page. If the page is taller than the viewport, the message card stays centered within its section but does not remain fixed to the viewport.

name: Training List
title: Training Courses
description: Lists CNC training courses with filtering, sorting, and navigation to detailed course pages for registration.

core_features: Display a paginated list of training courses using the listTrainingCourses interface, showing key attributes such as title, level, delivery method, start date, location, duration, and price where available.

Allow users to filter courses by delivery method (online, in-person, hybrid), skill level (beginner, introductory, intermediate, advanced), and start date range, and apply these as filters in the listTrainingCourses call.
Support sorting of the course list via the sortBy URL parameter (e.g., by start date, duration, or price, ascending or descending), passing the selected sort to listTrainingCourses.
Allow users to navigate between pages of results using pagination controls that update the page URL parameter and the listTrainingCourses call.
Enable users to open individual course detail pages (training_detail.html) by clicking on a course entry, passing the selected course id as courseId in the URL.
Provide access to the global search functionality from the page header using the globalSearch interface to search across machines, spare parts, documents, and case studies.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Arrive from navigation with or without preset filters
1. User clicks the 'Training' or 'Training & Education' navigation link, or follows a link from index.html that may include deliveryMethod, level, and/or startDateFrom query parameters.
2. The page shell parses URL parameters: deliveryMethod, level, startDateFrom, optional startDateTo (if present), sortBy, and page.
3. The filter bar initializes its controls based on these URL values (e.g., pre-selecting delivery method and level and prefilling the date range start/end fields).
4. The page constructs a filters object for listTrainingCourses using the parsed parameters (e.g., { deliveryMethod, level, startDateFrom, startDateTo }).
5. The page invokes listTrainingCourses with { filters, sortBy, page, pageSize }.
6. When the response returns, the course list component renders the items along with pagination controls using totalItems, page, and pageSize.
7. The user reviews the list and can proceed to refine filters, change sorting, or open a course detail.

Workflow 2: Adjust filters and date range
1. User changes one or more filter controls in the filter bar (e.g., selects a different delivery method, level, or updates the start date range).
2. The filter bar emits an event with the updated filter values to the page shell.
3. The page shell updates the URL query string to reflect the new filters (e.g., ?deliveryMethod=online&level=beginner&startDateFrom=YYYY-MM-DD&startDateTo=YYYY-MM-DD) and resets page to 1, removing any parameters that no longer have a value.
4. After updating the URL, the page shell reconstructs the filters object from the URL parameters.
5. The page invokes listTrainingCourses with the new filters and page=1.
6. The course list re-renders with the updated items and refreshed pagination.
7. If no items are returned, the list component shows an empty-state message based on the current filters (without using any hardcoded course data).

Workflow 3: Change sorting
1. User opens the sorting control and selects a sort option (e.g., earliest start date first, price ascending, price descending, duration based ordering, depending on available sortBy options).
2. The sorting component emits an event with the newly selected sortBy value to the page shell.
3. The page shell updates the sortBy parameter in the URL and resets page to 1 while preserving current filters.
4. The page shell reconstructs the filters and sortBy from the URL.
5. The page invokes listTrainingCourses with { filters, sortBy, page: 1, pageSize }.
6. The course list re-renders in the new order, and pagination controls remain consistent with totalItems and pageSize.

Workflow 4: Paginate through results
1. User clicks a pagination control (e.g., next page, previous page, or a specific page number).
2. The pagination component emits an event with the new target page number to the page shell.
3. The page shell updates the page parameter in the URL (e.g., ?page=3) while preserving current filters and sortBy.
4. The page shell parses the URL again to reconstruct filters, sortBy, and page.
5. The page invokes listTrainingCourses with the current filters, sortBy, and the new page value.
6. The course list component renders the items for the requested page and updates active-state indications in the pagination control.

Workflow 5: Open a course detail page
1. User clicks on a course card or row in the course list.
2. The course list component reads the id of the clicked TrainingCourse item.
3. The component triggers navigation to training_detail.html, appending the course id as courseId in the query string (e.g., training_detail.html?courseId={id}).
4. The browser loads training_detail.html, which will use the courseId to display detailed information and handle registration (outside the scope of this page).

Workflow 6: Use global search from the training list page
1. User types a query into the global search bar in the header while on the training_list.html page.
2. On submit, the global search component calls the globalSearch interface with the query string and a chosen set of entityTypes (e.g., ['machine_models', 'spare_parts', 'documents', 'case_studies'] or equivalent identifiers), depending on sitewide search configuration.
3. The globalSearch response returns collections of MachineModel, SparePart, DownloadableDocument, and CaseStudy items matching the query.
4. The global search component displays results in a search UI (e.g., a dropdown or redirects to a global search results page) according to global site behavior; this does not alter the training_list filters, sort, or pagination.
5. If the global search UI navigates to a separate search results page, the training_list.html state remains encoded in its URL for when the user navigates back via browser controls or navigation links.
interactions: Changing delivery method filter: User selects a delivery method in the filter bar. Event handler updates the deliveryMethod URL parameter (or removes it if cleared), resets page to 1, and triggers a new listTrainingCourses call with the updated filters.

Changing level filter: User selects a level (beginner, introductory, intermediate, advanced). Event handler updates the level URL parameter (or removes it if cleared), resets page to 1, and triggers a new listTrainingCourses call.

Selecting a start date range: User picks start date range values in the filter bar. Event handler updates startDateFrom and optional startDateTo URL parameters (or removes them if cleared), resets page to 1, and triggers listTrainingCourses with the new date filters.

Resetting filters: User clicks a 'Reset filters' control (if provided). Event handler removes deliveryMethod, level, startDateFrom, and startDateTo from the URL, resets page to 1, and calls listTrainingCourses with an empty filters object (or only globally enforced filters).

Changing sort order: User selects a sorting option in the sort control. Event handler updates the sortBy URL parameter, resets page to 1, and triggers listTrainingCourses with the existing filters and new sortBy value.
Changing page: User clicks pagination controls. Event handler updates the page URL parameter and triggers listTrainingCourses with the current filters and sortBy but the new page value.
Clicking a course: User clicks on a course entry in the list. Event handler reads the TrainingCourse.id and navigates to training_detail.html?courseId={id}.

Submitting global search: User submits the global search form. Event handler calls globalSearch with the user's query and predefined entityTypes and then either displays inline results or navigates to a search results page according to global behavior.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is fully expressed through URL query parameters and server responses, without using localStorage.

1) URL Parameters -> Page State
- deliveryMethod (string): If present, used to filter by TrainingCourse.deliveryMethod. Accepted values are aligned with TrainingCourse.deliveryMethod enum (e.g., 'online', 'in_person', 'hybrid'). If missing, no delivery method filter is applied.
- level (string): If present, used to filter by TrainingCourse.level. Accepted values match TrainingCourse.level enum (e.g., 'beginner', 'introductory', 'intermediate', 'advanced'). If missing, no level filter is applied.
- startDateFrom (string, ISO date): If present, used as the minimum start date for TrainingCourse.startDate in filters. If missing, no lower bound on start date is applied.
- startDateTo (string, ISO date, optional extension): If present, used as the maximum start date for TrainingCourse.startDate. This parameter may be set by this page when the user uses the upper bound of the date range picker; if missing, no upper bound is applied.
- sortBy (string): If present, determines the sort order passed to listTrainingCourses (e.g., 'start_date_asc', 'start_date_desc', 'price_asc', 'price_desc', 'duration_asc', etc., according to backend support). If missing, the frontend applies its configured default sort when calling listTrainingCourses.
- page (number): If present and a valid positive integer, determines which page of results is requested from listTrainingCourses. If missing or invalid, it is treated as 1.

2) Constructing filters object for listTrainingCourses
On every load or URL change, the page shell:
- Reads deliveryMethod, level, startDateFrom, and startDateTo from the URL.
- Builds a filters object including only the parameters that have valid values. For example, if deliveryMethod and startDateFrom are present, filters might be { deliveryMethod, startDateFrom }.
- Passes this filters object to listTrainingCourses along with sortBy, page, and a configured pageSize value.

3) Keeping URL and UI in sync
- On initial load: The filter bar and sort controls initialize their UI values from the parsed URL parameters so the visible selections always match the state used for listTrainingCourses.
- On user interaction: Filter, sort, and pagination components never change state only internally; each interaction updates the URL parameters first, then triggers listTrainingCourses based on the new URL. This ensures sharable, bookmarkable URLs for any filtered/sorted/paged state.
- On browser navigation: Using the browser back/forward buttons changes the query string. The page listens for URL changes, reparses the parameters, updates control states, and calls listTrainingCourses accordingly so the UI reflects the navigated state.

4) Handling API responses
- listTrainingCourses response fields items, page, pageSize, and totalItems are used to render the course list and pagination; no course data is hardcoded.
- The pagination component derives totalPages from totalItems and pageSize, and validates requested page numbers against this range before emitting navigation events.

5) globalSearch state
- globalSearch invocations from this page do not modify the training_list URL parameters. Any state for search results is handled in the global search component or a separate search results page, so training_list state remains stable and URL-driven.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL state, invokes listTrainingCourses, provides data and callbacks to all child components (filters, sorting, list, pagination), and keeps UI state synchronized with URL parameters.

Displays and manages filters for delivery method, skill level, and start date range. Reads initial values from URL-derived state and notifies the page shell when the user changes filters or resets them.

Provides sorting options for the course list (e.g., by start date, price, or duration). Reflects the current sortBy state from the URL and notifies the page shell when the user selects a different sort option.
Renders the list of TrainingCourse items returned by listTrainingCourses, displaying key details for each course and allowing the user to click an item to navigate to its detail page.
Renders pagination controls based on page, pageSize, and totalItems from listTrainingCourses, and emits events when the user navigates between pages.

Implements the global search input available in the header on this page. Uses the globalSearch interface to search across machines, spare parts, documents, and case studies without affecting the training list filters.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered fixed-width grid (approximately 1140–1200px max-width) on a full-bleed dark background, conceptually compatible with a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: a full-width dark page shell with a fixed-height header at the very top (logo left, nav center/right, language selector far right), then a hero-like training header band containing the page title and global search, followed by a horizontal filter bar, then the training list with a sort control at its top-right, and finally centered pagination at the bottom. All content sits in a centered, fixed-width column (≈1140–1200px) within the dark full-bleed background.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: a full-width dark page shell with a fixed-height header at the very top (logo left, nav center/right, language selector far right), then a hero-like training header band containing the page title and global search, followed by a horizontal filter bar, then the training list with a sort control at its top-right, and finally centered pagination at the bottom. All content sits in a centered, fixed-width column (≈1140–1200px) within the dark full-bleed background.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies roughly 10–12% of the viewport height. The training hero/search band below it takes up the upper part of the main area (about 25–30% of the viewport height on first load), dominated by a wide, cinematic industrial CNC image with overlaid page title and search. The filter bar is a shallow strip beneath this band. The course list then takes the majority of the remaining vertical space. Side margins leave ~10–15% of the screen width as dark negative space on each side, with the content column spanning about 70–80% of the total width. Vertical gaps between major sections (hero → filters → list → pagination) are in the 32–64px range to keep the layout spacious.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low, premium-feeling density: few elements per row, plenty of dark breathing room between sections, and vertically separated course cards rather than tightly packed rows. Filters and sorting are grouped into a single, well-spaced band rather than scattered. The search area has generous padding and whitespace above and below, mirroring the hero focus from the mockup but adapted to functional content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve an F/Z-pattern. The eye is first drawn to the yellow industrial logo at top-left, then moves across the header navigation to the language selector at top-right, forming the top stroke. It then drops diagonally into the training hero area where the page title and large central search field sit over the cinematic CNC imagery. From there, attention moves horizontally across the filter bar, then down into the vertically stacked course cards, scanning left-to-right within each. A small floating utility button in the bottom-right of the viewport (part of the page shell) acts as a secondary attractor at the end of the visual path, similar to the mockup’s bottom-right control.

layout.overall_description: The Training List page sits within a full-bleed charcoal shell. At the top, a low, dense header bar anchors the yellow stencil-style logo on the far left, navigation links across the center, and a compact language selector with a flag icon on the far right. Beneath this, the main content begins with a wide, cinematic hero band centered on the page: a dark, high-contrast CNC training image stretches across roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, framed by deep dark margins. Over this image, aligned to the left half of the content column, sits the page title and a prominent, wide training search field. Below the hero band, a sleek horizontal filter bar spans the full content column, with filter controls laid out in a row on the left and secondary actions on the right. The training courses themselves appear as a vertical stack of wide, card-like rows, each occupying the full width of the content column with comfortable separation between them. At the top-right edge of this list area, a compact sort control aligns with the first course card, balancing the filters visually. Finally, centered below the list, a simple pagination row provides navigation through pages of courses. Throughout, the layout feels spacious and industrial: dark surfaces, clear typography, strong horizontals, and consistent 8px-based spacing. A small, round floating utility button sits in the bottom-right of the viewport, offset neatly from the edges, reinforcing the mockup’s floating-control pattern without competing with the primary content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page shell spans the full browser viewport with a solid charcoal background. At the very top sits a fixed-height horizontal header bar (about 72–88px tall), stretching edge-to-edge. Inside this header, content is constrained to a centered max-width column (≈1140–1200px): the logo is anchored at the far left, navigation items form a horizontal row starting just to the right of the logo, and the language selector with a flag icon is right-aligned to the far edge of the column. Below the header, the main content area is centered on the page, leaving roughly 10–15% of the viewport width as dark side margins on each side. The top of main content begins with a hero-like training header band: a wide, cinematic CNC imagery block sits centered within the content column, spanning roughly 70–80% of the viewport width and about 25–30% of the viewport height on desktop, with 40–64px of dark margin above and to the sides. The training title and global search sit within this band (described in that component’s layout), aligned to the content column grid rather than the image edges. Directly beneath this hero band, the filter bar, course list, sort control, and pagination stack vertically in a single central column, each separated by 32–48px of vertical spacing. A small circular utility button (e.g., support/help) floats at the bottom-right of the viewport, offset about 24–32px from the right and bottom edges, overlaying all content while staying clear of the pagination and footer areas.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The shell background and main column are static. The header is a fixed-height bar at the top of the document (not overlaying content as sticky by default). The floating utility button within the shell remains fixed relative to the viewport bottom-right corner as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search sits within the hero-like training header band at the top of the main content area. Inside the centered content column, the search is aligned slightly left-of-center, creating a strong anchor point: the page title appears above it, left-aligned, and the search field sits directly below the title with about 16–24px vertical gap. The search field itself spans roughly 60–70% of the content column width, forming a long, horizontal input that visually echoes the mockup’s wide hero image. It is horizontally centered within the column but slightly biased to the left to align with the logo and main text column. There is generous padding around it: about 32–40px of space above (to the bottom of the header band imagery) and 32–40px below, before the filter bar begins, making it a clear focal point. On taller screens, the hero band plus search occupy the upper third of the viewport before any list items appear, preserving a focused, cinematic feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero band; it scrolls away with the hero content. On smaller screens the search field reduces in width to maintain comfortable side padding within the column but retains its dominant, single-row layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar is positioned immediately below the hero/search band, separated by roughly 32–40px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the centered content column, forming a low, horizontal strip with a slightly lighter dark background to distinguish it from the page shell. Internally, it has 16–20px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding. Filter controls (e.g., dropdowns for machine type, difficulty level, location, date) are laid out from left to right in a single row, each occupying roughly equal width segments, with 16px gaps between them. The left edge of the first filter aligns with the main text column. On the far right of the filter bar, any secondary controls like a “Reset filters” or “More filters” button are right-aligned to the column edge, mirroring the language selector’s alignment in the header above. Visually, the bar feels like a functional, minimalist control panel, sitting as a clear horizontal divider between the more visual hero area and the content-heavy list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The filter bar initially sits below the hero band, but as the user scrolls, it becomes sticky to the top of the viewport (just below the main header), maintaining its full width aligned to the content column. When sticky, it preserves its height and padding, providing always-available filtering without crowding the view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is anchored at the top of the course list area, just below the filter bar. There is about 24–32px of vertical space between the bottom of the filter bar and the row containing the sort control. Within the centered content column, the sort control sits on the far right side of this row, aligned to the column’s right edge. On the left side of the same row, aligned to the column’s left edge, there can be a simple text label such as “X trainings found,” creating a balanced left-right composition. The sort control itself is compact, roughly 200–260px wide, appearing as a dark, flat dropdown or segmented control with clear, white or light gray label text. It does not span the full width; instead it occupies the rightmost third of the row, leaving the central area mostly empty to preserve the layout’s airy quality.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The sort control scrolls with the course list; it is not sticky. On smaller screens it can wrap onto its own row beneath the results-count label, still right-aligned within the column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The course list sits directly below the sort-control row, with a vertical gap of about 24px. It occupies the full width of the centered content column and extends downward as the primary scrollable content. Each course appears as a horizontally oriented card or row, spanning nearly 100% of the column width, leaving only the standard inner padding to the card edges. Cards have a slightly lighter dark background than the page shell, with 24–32px of internal padding and 24–32px of vertical spacing between cards, reinforcing the low-density, premium feel. Within each card, information can be arranged in a two-column structure: the left two-thirds for course title, brief description, and meta-tags (e.g., level, machine family), and the right one-third for key details like next available date, duration, location, and a primary CTA button. All cards align flush with the left and right edges of the content column, creating a strong central vertical axis. As the user scrolls, these cards form a clean vertical stack, with the dark background of the page visible between them, preserving the cinematic, gallery-like impression seen in the hero section but applied to structured content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The list is a standard vertically scrolling region within the page; it does not have its own internal scroll. It expands to fit the number of courses on the current page. On smaller screens the internal two-column card layout collapses to a single column while maintaining the same outer alignment and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are located below the last course card, separated by approximately 32–48px of vertical space. They are horizontally centered within the content column, occupying only as much width as needed for the page numbers and previous/next controls. The row is low and compact, with 8–12px gaps between individual page number buttons and slightly larger padding around the current page indicator to make it subtly stand out. The entire pagination strip sits well above the bottom of the viewport on longer pages, with at least 64px of margin to any footer or end content, ensuring it does not feel cramped. The dark theme continues, with flat, unobtrusive controls that do not visually compete with the course cards but are clearly discoverable at the end of the scroll path.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it sits at the bottom of the list region and scrolls in and out of view with the content. On narrow screens, the pagination row remains centered and may switch to a condensed layout (e.g., fewer visible page numbers) but retains its central alignment and vertical spacing.

name: Training Detail
title: Training Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a specific training course and provides a registration workflow using the courseId URL parameter.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for a specific training course using getTrainingCourseDetails with the courseId URL parameter.
Show key course attributes such as title, description, delivery method, level, start and end dates (or duration), location, price, and maximum participants using the TrainingCourse model.
