component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow, mutually exclusive with the dealer-info-panel. It replaces the hero content when active, maintaining the layout’s structural dimensions so the page doesn’t shift dramatically between loading, error, and loaded states.

name: Downloads
title: Downloads
description: Page for locating and downloading technical documents (manuals, safety instructions, datasheets, etc.) for CNC machines using filters and search.

core_features: Initialize dynamic filter controls for product category, machine model, document type, and language using getDownloadFilterOptions, and preselect values from URL parameters when present.
Retrieve and display a paginated list of downloadable documents using listDownloadableDocuments, filtered by the current URL parameters.
Allow users to refine results by changing filter selections and applying them, which updates the URL parameters and refreshes the document list without requiring local storage.
Provide direct download actions for each listed document using the fileUrl returned in DownloadableDocument items.
Integrate the global header search (globalSearch) so users can search across entities and jump directly to relevant documents or pre-filtered views on the downloads page.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Find documentation by filters:
1. User opens downloads.html without any specific parameters.
2. On page load, the filter component calls getDownloadFilterOptions to obtain available product categories, machine models, document types, and languages.
3. The page reads URL parameters (productCategory, machineModelId, modelName, documentType, language, page). Since none are set, no filters are preselected and the page number defaults to the first page.
4. The document list component calls listDownloadableDocuments with an empty or minimal filters object (no filter values) and omits sortBy so the backend default ordering is used, along with page = 1.
5. The user selects a product category and (optionally) a specific machine model from the dynamically populated options, and chooses a document type and language.
6. The user clicks an “Apply filters” action in the filter component.
7. The filter component updates the browser URL parameters with the selected values (productCategory, machineModelId, modelName derived from the selected MachineModel, documentType, language) and resets page to 1.
8. A URL change listener on the page detects the updated parameters and triggers a new listDownloadableDocuments call with filters corresponding to the URL.
9. The document list updates to show only matching DownloadableDocument items; the active filters summary component reflects the current filters.
10. The user clicks the download control for a document row, which initiates a file download using that item’s fileUrl.

Workflow 2 – Arrive pre-filtered from a product detail page:
1. On product.html, the user clicks a contextual “Documentation / Downloads” link for the current machine model.
2. That link navigates to downloads.html with URL parameters populated from the product context (for example: productCategory, machineModelId, and optionally modelName, documentType, and language chosen by the product page).
3. On page load, the downloads page reads these URL parameters and passes them to the filter component.
4. The filter component calls getDownloadFilterOptions, then matches the incoming productCategory, machineModelId, modelName, documentType, and language to the options it received.
5. Matching filter controls are preselected to reflect the incoming parameters; any non-matching values are ignored in the UI but remain in the URL until the user changes them.
6. The document list component calls listDownloadableDocuments with filters built from the validated URL parameters and with page set to the URL’s page value or to 1 if none is provided.
7. The user sees only documents relevant to that machine and chosen document type/language (for example, an English user manual for a specific model).
8. If the user changes the documentType (for example, from user manual to safety instructions) while keeping the same model and language, the filter component updates only the documentType URL parameter and resets page to 1.
9. The document list refreshes using the updated filters, effectively switching the results from one document type to another while preserving machine context.

Workflow 3 – Use global search to locate documents:
1. On the downloads page, the user types a search phrase into the global search bar in the header.
2. When the user submits the search, the global search component calls globalSearch with the query text and an entityTypes array that includes documents.
3. globalSearch returns grouped results (machineModels, spareParts, documents, caseStudies).
4. The search component can either display an overlay of results or navigate to a dedicated search results page; for document-type results specifically, each result entry exposes enough information (including identifiers) to construct navigation back to downloads.html.
5. If the user chooses a document-oriented shortcut such as “View all documentation for this model”, the search component navigates to downloads.html with appropriate URL parameters (e.g., productCategory, machineModelId or modelName, and possibly documentType and language inferred from context).
6. The downloads page then follows Workflow 2, preselecting filters and listing matching documents based on the URL.

Workflow 4 – Paginate through many documents:
1. The user has already applied filters, and the document list shows the first page of documents.
2. The listDownloadableDocuments response includes metadata: totalItems, page, and pageSize.
3. The pagination component uses these values to compute how many pages of results exist.
4. The user clicks a page navigation control (e.g., “Next page” or a specific page number).
5. The pagination component updates the page URL parameter to the selected page number, leaving all other parameters unchanged.
6. A URL change listener triggers a new listDownloadableDocuments call with the same filters but with the updated page value.
7. The document list component re-renders to show the documents for the selected page while all filter controls and their selections remain unchanged.

Workflow 5 – Clear filters and start over:
1. The user has several active filters (e.g., specific product category, machine model, document type, and language) visible in the active filters summary.
2. The user clicks a “Clear all filters” action in the summary component.
3. The summary component removes all filter-related URL parameters (productCategory, machineModelId, modelName, documentType, language) and resets page to 1 in the URL.
4. The filter component receives the URL change, clears its selected values in the UI, and returns to an unfiltered state.
5. The document list component calls listDownloadableDocuments again with an empty filters object and page = 1.
6. The user now sees the default unfiltered list of documents and may start a new filtering process.
interactions:
Filter selection change:
- Trigger: User selects or changes a product category, machine model, document type, or language in the filter component.
- Behavior: The UI updates the in-memory filter state. The user can either see an immediate list update or click an explicit “Apply” control (depending on configuration). When filters are applied, the component updates the URL parameters (productCategory, machineModelId, modelName, documentType, language) and resets page to 1. A URL change listener then triggers a new listDownloadableDocuments call with filters built from the URL.

Apply filters action:
- Trigger: User clicks an “Apply filters” button.
- Behavior: The filter component composes the filters object using current selections and writes the corresponding URL parameters. It does not call APIs directly; instead, the URL change causes the document list component to call listDownloadableDocuments, ensuring URL-driven state.

Remove individual filter:
- Trigger: User clicks a remove control on a specific active filter in the active filters summary (e.g., remove language filter only).
- Behavior: The summary component removes the corresponding parameter from the URL while leaving other filter parameters unchanged and resets page to 1. The resulting URL change triggers a new listDownloadableDocuments call with an updated filters object.

Clear all filters:
- Trigger: User clicks a “Clear all filters” control.
- Behavior: The summary component removes all filter-related parameters from the URL and sets page to 1. After the URL changes, the filter component clears its selections, and the document list calls listDownloadableDocuments with no filters applied.

Pagination interaction:
- Trigger: User clicks a page number, “Previous”, or “Next” in the pagination controls.
- Behavior: The pagination component updates only the page URL parameter to the selected value. The URL change triggers listDownloadableDocuments with the same filters and the new page value. The list component updates based on the new response.

Document row click:
- Trigger: User clicks on a document entry (row or card) in the list.
- Behavior: Depending on configuration, this may expand details (such as version, language, and publication date) or initiate a download. If details are expanded, no additional API call is required; data is taken from the DownloadableDocument item already returned by listDownloadableDocuments.

Download button/icon click:
- Trigger: User clicks the download button/icon on a document entry.
- Behavior: The browser initiates a file download or navigation using the fileUrl field from the DownloadableDocument. No additional backend interface is called; this uses the URL already provided in the listDownloadableDocuments response.

Global search submit:
- Trigger: User submits a query in the global search bar.
- Behavior: The global search component calls globalSearch with the query text and an entityTypes list that includes documents. It displays results grouped by entity type. For document-related results, selecting a result can either initiate a direct download (using its fileUrl) or navigate to downloads.html with appropriate URL parameters to show related documents.

Back/forward browser navigation:
- Trigger: User uses browser back or forward buttons.
- Behavior: A popstate or equivalent URL-change listener on the page reads the updated URL parameters and updates filter selections and pagination state accordingly, then calls listDownloadableDocuments with filters derived from the new URL. This maintains consistency between browser history and visible filters/results.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Downloads page is entirely driven by URL parameters, with no use of localStorage or other client-side persistent storage. On initial load and whenever the URL changes, the page reads productCategory, machineModelId, modelName, documentType, language, and page from the query string. These values are treated as the single source of truth for the current filter and pagination state.

1) Initialization:
- On page load, a central state controller parses URL parameters.
- The filter component requests available filter options by calling getDownloadFilterOptions.
- Once filter options are loaded, the component attempts to match URL parameter values to valid options:
  • productCategory is matched against the list of productCategories returned by getDownloadFilterOptions.
  • machineModelId is matched against the machineModels list; if a MachineModel is found, its name is used as the modelName for display.
  • modelName can be used for display where an exact MachineModel match is not found.
  • documentType and language are matched against their respective options (documentTypes and languages) from getDownloadFilterOptions.
- Any invalid or unmatched values are ignored in the UI but can remain in the URL until the user changes or clears filters.
- The page determines the initial page value: if page is a positive number in the URL, it is used; otherwise the page defaults to 1 but does not have to be written back to the URL immediately.

2) Building API filters from URL:
- Whenever a listDownloadableDocuments call is needed, the page constructs a filters object based on the current URL parameters:
  • If productCategory is present and valid, filters.productCategory is set to that value.
  • If machineModelId is present and valid, filters.machineModelId is set accordingly.
  • If modelName is present, filters.modelName may also be included to support document-level matching by name.
  • If documentType is present and valid, filters.documentType is set.
  • If language is present and valid, filters.language is set.
- Only parameters that have valid values are included in the filters object. sortBy is omitted so the backend default ordering is used.
- listDownloadableDocuments is then called with { filters, sortBy: undefined or omitted, page: parsedPageFromUrl or 1, pageSize: omitted so the backend default applies }.

3) URL as the source of truth:
- Any user action that changes filters or pagination (selecting a different category, changing document type, changing language, switching models, or moving to another page) first updates the URL parameters and only then (via a listener) triggers API calls. This ensures deep-linkable states and shareable URLs.
- For example, changing documentType while keeping the same productCategory and model updates only the documentType URL parameter and resets the page parameter to 1.
- The page parameter is always kept in sync with the currently displayed page of results.

4) Incoming navigation scenarios:
- From index.html or product.html, navigation links to downloads.html can pass any subset of productCategory, machineModelId, modelName, documentType, language, and page.
- The Downloads page does not assume any particular combination; it simply reads whatever parameters are present and applies the above logic.
- When multiple identifying parameters are present (such as both machineModelId and modelName), machineModelId is used as the primary key for machine model selection, while modelName is used as a display label and may be included in filters for DownloadableDocument if needed.

5) Outgoing navigation scenarios:
- When a user refines filters (for example, to find a specific language version of a manual or to switch to safety instructions for the same model), the filter component composes a new URL using the current selections and navigates to that URL on the same downloads.html page.
- This corresponds directly to the described outgoing connections: adjusting documentType or language while keeping productCategory and model-related parameters unchanged results in navigation to a new URL on downloads.html with updated query parameters.

By consistently deriving API calls from URL parameters and writing all user-driven state changes back to the URL, the page guarantees that reloads, bookmarks, and shared links will always reproduce the same filter and pagination state without any static or hardcoded data.

components.functionality: Provides a global search input in the header that calls globalSearch and allows users to access documents and other entities from the Downloads page. For document-related results, it can navigate back to downloads.html with appropriate filter parameters or initiate direct downloads.

Fetches available filter options via getDownloadFilterOptions, initializes filter controls based on URL parameters, and manages the current filter state (product category, machine model, document type, language). When the user applies filters, it updates the URL parameters so that the document list can refresh via listDownloadableDocuments.

Displays the currently active filters (derived from URL parameters and filter component state) as removable tokens or labels. Allows the user to remove individual filters or clear all filters, which updates URL parameters and indirectly triggers listDownloadableDocuments.

Calls listDownloadableDocuments with filters derived from URL parameters and displays the resulting paginated list of DownloadableDocument items, including key metadata such as title, version, language, and file size. Provides a download action for each item using the fileUrl field.

Reads pagination metadata (page, pageSize, totalItems) from the latest listDownloadableDocuments response and renders pagination controls. User interaction updates the page URL parameter and triggers reloading the document list for the selected page.

Centralizes reading and writing of URL parameters for filters and pagination. On load and on browser navigation events, it parses current URL parameters and notifies the filter and document list components. When these components request state changes (e.g., filter apply, page change), it updates the URL accordingly.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered fixed-width grid (approximately 1140–1200px max-width) on a full-bleed dark background, conceptually compatible with a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: full-width dark header (logo + nav) at the top, then a centered main content column. For the Downloads page, the visual hero image is replaced by a "functional hero" – a prominent, centered global search band at the top of the content area. Below this, content continues in a vertical stack but splits into a two-column region: a left-hand filter sidebar and a larger right-hand document list with its active filters summary sitting just above it. Pagination sits at the bottom of the list, still inside the centered content column. All major elements stay within a fixed-width (approx. 1140–1200px) central grid, surrounded by a full-bleed dark background.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: full-width dark header (logo + nav) at the top, then a centered main content column. For the Downloads page, the visual hero image is replaced by a "functional hero" – a prominent, centered global search band at the top of the content area. Below this, content continues in a vertical stack but splits into a two-column region: a left-hand filter sidebar and a larger right-hand document list with its active filters summary sitting just above it. Pagination sits at the bottom of the list, still inside the centered content column. All major elements stay within a fixed-width (approx. 1140–1200px) central grid, surrounded by a full-bleed dark background.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Header height remains roughly 10–15% of the viewport. Below it, the search band occupies the upper portion of the main viewport (similar visual weight to the original hero), spanning about 70–80% of the content width and centered. The two-column filters + results region occupies most of the remaining vertical space: the left filter sidebar takes about 25–30% of the content width, while the right document list takes 70–75%. Consistent dark margins of roughly 10–15% of the viewport width remain on the far left and right, keeping the central grid floating within the dark shell. Vertical gaps between major blocks (search, filters/summary, list, pagination) follow the 48–64px section spacing, with intra-component spacing of 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep density low and premium: generous whitespace between the global search and the filters/results, ample vertical spacing between filter groups and document rows, and clear separation between the document list and pagination. No cluttered controls; each interactive area uses 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. The filter sidebar is structured but not packed; document items are card-like rows with breathing room between them, echoing the single-hero focus of the mockup but translated into a list context.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow continues to follow an F/Z pattern. The eye starts at the bright logo in the top-left, moves horizontally across the navigation, then drops down to the prominent, centered global search bar (replacing the hero image as the main focal point). From there, the gaze moves diagonally down to the left filter sidebar, then across to the right into the top of the document list. The eye then scans down through the list and ends on the pagination controls near the bottom-right of the content column, which echo the mockup’s bottom-right utility button as a secondary end-of-path anchor.

layout.overall_description: The Downloads page sits within the same full-bleed charcoal shell as the rest of the site: a fixed-height dark header at the top with the industrial yellow stencil logo on the left, navigation in the middle, and the language selector on the right. Below this, the main content is a centered column (about 1140–1200px wide) floating within wide dark side margins. At the top of this column, occupying the visual space where the hero image would be, is a prominent, horizontally centered global search bar that stretches across roughly 70–80% of the content width, with generous vertical padding above and below so it feels like a functional hero band. Beneath it, the layout resolves into a two-column structure: a tall, dark filter sidebar aligned to the left and taking about a quarter of the width, and a wider document results area on the right taking the remaining width. Just above the document list in the right column sits a compact active filters summary strip, visually connected to the list. Documents appear as a vertical stack of dark, card-like rows with ample spacing, filling most of the visible area. At the bottom of the list, pagination controls sit aligned toward the right edge of the content column with generous top and bottom padding, forming the final anchor in the visual path. Throughout, the dark background, wide margins, and disciplined spacing keep the page feeling minimal, industrial, and premium, replacing the single cinematic hero with a focused search-and-filter "machine" for documents.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search bar is the first functional element in the main content area, immediately below any page title/intro and well below the fixed header. It is horizontally centered within the main content column and spans roughly 70–80% of that column’s width, leaving even dark margins on its left and right within the content area. There is a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px from the bottom edge of the header to the top of the search bar, so it occupies the prime hero-like band in the upper half of the viewport. The search component itself is a single, wide input (with optional icon/button) that feels like a solid, industrial control: about 56–64px tall, with 16–20px internal padding. Below the search bar, there is about 32px of clear space before the two-column filters/results region begins, ensuring the search stands on its own visually as the main starting point for the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the normal document flow, centered within the fixed-width grid. It scrolls away with the page content rather than remaining sticky, mirroring the hero image behavior in the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar is realized as a vertical sidebar positioned directly beneath the global search area, aligned to the left edge of the main content grid. The overall content width is conceptually divided into 12 columns: the filter sidebar occupies roughly 3–4 columns (about 25–30% of the content width), while the document list to its right uses the remaining 8–9 columns. There is a vertical gap of about 32px between the bottom of the search bar and the top of the filter sidebar, maintaining consistency with the spacious feel. The sidebar extends downward alongside the document list, forming a tall column of grouped filters (e.g., machine type, document type, year). Each filter group is separated by 24px of vertical space, and inner controls (checkboxes, dropdowns, chips) have 8–16px spacing, keeping the column structured but not crowded. The left edge of the sidebar aligns with the left edge of the content column, leaving the same 10–15% outer dark margin on the far left of the viewport as seen around the hero in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The sidebar participates in normal layout but becomes sticky relative to the viewport once scrolled: its top edge remains pinned roughly 96px below the top of the viewport (to clear the header and search band), so filters stay accessible while the document list scrolls beside it. On smaller screens, it would naturally stack above the document list, but within the desktop layout it acts as a left-hand sticky column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active filters summary sits in the right-hand content region, visually bridging the search at the top and the document list below. Horizontally, it aligns with the document list column rather than the full page width, occupying the same 70–75% width to the right of the filter sidebar. Its left edge lines up precisely with the left edge of the first document row, and its right edge lines up with the right edge of the document column, creating a clean vertical alignment. Vertically, it is placed about 24px below the global search bar and roughly level with the top of the filter sidebar, so that the top of the filters on the left and the active filter summary on the right form a balanced horizontal band. The summary itself is a slim bar with filter chips or badges, with 12–16px padding and 8px spacing between chips, and 16–24px of space below it before the first document item starts. When no filters are active, this area shrinks to a minimal row or collapses, leaving more whitespace before the list while still preserving the vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the right-hand column, moving with the document list as the user scrolls. It does not stick to the top of the viewport, but remains visually anchored to the top of the results area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The document list occupies the main right-hand column beneath the global search and active filters summary, opposite the filter sidebar. In the conceptual 12-column grid, it spans about 8–9 columns (70–75% of the content width), starting at the vertical line where the filter sidebar ends. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space between the bottom of the active filters summary and the top edge of the first document item. Each document appears as a horizontal card or row with a slightly elevated contrast against the dark background, with 16–20px internal padding and 16–24px vertical spacing between rows. The list stretches downward, often filling the remainder of the viewport height and requiring scroll, with the right edge of all cards aligning cleanly underneath the navigation bar and search width. The left side of the list is visually balanced by the filter sidebar, creating a stable two-column composition. The overall list area maintains a comfortable right margin inside the content column, which in turn is inset from the viewport edges, echoing the generous margins around the original hero image.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable content within the page; the list itself scrolls as one continuous column alongside the sticky filter sidebar. It does not have its own internal scroll container, avoiding nested scrollbars.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls sit at the bottom of the document list, contained within the same right-hand column. There is a generous 32–48px of vertical space between the last document row and the top of the pagination controls, allowing the list to feel complete before the navigation appears. Horizontally, the pagination aligns to the right edge of the document list column, echoing the mockup’s bottom-right utility emphasis and forming the final focal point in the user’s scan path. Buttons or page numbers are laid out in a compact horizontal row, with 8–16px spacing between items and 12–16px internal padding per control. The overall pagination block occupies roughly 40–50% of the document column width, leaving dark space to its left and reinforcing its utility nature rather than competing with the document content. Below the pagination, an additional 48–64px of bottom padding separates it from the page bottom, mirroring the generous margins above the hero in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the end of the main content flow. It scrolls into view as the user reaches the bottom of the list and does not remain fixed to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state controller is a non-visual, infrastructural component with no on-screen footprint. It resides logically at the page level, coordinating the state of the search, filters, document list, and pagination. It does not occupy any physical space in the layout and does not affect the spacing, alignment, or dimensions of visible components. All visible elements are laid out as if this controller were not present, with its behavior only reflecting in URL/query changes and synchronized state across components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual spatial behavior; it is effectively invisible in the UI and exists solely as an underlying logical layer without rendered dimensions or positioning.

name: Industries Overview
title: Industries Overview
description: Page for viewing a selected manufacturing industry, including its overview, recommended CNC milling machines, and related case studies with filters, sorting, and navigation to detailed case studies and product listings.

core_features: Load and display overview information for the selected industry using the industryId URL parameter and the getIndustryOverview interface.
Display recommended machine models for the selected industry, with navigation links to the products listing page filtered by the machine’s product category and machine type.
Display a highlighted case studies section for the selected industry using highlightedCaseStudies from getIndustryOverview.
List industry case studies using listIndustryCaseStudies with filters for machineType and material, sort options (e.g., by date), and pagination based on URL parameters.
Indicate which case studies in the list are in the user’s favorites using getFavoriteCaseStudies.
Provide interactive filters (machineType, material) and sorting controls that update URL parameters and refresh the case study list via listIndustryCaseStudies.
Provide global search functionality via globalSearch to search across machines, spare parts, documents, and case studies from the page header or a global search area.
user_workflows:
View an industry overview:
  1. User arrives on industries.html with an industryId URL parameter.
  2. The page controller reads industryId and calls getIndustryOverview(industryId).
  3. The industry overview header displays the industry name and description.
  4. Recommended machines and highlighted case studies returned by getIndustryOverview are rendered in their respective components.

Browse and filter industry case studies:
  1. On page load, the controller reads industryId, machineType, material, sortBy, and page from the URL.
  2. The page calls listIndustryCaseStudies with parameters: industryId, filters (machineType, material), sortBy, page, and a defined pageSize.
  3. The case study list displays the returned items.
  4. The case study filters read the current machineType and material from the URL and set their initial selections.
  5. The user changes machineType or material in the filter controls.
  6. The filter component updates the URL parameters (machineType, material) and resets page to 1.
  7. The controller detects the URL change and calls listIndustryCaseStudies again with updated filters.
  8. The refreshed case study list shows only matching case studies.

Sort and paginate case studies:
  1. The user selects a sort option (e.g., newest first) in the sort control.
  2. The sort control updates the sortBy URL parameter and resets page to 1.
  3. The controller calls listIndustryCaseStudies with the new sortBy parameter.
  4. The case study list re-renders in the new order.
  5. The user clicks a pagination control (e.g., next page or a specific page number).
  6. The pagination component updates the page URL parameter.
  7. The controller calls listIndustryCaseStudies with the new page value; the list updates to show the corresponding page.

Open a case study detail page:
  1. In either the highlighted case studies or the main case study list, the user clicks a case study item.
  2. The click event navigates the browser to case_study.html with the caseStudyId set to the clicked case study’s id in the URL parameters.
  3. The detail page handles the caseStudyId and shows full case study content.

Navigate to relevant machine products for the industry:
  1. The recommended machines component displays machine models from getIndustryOverview.recommendedMachineModels.
  2. The user clicks a machine item.
  3. The click event navigates to products.html, setting categoryId to the selected MachineModel.productCategory and machineType to the selected MachineModel.machineType.
  4. The products page uses those URL parameters to present an appropriate product listing.

Identify favorite case studies in the listing:
  1. On page load, the controller calls getFavoriteCaseStudies().
  2. The returned favorite case studies are stored in page state.
  3. When rendering case studies (both highlighted and in the list), components compare each CaseStudy.id with the set of favorite.caseStudyId values.
  4. Matching case studies are marked as favorites (e.g., via an icon or label) but without modifying favorites (read-only on this page).

Use global search from the industry overview page:
  1. The user types a search term into the global search input and submits the form.
  2. The search component calls globalSearch with the query and selected entityTypes (e.g., machines, documents, case studies) as appropriate.
  3. The component displays inline search suggestions or a compact results panel using the returned machineModels, spareParts, documents, and caseStudies.
  4. When the user clicks a result, the component navigates to the relevant detail or listing page based on that result’s type (navigation behavior is defined globally for search results, outside this page’s own interfaces).
interactions:
Page load / URL change:
  - Trigger: Browser loads industries.html or the URL parameters change (industryId, machineType, material, sortBy, page).
  - Behavior: The controller reads parameters and calls getIndustryOverview(industryId), listIndustryCaseStudies({industryId, filters, sortBy, page, pageSize}), and getFavoriteCaseStudies().
  - Outcome: All dependent components re-render using the new data and parameters.

Industry selection via internal navigation:
  - Trigger: User clicks an internal link to another industry (e.g., in a navigation menu) pointing to industries.html?industryId=<other>.
  - Behavior: Browser navigates; the page controller detects the new industryId and re-runs getIndustryOverview and listIndustryCaseStudies with the new industryId, resetting filters and pagination according to URL defaults.
  - Outcome: The page now reflects the newly selected industry’s overview, machines, and case studies.

Machine type filter change:
  - Trigger: User selects or clears a machineType value in the case study filter controls.
  - Behavior: The filter component updates the machineType URL parameter (or removes it if cleared) and sets page=1.
  - Outcome: The controller re-calls listIndustryCaseStudies with the updated filters, and the case study list updates.

Material filter change:
  - Trigger: User selects or clears a material value in the case study filter controls.
  - Behavior: The filter component updates the material URL parameter (or removes it if cleared) and sets page=1.
  - Outcome: The controller re-calls listIndustryCaseStudies with the updated filters, and the case study list updates.

Sort order change:
  - Trigger: User selects a different sort option for case studies.
  - Behavior: The sort control updates the sortBy URL parameter (e.g., to a value corresponding to newest first) and sets page=1.
  - Outcome: The controller re-calls listIndustryCaseStudies with the new sortBy; the list re-renders in the chosen order.

Pagination click:
  - Trigger: User clicks next/previous page or a specific page number in the pagination controls.
  - Behavior: The pagination component updates the page URL parameter.
  - Outcome: The controller calls listIndustryCaseStudies with the new page; the list displays that page of results.

Click on a case study (highlighted or in the list):
  - Trigger: User clicks a case study item.
  - Behavior: Browser navigates to case_study.html?caseStudyId=<id>.
  - Outcome: The case study detail page is loaded and displays the selected case study.

Click on a recommended machine model:
  - Trigger: User clicks a machine in the recommended machines list.
  - Behavior: Browser navigates to products.html with URL parameters categoryId set to the clicked MachineModel.productCategory and machineType set to the clicked MachineModel.machineType.
  - Outcome: The products page shows relevant machine listings for that model category and type.

Global search submission:
  - Trigger: User submits the search form in the global search component.
  - Behavior: The component calls globalSearch({query, entityTypes}) and displays inline results.
  - Outcome: User can click a result to navigate to the corresponding detail or listing page.

Favorite indication (read-only):
  - Trigger: getFavoriteCaseStudies returns data; or listIndustryCaseStudies/getIndustryOverview return case studies.
  - Behavior: The case study components compare case study ids against the favorites set to visually indicate which ones are favorites.
  - Outcome: User can see which case studies they have previously favorited (favorites are not modified on this page).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is fully derived from URL parameters and API responses, with no use of localStorage or other persistent client storage on this page.

- URL parameters:
  - industryId (string, required for main content):
    - Determines which industry overview and case studies to load.
    - When industryId is missing or invalid, the controller does not call getIndustryOverview or listIndustryCaseStudies and the page may display a functional message indicating that no industry is selected.
    - When industryId changes (via navigation), the page re-fetches overview and case studies for the new industry and may reset dependent parameters (e.g., page) according to implementation rules.
  - machineType (string, optional):
    - Represents the currently active machine type filter for case studies.
    - When present, it is passed into listIndustryCaseStudies via filters.machineType.
    - When changed via filter UI, the page updates this parameter and resets page to 1 before re-calling listIndustryCaseStudies.
    - When removed (e.g., user chooses 'All'), the parameter is removed from the URL and machineType is omitted from filters.
  - material (string, optional):
    - Represents the active workpiece material filter for case studies.
    - When present, it is passed into listIndustryCaseStudies via filters.material.
    - When changed via filter UI, the page updates this parameter and resets page to 1 before re-calling listIndustryCaseStudies.
    - When removed, it is omitted from filters.
  - sortBy (string, optional):
    - Controls case study ordering (e.g., by publication date descending when set to a corresponding value).
    - On initial load, if absent, the page may use a default sort order at the UI level but does not hardcode specific case study data; this default is reflected by either leaving sortBy empty (if the backend has its own default) or by setting a default sortBy value.
    - When the user changes the sort order, the component updates sortBy in the URL and resets page to 1.
  - page (number, optional):
    - Represents the current page of the case study list.
    - If absent, the controller uses a default (such as 1) when calling listIndustryCaseStudies.
    - When pagination controls are used, the page parameter is updated and listIndustryCaseStudies is called with the new page value.

- API calls and derived state:
  - getIndustryOverview(industryId):
    - Output fields are stored in transient page state, such as:
      - currentIndustry: Industry
      - recommendedMachineModels: MachineModel[]
      - highlightedCaseStudies: CaseStudy[]
    - These objects directly feed the industry header, recommended machines, and highlighted case studies components.
  - listIndustryCaseStudies({industryId, filters, sortBy, page, pageSize}):
    - Returns a paged set of CaseStudy entities and pagination metadata.
    - The page stores:
      - caseStudyListItems: CaseStudy[] (for the current page)
      - caseStudyListPage: number
      - caseStudyListPageSize: number
      - caseStudyListTotalItems: number
    - These are bound to the case study list and pagination components.
  - getFavoriteCaseStudies():
    - Returns an array of objects containing favorite (CaseStudyFavorite) and caseStudy (CaseStudy).
    - The page derives a set of favorite caseStudyIds from this response for quick lookup.
    - This set is not persisted, only used in-memory to mark case studies across components.

- No local storage:
  - All user-visible state (selected filters, sort order, current page, selected industry) is represented in the URL and re-derived from it on each load.
  - API responses are not cached across page loads beyond what the browser or network layer might provide; functionally, each navigation that changes parameters triggers fresh calls to the relevant interfaces.

components.functionality: Central controller that reads URL parameters, coordinates API calls (getIndustryOverview, listIndustryCaseStudies, getFavoriteCaseStudies), and exposes normalized state to child components.
event_handlers: onMount: Read industryId, machineType, material, sortBy, page from URL; if industryId is valid, call getIndustryOverview(industryId), listIndustryCaseStudies({industryId, filters, sortBy, page, pageSize}), and getFavoriteCaseStudies().

onUrlChange: When any relevant URL parameter changes, re-run listIndustryCaseStudies with updated filters/sortBy/page; if industryId changed, also re-run getIndustryOverview and reset dependent listing state as necessary.
onPageSizeChange (optional): If pageSize is changed by UI or configuration, update internal state and re-call listIndustryCaseStudies.

components.functionality: Displays the selected industry’s name and description based on getIndustryOverview output.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate: When currentIndustry changes in controller state, re-render the name and description.

components.functionality: Shows the list of recommended machine models for the selected industry, each with a navigation link to the products listing filtered by the model’s productCategory and machineType.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate: When recommendedMachineModels from getIndustryOverview change, re-render the list.
onItemClick: On click of a MachineModel item, navigate to products.html?categoryId=<MachineModel.productCategory>&machineType=<MachineModel.machineType>.

components.functionality: Displays highlighted case studies for the selected industry, using data from getIndustryOverview.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate: When highlightedCaseStudies change, re-render the strip.
onFavoritesUpdate: When favorite caseStudyIds from getFavoriteCaseStudies change, update highlight indicators on matching case studies.
onItemClick: On click of a highlighted case study, navigate to case_study.html?caseStudyId=<CaseStudy.id>.

components.functionality: Provides filter controls for case studies by machineType and material, keeping controls in sync with URL parameters.
event_handlers: onMount: Initialize selected filter values from machineType and material URL parameters.
onMachineTypeChange: Update the machineType URL parameter (or remove it if cleared) and reset page=1; this indirectly triggers listIndustryCaseStudies via the controller.
onMaterialChange: Update the material URL parameter (or remove it if cleared) and reset page=1; this indirectly triggers listIndustryCaseStudies via the controller.
onClearFiltersClick: Remove machineType and material from the URL and reset page=1.

components.functionality: Allows users to select the sort order for case studies (e.g., by date), synchronizing with the sortBy URL parameter.
event_handlers: onMount: Initialize selected sort option from the sortBy URL parameter or use a default if none is set.
onSortChange: Update the sortBy URL parameter and reset page=1; the controller re-calls listIndustryCaseStudies with the new sortBy.

components.functionality: Displays the paginated list of case studies for the selected industry, using filters and sort options from URL parameters and indicating which items are favorites.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate: When listIndustryCaseStudies results change, re-render the list.
onFavoritesUpdate: When favorite caseStudyIds from getFavoriteCaseStudies change, update the favorite indicator for each case study.
onItemClick: On click of a case study row or card, navigate to case_study.html?caseStudyId=<CaseStudy.id>.

components.functionality: Provides pagination controls for the case study list based on the current page, pageSize, and totalItems returned from listIndustryCaseStudies.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate: When page, pageSize, or totalItems change, recompute visible page buttons and re-render.
onPageChange: When a user selects a new page (including next/previous), update the page URL parameter; the controller then calls listIndustryCaseStudies with the new page.

components.functionality: Shows a compact list of the user’s favorite case studies (read-only) and allows navigation to their detail pages.
event_handlers: onMount: Request favorites via getFavoriteCaseStudies through the controller and render the resulting caseStudy entries.
onDataUpdate: When favorites data changes, re-render the summary list.
onItemClick: On click of a favorite case study in the summary, navigate to case_study.html?caseStudyId=<CaseStudy.id>.

components.functionality: Provides a global search entry on the page that queries machines, spare parts, documents, and case studies and shows inline results or suggestions.
event_handlers: onSubmit: Call globalSearch({query, entityTypes}) with the user’s query and selected entity type filters; handle the returned machineModels, spareParts, documents, and caseStudies.
onResultsUpdate: When globalSearch returns data, update an inline results list or suggestion panel.
onResultClick: Navigate to the appropriate detail or listing page depending on the result type (navigation behavior is defined by global rules outside this page’s interfaces).

components.functionality: Displays a functional message or placeholder when no valid industryId is provided or when getIndustryOverview fails to return an industry.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate: When the controller cannot obtain a valid industry (e.g., industryId missing or invalid), show the empty state; hide it when a valid industry is present.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered fixed-width grid (approximately 1140–1200px max-width) on a full-bleed dark background, conceptually compatible with a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strictly top-to-bottom, single primary column layout centered on a dark, full-bleed background. Immediately below the global site header, the industry overview functions as the hero section. Under that, content stacks in clear bands: recommended machines, a highlighted case studies strip, then a combined filter/sort control row directly above the main case study list, followed by pagination. The favorites summary follows the floating utility pattern from the mockup and sits above the content in the bottom-right corner of the viewport. The global search is integrated into the top portion of the page, aligned right, echoing the top-bar utility placement from the mockup. State/empty components overlay or replace the central column content as needed without breaking this linear stacking.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strictly top-to-bottom, single primary column layout centered on a dark, full-bleed background. Immediately below the global site header, the industry overview functions as the hero section. Under that, content stacks in clear bands: recommended machines, a highlighted case studies strip, then a combined filter/sort control row directly above the main case study list, followed by pagination. The favorites summary follows the floating utility pattern from the mockup and sits above the content in the bottom-right corner of the viewport. The global search is integrated into the top portion of the page, aligned right, echoing the top-bar utility placement from the mockup. State/empty components overlay or replace the central column content as needed without breaking this linear stacking.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The dark shell spans the full viewport. The centered content column uses roughly 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop (aligned with an ~1140–1200px max width), leaving 10–15% dark margins at the sides. The top header (outside this spec) occupies ~10–15% of viewport height. The industry hero section takes up the majority of the first screen (about 50–70% of viewport height), with a strong visual block (image + text). Subsequent sections (recommended machines, highlight strip, case study list) each behave as horizontal bands with 48–64px vertical spacing between them. Cards, filters, and lists use 16–24px internal padding and 24–32px gaps, maintaining a spacious, premium feel. The floating favorites summary occupies a small footprint (about the size of a compact button/pill) and is inset 24–32px from the bottom and right edges, mirroring the floating button in the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall density is low and controlled. Sections have clear breathing room above and below, and only a limited number of items appear per row (e.g., 2–3 machine cards on desktop, 2 case study cards/columns). Headings, filters, and controls are spaced with at least 16–24px vertical separation to avoid crowding. Interior padding in interactive elements follows a 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal rhythm. The design favors large, legible typography and clear groupings, ensuring that even lists and filters feel clean and uncluttered on the dark background.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow maintains an F/Z-pattern. The eye first lands top-left within the main content on the industry title in the hero header, then travels right across to the top-aligned global search (mirroring the mockup's nav line). From there, it drops diagonally down into the central hero visual and key industry details, then continues downward to the recommended machines section. The next horizontal scan happens across the highlighted case studies strip and then across the filter/sort row above the main case study list. The list invites a vertical scan down through entries. Finally, attention is pulled to the bottom-right floating favorites summary, echoing the mockup’s floating utility button as a secondary attention point for saved items.

layout.overall_description: The page sits inside a full-bleed charcoal background, with all key content aligned to a centered, fixed-width column that occupies about three-quarters of the screen width. At the top of this column, a tall, cinematic industry hero band stretches nearly the full column width, inset from the viewport by wide dark margins. The hero combines a left-anchored industry title, brief overview, and key stats with a large, industrial product-or-process image, establishing a strong, mechanical aesthetic. In the right part of this top area, a compact global search aligns to the upper edge, echoing the utility placement of the original nav bar. Below the hero, the layout drops into a sequence of wide, low-density bands: first a recommended machines grid, then a darker, almost strip-like row for highlighted case studies, followed by a lean horizontal filter/sort control bar that snaps to the top as the user scrolls. Under this bar, the main case studies list occupies the center of the page, using generous vertical spacing between items on the dark background. Pagination controls appear centered beneath the list. Throughout, the lateral margins remain consistent, framing the content and keeping the center column visually prominent. A small, glowing favorites summary floats fixed at the bottom-right, inside the dark shell but above all content, echoing the small floating button of the mockup and acting as a subtle, persistent utility. When data is missing, a large, centered empty state replaces the usual sections, still maintaining the same centered alignment and breathing room.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The industry route controller wraps the entire main content column beneath the global site header. It does not have a visible UI in the resting state; instead, it defines the vertical stack of sections (industry hero, machines, case studies). When in loading or error states, it can temporarily overlay the central content column with a semi-opaque dark layer and a centered status indicator, keeping the focus in the middle of the page while respecting the same lateral margins as the other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual structural wrapper; when active in loading/error states, it overlays the central content column with a centered status layer, aligned to the same max-width and vertical centering without affecting the global header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The industry overview header occupies the first major section below the global site header and acts as the hero. It spans the full width of the centered content column (approximately 70–80% of the viewport width) and roughly half to two-thirds of the initial viewport height on desktop. The entire block is inset from the top of the viewport by about 48–64px and from the side edges by wide dark margins. Inside this band, content is arranged in two conceptual columns on larger screens: on the left 55–60% of the width, the industry name, short description, and key metrics stack vertically with generous 16–24px spacing; on the right 40–45%, a large, cinematic industry or machine image sits, aligned to the right edge of the content column, mirroring the mockup’s central hero image treatment. The background remains dark, with the image either full-bleed within its area or framed slightly, but always visually dominant without overcrowding text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the normal scroll flow; it anchors the top of the content and scrolls away as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recommended machines list sits directly below the industry hero, separated by a generous 48–64px vertical gap to preserve the airy feel. The section heading aligns left with the hero title, forming a clean vertical line. The list content spans the same content column width, with machine cards arranged in a responsive grid: on desktop, 2–3 cards per row depending on viewport width, each card occupying roughly one-third to one-half of the column width with 24–32px gutters between them. Each card uses internal padding of about 16–24px and clear vertical spacing between image, title, and specs to maintain a low-density look. The overall height of this section is moderate, usually allowing the first row or two of cards to be visible immediately below the hero before the fold.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, flowing section; cards reflow from multi-column on desktop to a single column on small screens, always centered within the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The highlighted case studies strip appears beneath the recommended machines section, with about 48px of vertical spacing separating them. It forms a distinct, slightly darker horizontal band within the content column, spanning its full width but remaining within the same left and right margins. The strip is relatively low in height compared to the hero—approximately one-quarter of the viewport height. Inside, 2–3 prominent case study highlight cards are arranged horizontally, either as a horizontally scrollable row or a limited carousel. The cards are shorter and wider than standard list items, emphasizing key imagery and 1–2 lines of text. The section title and any minimal controls (like arrows or a ‘View all case studies’ link) align to the top-left of the strip, with cards stretching to the right, encouraging a left-to-right scan across this row while still feeling visually lightweight.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline section; the horizontal row may scroll/slide horizontally on smaller screens, but the overall strip scrolls vertically with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The case studies filter bar is positioned just below the highlighted case studies strip, separated by about 40–48px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the content column but is visually compact in height, resembling a clean control rail. Filter controls (industry sub-segments, material types, machine models, etc.) are aligned left, starting at the same left edge as section headings, and arranged horizontally where possible with 16–24px gaps. On narrower screens, they wrap to multiple rows while maintaining at least 8–16px spacing between pill-like filter elements. The bar sits immediately above the case studies list and forms a clear visual break between curated highlights above and the full, filterable list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky horizontal bar: once the user scrolls past its initial position, it pins to the top of the viewport (just below the global site header) so that filters remain visible while the case studies list scrolls beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control shares the same horizontal row as the filter bar but is aligned to the right edge of the content column, creating a clear left-right tension similar to the original mockup’s nav and language selector. While filters occupy the left two-thirds of the bar, the sort dropdown or segmented control occupies roughly the right one-third, anchored flush to the right margin of the main column. It is compact in width, with 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, and is visually aligned vertically with the center of the filter controls. On small screens, the sort control may stack beneath the main filter row, but still right-aligned within the column to preserve hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shares the same sticky behavior as the filter bar, moving together as a single control strip pinned to the top of the viewport during scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The case studies list sits immediately below the filter/sort bar, with a modest 24–32px vertical gap. It extends down through the center of the page as the primary scrollable content area. Each case study item is presented as a wide card or row, aligned to the content column edges, with generous internal padding (16–24px) and 24–32px spacing between cards. On desktop, each card typically spans close to the full width of the column (taking about 80–90% of the column width with subtle side insets inside the card if needed), emphasizing an easy, vertical reading flow. A thumbnail image or small hero visual sits on the left of each card, with title and metadata stacked on the right, mirroring the left-to-right reading flow of the rest of the page. The list continues down the page with consistent spacing, forming the main content mass beneath the lighter, more curated sections above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard vertically scrolling list contained within the central column; it scrolls behind the sticky filter/sort bar and above the fixed favorites summary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls appear directly below the final case study card, separated by about 32–40px of vertical space. The pagination module is horizontally centered within the content column, occupying roughly one-third to one-half of the column width. Page numbers or controls are arranged in a horizontal row with even spacing, and primary actions (next/previous) are slightly emphasized but still minimalistic, matching the flat, typography-led style. Extra whitespace (at least 24px) above and below ensures the pagination reads as the clear end of the list and not part of the last card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline at the bottom of the case studies list; scrolls with the content and sits above the bottom-fixed favorites summary control.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites summary adopts the floating utility pattern seen in the mockup’s bottom-right button. It appears as a compact pill or small card in the bottom-right corner of the viewport, overlaying the dark background and content. It is inset approximately 24–32px from both the right edge and the bottom edge, ensuring it feels deliberate and not cramped. The component width is modest—around 220–320px—just enough to show a short label (e.g., ‘Saved case studies (3)’) and possibly a small icon. Its height remains low, roughly equivalent to a large button. On scroll, it stays fixed in this position, hovering above all other content, and on smaller screens it may expand slightly upward or shift to a bottom-centered docked bar while still preserving at least 16px padding from screen edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position floating utility; remains anchored to the bottom-right of the viewport regardless of scroll, always above the main content stack and respecting a 24–32px margin from edges.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search form is positioned near the top of the page, integrated subtly into the upper-right area of the industry overview header. It aligns horizontally to the right edge of the main content column and vertically near the top third of the hero section, mirroring the top navigation/utility alignment in the mockup. The search field itself spans roughly 25–35% of the column width, appearing as a single, clean input with an icon, with 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. There is ample whitespace around it—at least 24px above and below—so it does not compete visually with the hero title on the left. On smaller screens, it may drop below the main hero text, still right-aligned within the content column and separated by 16–24px vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section; it scrolls away with the industry overview header and does not remain fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The industry empty state appears when no valid industry data is available or when filters result in zero results. In a full-page empty scenario, it replaces the usual hero, machine list, and case study sections within the central column. The message and accompanying icon or illustration are centered horizontally and vertically within the available viewport area beneath the global header, occupying roughly the central third of the screen height. The component width is constrained to about half of the content column, ensuring a focused message with ample dark space around it. In a filter-only empty state, it appears directly below the filter/sort bar, centered within the column, pushing the case studies list and pagination out of view. In both cases, it maintains generous vertical padding (32–48px) and clear breathing room on all sides to align with the spacious, premium aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces or occupies the main content area of the central column; remains inline in the scroll flow but often appears visually centered within the viewport when no other content is present.

name: Case Study Detail
title: Case Study Detail
description: Displays full details for a selected case study, with options to favorite, share, and navigate to related industry and machine model pages.

core_features: Load and display a single case study based on the caseStudyId URL parameter using getCaseStudyDetails.
Show full case study information including title, summary, content, publication date, machine type, material, and image when provided by the API.
Provide navigation back to the related industry case studies list (industries.html) using the case study’s industryId.
Highlight key technical metadata such as machineType and material to emphasize machining context (e.g., 5-axis, titanium) based on CaseStudy fields.
List related machine models referenced in the case study using relatedMachineModelIds, with navigation to product.html for each machine model.
Allow the user to add or remove the current case study from their local favorites using addCaseStudyToFavorites and removeCaseStudyFromFavorites.
Provide a share/copy-link control that copies the CaseStudy.shareableUrl to the clipboard for external sharing.
Offer a global search bar that uses globalSearch to search across machine models, spare parts, documents, and case studies and allows navigation to the appropriate detail pages from the results.
user_workflows: View a case study from an industry list: (1) User clicks a case study link on industries.html, which navigates to case_study.html with a caseStudyId URL parameter. (2) The Case Study Detail page reads caseStudyId from the URL and calls getCaseStudyDetails. (3) The page displays the case study header, metadata, content, and related machine links. (4) User can click the industry breadcrumb or 'Back to case studies' link to return to industries.html with industryId set to the case study’s industryId.

View a case study from the homepage: (1) User clicks a highlighted case study on index.html that links to case_study.html?caseStudyId=.... (2) The page loads the case study via getCaseStudyDetails. (3) User reads the content and may navigate to product.html via related machine model links or back to industries.html for more case studies in the same industry.

Add a case study to favorites: (1) On a loaded case study, the user clicks the favorite toggle control. (2) If the case study is currently not marked as favorited in the page state, the page calls addCaseStudyToFavorites with the current caseStudyId. (3) On a successful response, the component updates its local favorite state to 'favorited' and may update a displayed favorites count using the returned totalFavorites.

Remove a case study from favorites: (1) If the case study is currently marked as favorited in the page state, the user clicks the favorite toggle again. (2) The page calls removeCaseStudyFromFavorites with the current caseStudyId. (3) On a successful response, the component updates its local favorite state to 'not favorited' and may update the displayed favorites count using the returned totalFavorites.

Share a case study link: (1) User clicks the share/copy-link button in the case study header. (2) The component reads CaseStudy.shareableUrl from the loaded case study data. (3) The component copies this URL to the clipboard using client-side clipboard functionality. (4) The component may briefly indicate success or failure of the copy operation.

Navigate to related machine models: (1) User reviews the 'Related machine models' section showing links derived from CaseStudy.relatedMachineModelIds. (2) User clicks a related machine model link. (3) The page navigates to product.html with URL parameters itemType=machine_model and id set to the clicked relatedMachineModelId.

Use global search from the case study page: (1) User enters a search term into the global search bar. (2) On submit, the page calls globalSearch with the query and relevant entityTypes (e.g., machineModels, spareParts, documents, caseStudies). (3) Search results are displayed in a results panel or overlay. (4) User clicks a result: case study results navigate to case_study.html?caseStudyId=..., machine model results navigate to product.html?itemType=machine_model&id=..., and other entity types navigate to their respective pages defined elsewhere.
interactions: On page load or when the caseStudyId URL parameter changes, the page controller calls getCaseStudyDetails(caseStudyId) and updates all bound components with the returned CaseStudy data.
Clicking the industry breadcrumb or 'Back to case studies' link reads CaseStudy.industryId and navigates the browser to industries.html?industryId=<industryId>.
Clicking a related machine model link uses the associated relatedMachineModelId to navigate to product.html?itemType=machine_model&id=<relatedMachineModelId>.
Typing in the global search bar updates an internal query state; submitting the search triggers globalSearch with the current query and configured entityTypes.

Clicking a search result item triggers navigation: caseStudies results route to case_study.html with the corresponding caseStudyId; machineModels route to product.html with itemType=machine_model and the appropriate id; other entity types route to their respective pages using their identifiers.

Clicking the favorite toggle when the internal favorite state is 'not favorited' calls addCaseStudyToFavorites with the current caseStudyId and, on success, updates the state to 'favorited' and optionally updates a favorites count display from totalFavorites.

Clicking the favorite toggle when the internal favorite state is 'favorited' calls removeCaseStudyFromFavorites with the current caseStudyId and, on success, updates the state to 'not favorited' and optionally updates a favorites count display from totalFavorites.
Clicking the share/copy-link button reads CaseStudy.shareableUrl from the loaded data and attempts to copy it to the clipboard; the component may display a transient confirmation or error state.

If getCaseStudyDetails fails or returns no data for the given caseStudyId, the page controller updates an error state that the content section can use to show an error message or fallback UI instead of normal case study content.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by the caseStudyId URL query parameter. When the page is accessed as case_study.html?caseStudyId=<id>, the page controller reads this value and invokes getCaseStudyDetails(caseStudyId). If caseStudyId changes (for example, via navigation from a search result), the page reloads the case study data accordingly. The industry navigation link derives its industryId from the loaded CaseStudy.industryId and builds a URL of the form industries.html?industryId=<industryId> without storing additional state. Related machine model navigation links derive their targets from CaseStudy.relatedMachineModelIds and build URLs of the form product.html?itemType=machine_model&id=<relatedMachineModelId>. Favorite status is not stored in the URL; it is maintained as in-memory component state, updated only through addCaseStudyToFavorites and removeCaseStudyFromFavorites responses. The share feature uses CaseStudy.shareableUrl directly from the loaded data and does not modify the URL. Any additional UI state (such as loading, error, search query text, or whether the favorites state is currently being updated) is held in component-level state and is not persisted to localStorage or other client storage, relying instead on the URL caseStudyId to restore the primary context when the page is revisited.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and high-level state for the Case Study Detail page. On initialization, reads the caseStudyId URL parameter and calls getCaseStudyDetails to retrieve the CaseStudy. Manages loading, success, and error states and provides the CaseStudy data to child components.

Provides a global search input accessible from the Case Study Detail page. Submitting a query calls globalSearch with the inputted query and selected entityTypes, then displays returned MachineModel, SparePart, DownloadableDocument, and CaseStudy results. Clicking a result navigates to the corresponding detail page.

Displays a breadcrumb or simple back link to the related industry’s case study listing. Uses the loaded CaseStudy.industryId to construct navigation to industries.html with the industryId URL parameter.

Shows key information from the loaded CaseStudy, including the title, optional summary, publication date, and high-level metadata such as machineType and material. Hosts the favorite-toggle and share-link controls so that the user can quickly favorite or share the current case study.

Renders the main body of the case study from CaseStudy.content. Supports displaying multiple sections (for example, challenge, solution, and results) based on the structured or rich content provided by the API. Reacts to loading and error states from the page controller to either show content or appropriate fallback content.

Highlights technical metadata extracted from the CaseStudy, such as machineType, material, and datePublished. May also show the count of relatedMachineModelIds if present to indicate the number of machines referenced in the case study.

Displays a list of links for machine models referenced in the case study using CaseStudy.relatedMachineModelIds. Each item links to product.html with itemType set to machine_model and id set to the relatedMachineModelId. The component relies on identifiers from the CaseStudy and does not call additional interfaces.

Provides a toggle control to add or remove the current case study from the user’s local favorites. Tracks the current favorite state in memory. When activated to favorite, calls addCaseStudyToFavorites with the current caseStudyId and updates the state using the returned CaseStudyFavorite and totalFavorites. When activated to unfavorite, calls removeCaseStudyFromFavorites and updates the state based on the success flag and returned totalFavorites.

Offers a button to copy the case study’s shareable URL to the clipboard. Reads CaseStudy.shareableUrl from the loaded data and attempts to copy it using client-side clipboard APIs. Indicates success or failure through transient UI states.

Displays high-level status messages for the Case Study Detail page, such as loading state, errors from getCaseStudyDetails, or feedback for favorite and share actions. Listens to events from the page controller and other components to update its displayed message.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered fixed-width grid (approximately 1140–1200px max-width) on a full-bleed dark background, conceptually compatible with a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow inside a centered, fixed-width content column (max 1140–1200px) on a full-bleed dark background. A global top header (logo + navigation + search) sits at the very top. Within the main page content, elements stack in this order: breadcrumb at the top-left of the content area, a slim status banner beneath it, then a prominent case study header block with title and controls. Below that, the main body splits into a two-column band: a dominant left column for the case study content (including a large hero-style image followed by text) and a narrower right column for the meta panel. After this band, the layout returns to a full-width single column for related machines. A compact page controller floats above everything at the bottom-right of the viewport.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow inside a centered, fixed-width content column (max 1140–1200px) on a full-bleed dark background. A global top header (logo + navigation + search) sits at the very top. Within the main page content, elements stack in this order: breadcrumb at the top-left of the content area, a slim status banner beneath it, then a prominent case study header block with title and controls. Below that, the main body splits into a two-column band: a dominant left column for the case study content (including a large hero-style image followed by text) and a narrower right column for the meta panel. After this band, the layout returns to a full-width single column for related machines. A compact page controller floats above everything at the bottom-right of the viewport.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header consumes about 10–15% of the viewport height. The main case study hero/image area within the content section occupies roughly 50–60% of the visible viewport height on initial load, with generous dark margins around it. All content is centered with ~10–15% of the viewport width as dark side margins, meaning the visible content column uses about 70–80% of the page width. Vertical gaps between major sections (breadcrumb/status area, header, content/meta band, related machines) are 48–64px, while inner gaps within components (between title and controls, between image and text) are 16–24px. The floating page controller is small, offset ~24–32px from the bottom and right edges.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density is intentionally low and spacious. Text blocks are relatively short and separated by clear vertical spacing. Large dark background areas frame the hero image and content columns, emphasizing the central visuals and typography. Supporting elements (breadcrumb, status, favorite/share, meta panel, related links) are compact and visually restrained so that the case study hero and title remain dominant.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow preserves an F/Z-pattern: the eye starts at the bright logo in the top-left of the global header, traces right through navigation to the global search bar, then drops diagonally into the main content area where the breadcrumb and status banner subtly introduce context. From there, attention moves to the bold case study title in the header, then down and slightly left into the large hero image within the content section, scanning across its width. The gaze naturally continues down through the body text, then across to the right-side meta panel. Finally, as users near the bottom or pause, the floating page controller at the bottom-right, along with the related machines row beneath the content, catches attention as secondary interaction points.

layout.overall_description: The page sits in a dark, full-bleed shell with a centered content column that feels like a precision-machined panel floating in space. A low, wide global header bar runs across the top with the industrial yellow logo on the far left, navigation in the center, and a compact search bar on the right, all tightly aligned on a horizontal axis. Below, the main case study view begins with a subtle breadcrumb and a slim status banner that gently introduce context without crowding the screen. The core of the page is a broad horizontal band where a large, cinematic hero image of the case study anchors the left two-thirds of the space, while a clean, card-like meta panel occupies the right third, both framed by generous dark margins.
