Above this band, the case study header spans the width of the content area, with the title and key information on the left and discreet favorite/share controls on the right, reinforcing the industrial, typography-first aesthetic. As the user scrolls down, detailed content flows linearly below the hero in the left column, while the meta panel remains visually aligned on the right. At the bottom of the main narrative, a full-width related machines section presents a row of product cards, echoing the structured, grid-based layout. Throughout, a small, rounded page controller button floats at the bottom-right of the viewport, offset comfortably from the edges; it acts as a quiet but ever-present utility touchpoint, echoing the floating control in the original mockup and completing the Z-shaped visual journey across the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller appears as a compact, rounded-rectangle or circular button cluster floating at the bottom-right corner of the viewport, visually detached from the main content column. It is offset approximately 24–32px from the right edge and 24–32px from the bottom edge, sitting above the dark background rather than inside the content grid. Its footprint is small—roughly the size of a single medium button or a tight stack of 2–3 icon buttons—so it never dominates the page but remains easy to reach. On wide screens, it does not align with any specific column; instead, it floats over the dark margin area just outside the main content block, echoing the utility button placement in the hero mockup. As users scroll, it remains in place, always visible as a secondary utility entry point (e.g., for navigating between case studies or returning to the case studies list).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position floating control anchored to the bottom-right of the viewport; it stays visible on scroll and is intentionally placed slightly outside the main content column to emphasize its utility nature without competing with primary content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search bar is integrated into the top site header, aligned to the far right of the navigation row. Within the header’s horizontal band (approximately 10–15% of viewport height), the search bar occupies about 20–25% of the header width—roughly a 240–320px field—sitting to the right of the main navigation links with a comfortable 16–24px gap. Vertically, it is centered within the header bar, matching the height and visual weight of the nav items but differentiated by a subtle input outline or understated filled style. On larger screens, the search input’s left edge lines up roughly with the right edge of the centered content column below, creating a subtle alignment that ties the header to the body grid. When the user scrolls, the search bar moves with the header (assuming the entire header is not sticky), disappearing off the top of the viewport as focus shifts to the case study content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as part of the global header; positioned in normal flow within the header’s horizontal layout and scrolls off-screen with the header. On smaller screens, it may collapse into an icon or shorter field but retains its top-right alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb sits at the very top of the main content area, directly below the global header, aligned to the left edge of the centered content column. It spans only as wide as its text requires, typically occupying about 30–50% of the content width, leaving ample dark space to its right. There is a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px between the bottom of the header and the top of the breadcrumb, giving it breathing room. The breadcrumb text is small and muted compared to the case study title, so it functions more as a quiet contextual trail than a focal element. A 8–16px gap below the breadcrumb separates it from the status indicator, keeping the hierarchy clear without feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element in the normal document flow, always positioned at the top-left of the main content column and moving upward off-screen as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The case study header is a wide horizontal block that spans the full width of the centered content column, sitting beneath the breadcrumb and status indicator. It begins about 24–32px below the status banner, forming a clear hierarchy. Inside, the header is structured as a two-part horizontal layout: on the left, occupying roughly 65–70% of the column width, is the prominent case study title, subtitle, and possibly a short one-line descriptor or key metrics; on the right, using the remaining 30–35% of the width, is a compact area reserved for the favorite and share controls, aligned to the top-right corner of the header block. The header’s vertical height is generous but not towering—sufficient to host one to three lines of title plus a subtitle, with 16–24px padding above and below the text. The left edge of the title aligns with the breadcrumb above and the content column below, while the right edge of the controls column visually lines up with the right edge of the meta panel that appears further down, creating a strong vertical axis on the right. The overall effect is a bold, typography-led slab of information that firmly anchors the start of the case study.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in the main content flow. On smaller screens, the right-side controls stack beneath the title, but on larger screens they remain right-aligned on the same horizontal row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content section forms the dominant left column of the main body and starts directly below the case study header, with a vertical gap of roughly 32–48px. It occupies about 65–70% of the content column width on desktop, leaving 30–35% for the meta panel on the right, separated by a 48–64px gutter. At the top of this section is a large, hero-style image representing the case study—this image spans the full width of the left column and roughly 50–60% of the viewport height when initially in view, but is inset horizontally by the global content margins so that there is a visible dark background framing it on the outer sides of the page. Below the hero image, there is 24–32px of vertical spacing before the main textual narrative begins, which is broken into reasonably short paragraphs or subsections to maintain the low-density, premium feel. Inner text spacing (between headings, paragraphs, and any inline visuals) follows the 16–24px rhythm. As the user scrolls, this left column provides a continuous, linear reading experience, while the right-side meta panel remains visually aligned beside it. On smaller screens, the content section expands to 100% width and the meta panel stacks beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Primary content column in normal flow. On wide screens, it forms the left column of a two-column layout; on narrow screens, it becomes full-width above the meta panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The meta panel sits to the right of the main content section in a narrower column, starting at approximately the same vertical level as the top of the hero image in the content section. It occupies about 30–35% of the content width, separated from the left column by a generous 48–64px gutter that keeps the layout airy and avoids a cramped two-column feel. The panel itself is styled as a clean, card-like block with subtle boundaries—a slightly lighter dark surface or a fine border—containing key metadata such as industry, machine model, project duration, location, and links to related machine or industry pages. It has 16–24px of internal padding on all sides, and its height may extend beyond the height of the hero image depending on content. The right edge of the panel aligns vertically with the right edge of the favorite/share area in the header, reinforcing the visual column structure. There is free dark space above and below the panel, so it feels like a distinct, precision-machined module rather than a full-height sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned in the normal flow as the right column adjacent to the content section. On larger screens, it can use a sticky behavior, pinning near the top of the viewport as the user scrolls through the left-column content, as long as its height allows; on smaller screens, it loses stickiness and stacks below the main content section at full width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related machines section sits below the main content + meta panel band, reuniting the layout into a single full-width row inside the centered content column. It begins about 48–64px beneath the lowest point of either the content section or the meta panel, whichever extends further down the page, maintaining the generous section spacing. Within this section, a heading (e.g., "Related Machines" or similar) aligns with the left edge of the main content column, followed by a horizontal row of machine cards beneath it. Each card occupies roughly one-third of the column width, with 16–24px gaps between cards, allowing for three cards per row on desktop. The cards themselves are simple and flat, potentially featuring a thumbnail image, model name, and a key spec line, echoing the industrial, minimal aesthetic. The section is visually important but not overpowering; it acts as a logical continuation for users who reach the end of the case study narrative.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard section in normal flow, spanning the full content width beneath the main two-column body. On smaller screens, the cards collapse into a single-column vertical list while retaining generous vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite toggle is integrated into the top-right area of the case study header block, sharing space with the share control. It is positioned in the header’s right column, aligned to the top-right corner with around 16–24px of padding from the header’s top and right edges. Visually, it may appear as an icon-only control (e.g., a star or heart) or a compact icon-with-label pill, but it remains small—roughly the size of a standard icon button—so it does not compete with the title. Horizontally, it is placed to the left of or above the share link, with an 8–16px gap between the two controls. This placement ensures that users see it immediately after reading the title, in line with the F-pattern flow. As the page scrolls, the favorite toggle scrolls away with the header content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored within the case study header’s right-hand control area in normal flow. It remains in place relative to the header and does not float or stick independently; on smaller screens it may stack above or beside the share control but remains in the same top-of-header region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The share control is located adjacent to the favorite toggle in the case study header’s right-hand area. It aligns to the top-right corner of the header block, typically sitting to the right of or just below the favorite toggle with an 8–16px gap between them, forming a compact two-control cluster. Depending on design, it can be a text link with a small share icon or an icon-only button; either way, its visual weight is intentionally subtle so as not to overshadow the title. Together, the favorite and share controls occupy roughly a 25–30% wide column of the header, but in practice only use a small portion of that width, leaving negative space around them that reinforces the premium, minimal feel. They live at the end of the initial F-pattern scan line across the header—after the user has seen the title—inviting secondary interaction.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored within the case study header in normal flow alongside the favorite toggle. Like the favorite control, it scrolls with the header and reflows to sit beneath the title on smaller viewports if space is constrained.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status indicator appears as a slim, full-width banner or pill-like strip directly beneath the breadcrumb and above the case study header, aligned with the centered content column. It spans approximately 80–100% of the content column width but remains shallow in height—about the height of a single line of text plus 8–12px padding top and bottom—so that it reads as a subtle status cue rather than a heavy panel. Depending on the status (e.g., Active, Completed, In Development), the banner may use a slightly brighter accent color (such as a muted variant of the brand’s saturated yellow) against the dark background, but with restrained contrast and typography to avoid overpowering the header. A vertical gap of around 8–16px separates it from the breadcrumb above and about 24–32px from the case study header below, clearly delineating its role as a contextual label for the case study.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in the normal vertical stack between breadcrumb and case study header. It scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page and does not have any sticky or floating behavior.

name: Maintenance Plans
title: Maintenance Plans
description: Browse, filter, sort, and compare CNC maintenance plans, then open individual plan detail pages to review full terms and subscribe.

core_features: Load and display a paginated list of active maintenance plans using the listMaintenancePlans interface with URL-driven filters and sorting.
Filter maintenance plans by inclusion of specific features such as annual on-site inspection and by maximum monthly price.
Sort maintenance plans by criteria such as price or coverage level via the sortBy URL parameter.
Allow users to select multiple plans from the list for side-by-side comparison of key attributes including price, response time, and coverage level.
Provide pagination controls to navigate between pages of maintenance plans using the page URL parameter.
Enable navigation from a plan in the list to its detail page (maintenance_plan_detail.html) by passing the selected planId.
Expose a global search form on the page that uses the globalSearch interface to search across machines, spare parts, documents, and case studies (e.g., for quick access to related support resources).
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Arrive with pre-applied filters from index.html:
1. User clicks a link or CTA on index.html that points to maintenance_plans.html with URL parameters such as includesAnnualOnSiteInspection and/or monthlyPriceMax.
2. When maintenance_plans.html loads, the page controller reads includesAnnualOnSiteInspection, monthlyPriceMax, sortBy, and page from the URL query string.
3. The page controller builds a filters object from these parameters and calls listMaintenancePlans with filters, sortBy, page, and a configured pageSize.
4. The maintenance-plan-list component renders the returned MaintenancePlan items.
5. The filter and sort components initialize their UI state based on the current URL parameters so the user sees which filters and sort order are active.

Workflow 2 – Adjust filters and refresh the listing:
1. User toggles the annual on-site inspection filter in the filter bar.
2. User optionally enters or updates a maximum monthly price value.
3. User submits the filter form or the filter changes trigger an update.
4. The filter component updates the URL query string to reflect the new includesAnnualOnSiteInspection and monthlyPriceMax values and resets page to the first page.
5. The browser navigates or the SPA router updates the route, causing the page controller to re-read URL parameters.
6. The page controller calls listMaintenancePlans with the updated filters, and the list component re-renders with the filtered plans.

Workflow 3 – Change sort order:
1. User selects a sort option (for example, sort by price or coverage level) from the sort controls.
2. The sort component updates the sortBy parameter in the URL and resets page to the first page.
3. The page controller observes the new URL, calls listMaintenancePlans with the same filters but updated sortBy and page, and receives the appropriately ordered MaintenancePlan list.
4. The list and pagination components update to reflect the new order and page state.

Workflow 4 – Use pagination to browse more plans:
1. User reviews the current list of maintenance plans and clicks a different page number or a next/previous control in the pagination component.
2. The pagination component updates the page parameter in the URL while preserving current filter and sortBy values.
3. The page controller re-reads URL parameters and calls listMaintenancePlans with the same filters and sortBy but the new page value.
4. The list component renders the MaintenancePlan items for the selected page, and pagination controls highlight the active page.

Workflow 5 – Compare multiple plans:
1. From the plan list, user selects a comparison checkbox or toggle on one or more MaintenancePlan cards.
2. The list component updates its internal comparison selection state and passes the selected plan objects to the comparison panel component.
3. The comparison panel displays key attributes (such as name, monthlyPrice, includesAnnualOnSiteInspection, responseTimeHours, coverageLevel, and salient features) for each selected plan in a structured layout.
4. User can remove plans from the comparison within the panel; the selection in the list component is updated accordingly.
5. Comparison selection is maintained while the user remains on the same page and list results; if filters, sorting, or page change, the component reconciles any selected plan IDs that are still present in the new result set.

Workflow 6 – Open a plan detail page to subscribe:
1. User identifies a plan of interest in the list and clicks the plan card or a dedicated action (e.g., “View details”).
2. The list component triggers navigation to maintenance_plan_detail.html with a planId query parameter corresponding to the selected MaintenancePlan id.
3. The detail page (outside the scope of this document) uses planId to load full plan information and offer a subscription or request form.

Workflow 7 – Use global search from the maintenance plans page:
1. User types a query into the global search input available in the site header.
2. As the user submits the form (or optionally on type), the global-search component calls the globalSearch interface with the query and a configured set of entityTypes.
3. Based on the implementation, the page either shows inline suggestions or navigates to a dedicated search results page, passing the query as a URL parameter.
4. The user can click on any search result to navigate to machine, spare part, document, or case study pages as appropriate.
interactions:
Interaction – Initialize page from URL:
- Trigger: Page load or route change to maintenance_plans.html.
- Behavior: Read includesAnnualOnSiteInspection (string), monthlyPriceMax (number), sortBy (string), and page (number) from the URL. Normalize these into a filters object and pagination/sort parameters. Call listMaintenancePlans({ filters, sortBy, page, pageSize }). Bind the returned items, page, pageSize, and totalItems to the list and pagination components. Sync filter and sort UI controls with the URL values.

Interaction – Filter by annual on-site inspection:
- Trigger: User toggles the annual inspection filter control in the filter bar.
- Behavior: Transform the control state into a URL parameter includesAnnualOnSiteInspection (e.g., set to 'true' or remove the parameter if not filtering). Reset page to the first page. Update the URL query string. The updated route causes the page controller to call listMaintenancePlans with the new filter set.

Interaction – Filter by maximum monthly price:
- Trigger: User changes the maximum monthly price input and confirms (e.g., blur or submit).
- Behavior: Validate the input as a number. If valid, update monthlyPriceMax in the URL query string; if cleared, remove the parameter. Reset page to the first page. The route update causes page re-evaluation and a new listMaintenancePlans call with the updated filters.

Interaction – Change sort order:
- Trigger: User selects a new sort option in the sort controls (e.g., price ascending or coverage level).
- Behavior: Update the sortBy parameter in the URL and reset page to the first page. The route change triggers listMaintenancePlans with unchanged filters but new sortBy and page values, and the list re-renders accordingly.

Interaction – Pagination control:
- Trigger: User clicks a page number, next, or previous control in the pagination component.
- Behavior: Compute the new page value and update the page parameter in the URL while leaving includesAnnualOnSiteInspection, monthlyPriceMax, and sortBy unchanged. The route change triggers a new listMaintenancePlans call and updates the list and pagination state.

Interaction – Select plans for comparison:
- Trigger: User toggles a comparison checkbox on a plan card in the list.
- Behavior: Add or remove the corresponding MaintenancePlan from the comparison selection maintained in page state. Notify the comparison panel of the updated selection. The comparison panel re-renders the selected plans and their key fields. This selection is not encoded in the URL by default and is reset if the page is reloaded.

Interaction – Navigate to plan detail:
- Trigger: User clicks on a plan card or an explicit "view details" action.
- Behavior: Extract the MaintenancePlan.id from the clicked card and navigate to maintenance_plan_detail.html with a query parameter planId equal to this id. No additional API calls are made on maintenance_plans.html after navigation.

Interaction – Global search submit:
- Trigger: User submits a query in the global search input.
- Behavior: Call globalSearch with the entered query and a configured entityTypes array. Use the returned machineModels, spareParts, documents, and caseStudies either to show inline results (e.g., suggestions dropdown) or to redirect to a search results page (depending on site-wide design), passing the query via URL parameters in that target page.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state of maintenance_plans.html is encoded in the URL query parameters, ensuring that listings are fully reproducible, shareable, and bookmarkable.

- includesAnnualOnSiteInspection (string):
  - Accepted values are determined by backend expectations (for example, 'true').
  - When present, the page controller includes a corresponding filter flag in the filters object passed to listMaintenancePlans.
  - When the user disables this filter, the parameter is removed from the URL, and the filters object no longer constrains by this feature.

- monthlyPriceMax (number):
  - When present and valid, it is parsed as a number and added to the filters object passed to listMaintenancePlans (e.g., filters.monthlyPriceMax).
  - If the user clears the maximum price, the parameter is removed from the URL and excluded from the filters object.

- sortBy (string):
  - Represents the selected sorting strategy (for example, price-based or coverage-based codes defined by the backend).
  - When present, it is passed directly to listMaintenancePlans as the sortBy argument.
  - When absent, the backend or application default sorting is used; the UI initializes the sort controls to reflect this default.

- page (number):
  - Represents the current pagination page. When present and valid, it is passed to listMaintenancePlans as page.
  - If page is missing or invalid, the application treats the listing as starting from its default first page; the URL can be normalized if needed.
  - Whenever filters or sortBy are changed by the user, the page parameter is reset to the first page and the URL is updated accordingly.

The listMaintenancePlans call always uses the current combination of filters, sortBy, and page derived exclusively from these URL parameters (plus a configured pageSize that is not user-controlled). The returned items, page, pageSize, and totalItems are then bound to the list and pagination components.

Short-lived UI-only state, such as which plans are selected for comparison, is stored in in-memory component state and is not mirrored in URL parameters. This state is intentionally ephemeral and resets on full page reload or when the user navigates away.

No localStorage or other persistent client-side storage is used for core page state; all restorable configuration is driven by the URL parameters and the data returned via listMaintenancePlans and globalSearch.

components.functionality: Orchestrates the maintenance plans page: reads URL parameters, constructs filters and sorting, calls listMaintenancePlans, and distributes the resulting data to child components (filter bar, sort controls, list, pagination, and comparison panel). Ensures that child components are initialized according to the current URL state and updates are triggered when the URL changes.

Provides controls for filtering maintenance plans by feature inclusion (such as annual on-site inspection) and by maximum monthly price. Reflects current filter state from URL parameters and updates the URL (and thus the listing) when the user changes filters.

Allows the user to select a sort order for maintenance plans (e.g., by price or coverage level). Reads the current sortBy value from the URL, updates it when the user selects a new option, and resets pagination to the first page.

Displays the current page of MaintenancePlan items returned by listMaintenancePlans as a list of selectable cards or rows. Each plan shows key attributes (name, monthly price, annual inspection inclusion, response time, coverage level, and selected features). Supports selecting plans for comparison and navigating to plan detail pages.

Displays pagination controls based on page, pageSize, and totalItems from listMaintenancePlans. Lets the user move between pages of maintenance plans while preserving the current filters and sort order by updating the page URL parameter.

Shows a side-by-side comparison of selected maintenance plans from the current result set. Displays key fields such as pricing, response time, coverage level, inclusion of annual on-site inspection, and other feature indicators. Updates whenever the user modifies the selection in the list component.

Implements the global search bar available on this page. Accepts a text query and utilizes the globalSearch interface to search across machines, spare parts, documents, and case studies. Can display quick suggestions or trigger navigation to a dedicated search results page based on the returned data.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered fixed-width grid (approximately 1140–1200px max-width) on a full-bleed dark background, conceptually compatible with a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page continues the linear, top-to-bottom stacking pattern from the mockup: fixed dark header at the top (global, not part of this spec), then the main content column centered on a full-bleed charcoal background. Within the main content for this page, the sequence is: (1) Maintenance Plans page controller (page title/context + high-level mode controls), (2) global search, (3) horizontal filter bar, (4) sort controls aligned to the right of or just below the filter bar, (5) the maintenance plan list as the main visual area, (6) pagination centered below the list, and (7) a comparison panel anchored to the bottom edge of the viewport as a floating, utility-like element, echoing the mockup’s bottom-right floating button behavior.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page continues the linear, top-to-bottom stacking pattern from the mockup: fixed dark header at the top (global, not part of this spec), then the main content column centered on a full-bleed charcoal background. Within the main content for this page, the sequence is: (1) Maintenance Plans page controller (page title/context + high-level mode controls), (2) global search, (3) horizontal filter bar, (4) sort controls aligned to the right of or just below the filter bar, (5) the maintenance plan list as the main visual area, (6) pagination centered below the list, and (7) a comparison panel anchored to the bottom edge of the viewport as a floating, utility-like element, echoing the mockup’s bottom-right floating button behavior.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The content sits in a centered column of ~1140–1200px within a full-bleed dark shell. Each major band (page controller + heading area, controls area, list area, pagination area) is separated by 32–48px of vertical space, consistent with the mockup’s spacious spacing. The primary functional area—the maintenance plan list—occupies most of the visible viewport height once the user scrolls past the initial header area. Lateral margins keep all content within about 70–80% of the screen width on large displays, preserving 10–15% dark margin on both left and right. The comparison panel is compact in height (roughly 56–72px when collapsed and 160–220px when expanded) and spans the content width, resting slightly inset (24–32px) from the viewport bottom and sides to feel deliberate and floating.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density remains low and premium: each control row (search, filters, sort) has ample breathing room (24–32px padding above and below) and avoids crowding. Plan cards in the results list are separated by 24–32px vertical and horizontal gaps, with generous internal padding (16–24px) and clean, typography-led layouts. There are no tight clusters; every interactive element (search, chips, dropdowns, pagination controls) has comfortable spacing, maintaining a minimal, industrial feel where negative space is an active part of the design.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mirrors the F/Z pattern from the mockup. The eye starts at the top-left with the page controller heading, moves horizontally across any high-level toggles or breadcrumb-like controls, then drops diagonally to the center where the large, centered search field and filters sit. From there, the gaze flows downward and across the maintenance plan cards, scanning each row (or card column) left to right. The pagination at the bottom center completes the primary flow. The comparison panel acts as the secondary attractor at the bottom-right/bottom edge, revealed or emphasized once the user selects plans—echoing the mockup’s floating bottom-right button as a utility endpoint in the viewing journey.

layout.overall_description: Within a full-bleed charcoal background, a centered, fixed-width content column floats in the middle of the viewport, echoing the hero-centric, premium feel of the mockup but adapted to a functional listing page. At the top of this column, the Maintenance Plans page controller anchors the experience with a bold, left-aligned page title and subtle high-level controls, setting an industrial, typographic tone. Just below, a wide, center-weighted global search bar acts as a visual focal point, framed by generous dark space so it feels like the hero control of the page. Beneath it, a clean, horizontal filter bar stretches across the content width, with sort controls aligned to the right, creating a structured control band. When scrolling, these controls form a consistent cap above the main content. The maintenance plan list occupies the heart of the page — a calm grid or stacked set of plan cards, each surrounded by breathing room, contrasted against the dark shell. Pagination sits centered under the list, giving a clear sense of progression without visual clutter. At the bottom, a dark, slightly elevated comparison panel docks across the lower portion of the viewport, inset from the edges; when active, it becomes a compact control hub for comparing selected plans, visually reminiscent of the mockup’s floating bottom-right action but scaled to the complexity of plan comparison. The result is a modern, industrial, quietly cinematic layout where the user’s attention flows naturally from high-level discovery controls at the top to detailed plan exploration in the center, and finally to decision-making tools at the bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller sits at the very top of the main content area, directly below the global site header (logo/navigation) and any persistent hero band used by the site. It spans the full width of the centered content column (roughly 1140–1200px) and is horizontally aligned with it, leaving 10–15% dark margin on either side of the screen at large viewport widths. The primary element within this controller is a left-aligned page title (e.g., “Maintenance Plans”) in a strong, industrial, yellow-accented type treatment that visually echoes the logo’s weight. To the right of the title, on the same horizontal line, space is reserved for optional high-level controls such as plan category tabs, a time horizon toggle (e.g., monthly/annual), or breadcrumbs — these form a horizontal strip extending towards the right edge of the content column, but with 24–32px of right padding before the column’s boundary. Vertical height of this band is relatively modest (approximately 10–15% of the viewport height on first load), with 24–32px padding above and below the title line, giving it presence without overpowering the functional controls below. There is 32–48px of vertical space between the bottom of this controller and the global search component beneath it, preserving the spacious, low-density feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow; scrolls away with the rest of the content. It visually anchors the top of the page but does not stick or float as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search component is positioned immediately below the page controller, acting as the primary ‘hero’ control for this page. It is horizontally centered within the content column and occupies roughly 60–70% of the column width on large screens, leaving equal dark space to its left and right within the column for balance. Vertically, it is given generous breathing room: about 24–32px of space above (from the page controller) and 24–32px below (before the filter bar). The search field itself should be visually substantial — a single, long bar with 16–20px vertical padding and 20–24px horizontal padding inside, possibly with a subtle border or glow on the dark background to enhance legibility. A search icon sits inside on the left, with 8px spacing from the text, keeping with the 8px base inner spacing. On narrower screens, the search maintains full width of the content column (up to its max) and remains the dominant element in this band. This makes the search bar the first interactive element the eye lands on after reading the page title, aligning with the mockup’s pattern where a dominant central visual anchors the user’s focus.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal scroll; it does not stick, but its generous proportions and central placement make it feel like a stable anchor even as content below moves.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar sits directly below the global search, forming a horizontal control strip that spans nearly the full width of the content column. It begins approximately 24–32px beneath the bottom edge of the search component, maintaining the clear vertical rhythm established by the mockup. The filter controls (e.g., dropdowns for machine type, response time, on-site coverage, price range chips) are left-aligned, starting at the left margin of the content column. Each filter element uses 12–16px vertical padding and 16–20px horizontal padding, with at least 8–12px of space between individual filter pills or dropdowns to avoid visual crowding. The bar itself appears as a row of discrete controls rather than a full-height toolbar, floating slightly on the dark background with sufficient padding (16–24px) above and below its contents. There is intentionally more open space on the right side of this bar, allowing room for the sort controls to align along the right edge in the same band on larger screens. On narrow screens, filters may wrap into two lines but should remain visually grouped as a single band with consistent spacing. Overall, this bar reads as an organized, minimally styled control line that frames the beginning of the results area below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Preferrably sticky on larger screens: it can become a subtle sticky band just beneath the site header once the user scrolls past it, ensuring filters remain accessible while the user browses the plan list. If sticky, it should maintain its dark background and cast a very subtle shadow to sit above the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort controls are visually and spatially paired with the filter bar, occupying the right side of the same horizontal band on desktop. On large screens, they are right-aligned to the content column, starting roughly from the midline of the bar and extending to within 24–32px of the rightmost column edge. This creates a left-to-right visual tension: filters on the left, sort on the right, matching the navigation logic seen in the mockup’s header (logo left, language/right utility items right). The primary sort selector (e.g., a dropdown for ‘Sort by: Price, Response Time, Coverage Level’) sits at the far right, with any secondary sort toggles (ascending/descending) immediately to its left with 8–12px spacing. The vertical alignment matches the filter controls, so they share a common baseline, forming a clean, continuous horizontal band. On smaller screens, the sort controls stack underneath the filter controls within the same visual section, left-aligned but with a 16–24px vertical gap separating them from the last filter row. Overall height of the combined filter/sort region is about 64–96px, depending on wrapping, maintaining the low-density aesthetic. Below this combined band, there is 32–40px of space before the first row of plan cards begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shares the same spatial behavior as the filter bar. If the filter bar becomes sticky, the sort controls remain attached to it as part of the same sticky band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The maintenance plan list occupies the central and largest portion of the page, located immediately below the filter/sort band with a 32–40px vertical gap. It sits within the same centered content column, stretching nearly the full width of the column but leaving 24–32px of internal horizontal margin to prevent cards from touching the edges. On large screens, the list is arranged as a two-column grid of plan cards, each card spanning roughly 5–6 of the 12 grid columns with a 24–32px gutter between the two columns. This allows the content area to feel richly populated but never cramped, with each card surrounded by dark negative space. Cards are aligned in rows, with 24–32px vertical spacing between rows. On mid and small screens, the layout gracefully collapses to a single column, each card taking the full width of the content column. Each card has a flat, minimal aesthetic: dark surface, 16–24px padding inside, clear typographic hierarchy for plan name, coverage tier, key metrics, and a primary action (e.g., ‘View Details’) aligned consistently (right or bottom-right within the card). Selection states for comparison (e.g., checkboxes or ‘Add to compare’ toggles) are typically placed in the top-right of each card with 8–12px separation from the edges, maintaining the F-pattern inside the card. This list area will generally fill most of the viewport height when scrolled, becoming the primary locus of user attention. Below the last row of cards, 32–48px of vertical spacing separates the list from the pagination controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrollable within the page flow. It expands vertically as more plans are shown; on longer lists, the filter/sort bar (if sticky) remains visible while the user scrolls through this section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls sit directly below the maintenance plan list, centered within the content column. They are separated from the last row of plan cards by a generous 32–48px vertical gap to clearly mark the end of the current result set. The controls themselves form a compact horizontal cluster—previous/next arrows, page numbers, and possibly a page size selector—laid out in a single row. This row is visually light and minimal: small but legible labels, 8–12px spacing between page numbers, and 16–20px horizontal padding around the entire control group so it doesn’t feel cramped on the dark background. The full pagination cluster occupies roughly 30–40% of the content column width, centered so there is equal negative space on both sides. On smaller screens, the layout may simplify to ‘Previous / 1 of N / Next’ while preserving central alignment. Beneath the pagination, there is an additional 48–64px of vertical breathing room before reaching the bottom of the main content area, ensuring the pagination doesn’t feel glued to the bottom edge or the comparison panel when it appears.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page, appearing at the end of the list. It scrolls normally and does not stick, but its centered placement and spacing make it easy to find as users reach the bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison panel is positioned as a floating utility layer anchored along the bottom edge of the viewport, visually analogous to the mockup’s bottom-right floating button but expanded to span the width of the content column. When active, it appears as a dark, slightly elevated bar that runs horizontally across the bottom of the content area, aligned with the same centered width as the main column. On large screens, the panel is inset by approximately 24–32px from the bottom edge of the viewport, and by 24–32px from the left and right screen edges, creating a floating ‘tray’ effect against the charcoal background. Its height in expanded state is around 160–220px, divided into an upper strip with concise labels (e.g., “Comparing 3 plans”) and key actions (Compare now, Clear) and a lower zone where selected plan summaries are displayed in small, horizontally scrollable tiles. Each tile follows the industrial aesthetic: compact card with minimal details and a remove icon in the corner, spaced 8–12px apart. In its collapsed state (when there are no selected plans or the user minimizes it), the panel reduces to a slimmer bar (about 56–72px high) anchored primarily to the bottom-right, occupying roughly 30–40% of the content width, with a strong call-to-action or label like “Compare selected plans” and an icon—mirroring the visual prominence and placement of the original floating button. This collapsed bar sits roughly 24–32px above the viewport bottom and 24–32px from the right edge, overlapping the dark shell but not covering essential content. The main content scrolls underneath/behind this panel, so even when fully expanded, the user can still scroll the page; the panel remains fixed in place, always accessible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport near the bottom (within the central content width), always present when there are selected plans. It can toggle between collapsed and expanded states. It overlays the main content rather than pushing it, maintaining a utility feel similar to the floating bottom-right element in the mockup.

name: Maintenance Plan Detail
title: Maintenance Plan Detail
description: Displays details for a selected maintenance plan and provides a form to submit a maintenance subscription request.

core_features: Load and display full details of a maintenance plan based on the planId URL parameter using getMaintenancePlanDetails.
Show key plan information such as name, description, features, coverage level, response time, and any information about visit frequency or exclusions present in the plan data.
Present pricing information using monthlyPrice and currency, and surface contract-term-related information contained in description or features.
Provide a subscription request form with fields for name, email, company, phone, and preferred start date, tied to the currently viewed plan.
Submit subscription requests via submitMaintenanceSubscriptionRequest and display success/error states and any returned confirmation message.
Expose the global search bar that uses globalSearch to search machines, spare parts, documents, and case studies without leaving the page.
user_workflows:
View maintenance plan details:
1. User navigates to maintenance_plan_detail.html?planId={PLAN_ID} from the maintenance_plans.html listing.
2. Page controller reads the planId URL parameter.
3. Page calls getMaintenancePlanDetails(planId).
4. While loading, a loading state is shown in the plan detail area.
5. On success, plan details are passed to components showing summary, features, pricing, and other attributes.
6. If the plan is inactive or the API returns an error, an error state is shown instead of the detail content.

Submit a maintenance subscription request:
1. User reviews the plan details on maintenance_plan_detail.html.
2. User scrolls to the subscription request form or clicks a call-to-action button near the plan details that focuses the form.
3. The form is pre-bound to the current planId and shows the plan name read-only (from getMaintenancePlanDetails).
4. User fills out required fields: name and email; optionally fills company, phone, and preferredStartDate.
5. User submits the form.
6. Client-side validation runs (e.g., required fields present, email format, preferredStartDate format); if invalid, inline validation errors are shown and the request is not sent.
7. On valid input, the page calls submitMaintenanceSubscriptionRequest with parameters { planId, name, email, company, phone, preferredStartDate }.
8. While the request is in progress, the submit button is disabled and a loading state is displayed on the form.
9. On success, the API response (subscriptionRequest, success, message) is used to show a confirmation panel; the form may be hidden or disabled, and key details (such as subscriptionRequest.status and preferredStartDate) can be displayed.
10. On failure (success=false or network/API error), an error message is shown, and the form remains available for correction and resubmission.

Use global search from the plan detail page:
1. User types a query into the global search bar.
2. On search submission, globalSearch is called with parameters { query, entityTypes }.
3. While loading, a search loading indicator is shown (e.g., in a dropdown or overlay).
4. On success, machineModels, spareParts, documents, and caseStudies are shown as grouped results.
5. User can click a result to navigate to its respective detail page (navigation URLs handled outside this page’s interfaces). The maintenance plan detail page remains functional if the user stays on it.
interactions: On page load: Parse planId from the URL query parameters and trigger getMaintenancePlanDetails(planId).
On plan details load success: Populate the summary, features, coverage, response time, and pricing components. Enable the subscription form and display the plan name within the form context.
On plan details load error or missing planId: Show an error message component indicating that the plan could not be loaded; disable or hide the subscription form to prevent invalid submissions.
On click of a call-to-action button like “Request this plan” within the details area: Scroll/focus the subscription form component to guide the user to the form.
On change of subscription form fields: Update the internal form state; clear any field-specific validation messages for the updated field.
On subscription form submit: Validate required fields; if valid, call submitMaintenanceSubscriptionRequest with the current planId and form values; show submitting state until the response returns.

On successful subscription submission: Display a confirmation component using the returned subscriptionRequest and message; optionally disable the form or show it in read-only state; keep the user on the same URL (planId unchanged).
On failed subscription submission: Display an error message with the returned message (if present); keep existing form values so the user can correct and retry.
On typing into global search input: Update the local query state; optionally trigger debounced search or wait for explicit submit.
On global search submit: Call globalSearch({ query, entityTypes }); show loading and then render grouped search results; allow user to navigate to selected results via link clicks.
page_functionality.state_logic: The main URL parameter controlling the page is planId. On every page load or URL change where planId is present, the page controller calls getMaintenancePlanDetails(planId) to fetch the corresponding MaintenancePlan. If planId is absent or empty, the page enters an error state and no subscription submission is allowed, because submitMaintenanceSubscriptionRequest requires a valid planId. The page has internal (non-URL) UI states including: (1) planLoading (idle/loading/success/error) based on the getMaintenancePlanDetails call; (2) formState representing current field values and validation errors; (3) submissionState (idle/submitting/success/error) based on submitMaintenanceSubscriptionRequest; and (4) searchState (query, loading, results, error) based on globalSearch. After a successful subscription request, the page does not navigate away and the URL remains maintenance_plan_detail.html?planId={PLAN_ID}, fulfilling the outgoing navigation requirement that the user remains on the same page; the confirmation is shown by toggling internal submissionState and rendering the confirmation component.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading based on the planId URL parameter, manages high-level loading/error states, and passes the loaded MaintenancePlan to child components. Prevents subscription submissions when no valid plan is loaded.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Extract planId from URL query parameters; if present, invoke getMaintenancePlanDetails(planId); if absent, set planLoadState='error'.
onPlanLoadSuccess: Receive MaintenancePlan from getMaintenancePlanDetails; set planLoadState='success' and store plan data; notify child components (summary, features, pricing, form) to render.
onPlanLoadError: Set planLoadState='error'; store error message; instruct child components to show error and disable the subscription form.

onSubscriptionSubmit: Receive form payload from subscription form; ensure a valid planId is associated; call submitMaintenanceSubscriptionRequest with { planId, name, email, company, phone, preferredStartDate }; set submissionState='submitting'.

onSubscriptionSubmitSuccess: Receive { subscriptionRequest, success, message }; if success is true, set submissionState='success', store subscriptionRequest and message; notify confirmation component to display; optionally notify form to switch to read-only or hidden state.
onSubscriptionSubmitError: Set submissionState='error'; store error message (from response or network error); notify form to display error feedback.

onGlobalSearchSubmit: Receive search query and entityTypes from global search component; call globalSearch({ query, entityTypes }); manage searchLoading/searchResults/searchError states; pass results to the search results component.

components.functionality: Displays a compact overview of the current maintenance plan, including plan name, high-level description, coverage level, and response time, using data from the loaded MaintenancePlan.
event_handlers: onPlanDataChange: When the page controller updates the current MaintenancePlan, re-render the summary fields (name, description, coverageLevel, responseTimeHours) and ensure they stay synchronized.

onRequestPlanClick: Triggered when a user clicks a contextual action (e.g., 'Request this plan') in this panel; sends an event to the page controller or directly to the subscription-form component to scroll/focus the subscription form.

components.functionality: Lists the features of the maintenance plan and any exclusions or notes that can be derived from the plan’s description and features array. This includes items such as included services, visit frequency, or exclusions where such information is embedded in the plan data.
event_handlers: onPlanDataChange: When MaintenancePlan data is updated, parse and display the features array entries and any additional descriptive text; re-group or re-label them according to the implementation’s rules (e.g., grouping features versus exclusions) without hardcoding specific item values.
onToggleSection: When the user expands or collapses sections (e.g., 'Included Services' or 'Exclusions'), update internal UI state to remember which sections are open.

components.functionality: Shows the pricing structure for the maintenance plan including monthlyPrice and currency, and surfaces information about contract term lengths or renewal conditions as expressed in description or features.
event_handlers: onPlanDataChange: When MaintenancePlan is loaded or updated, render pricing based on monthlyPrice and currency; extract and display any term-related information present in description or features (such as minimum duration or renewal notes) according to configurable rules.

onViewDetailsClick: If the panel has a 'view more details' interaction, toggle extended text that further explains pricing structure and terms by expanding or collapsing additional content derived from the MaintenancePlan.

components.functionality: Collects user information required to submit a maintenance subscription request for the currently viewed plan and initiates submitMaintenanceSubscriptionRequest when submitted.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize form fields (name, email, company, phone, preferredStartDate) with empty or previously-typed values; bind read-only display of the current plan name and planId from MaintenancePlan.
onPlanDataChange: When the current MaintenancePlan changes, update the read-only fields that show which plan the user is subscribing to; ensure internal planId reference matches the loaded plan.
onFieldChange: Update internal form state when a user edits name, email, company, phone, or preferredStartDate; clear any validation error for that field.

onValidate: Run client-side validation prior to submission, ensuring that required fields (at least name and email) are filled and that email and preferredStartDate (if provided) have acceptable formats; return validation results to the UI for inline error display.

onSubmit: When the user submits the form and validation passes, emit an event to the page controller containing { planId, name, email, company, phone, preferredStartDate }; disable the submit button and show a submitting indicator until a response is received.

onSubmissionStateChange: Respond to updates from the page controller: if submissionState='submitting', keep the form disabled; if submissionState='success', show a local success message or hide the form fields; if submissionState='error', re-enable the form and display the error message.
onReset: Allow user to reset the form to initial state (clearing user-entered values) if desired, without affecting the loaded MaintenancePlan.

components.functionality: Displays confirmation or error feedback after a subscription request attempt, using the MaintenanceSubscriptionRequest and message returned from submitMaintenanceSubscriptionRequest.
event_handlers: onSubmissionSuccess: When the page controller sets submissionState='success', render confirmation content using subscriptionRequest (e.g., showing its status and preferredStartDate) and the returned message string; optionally provide guidance on next steps.

onSubmissionError: When submissionState='error', display an error notice using the error message provided by the page controller; allow the user to close or dismiss the message so they can adjust the form.
onDismiss: Allow user to dismiss the confirmation or error panel, updating internal visibility state while preserving the underlying subscriptionRequest data.

components.functionality: Informs the user when the maintenance plan cannot be loaded because planId is missing, invalid, or getMaintenancePlanDetails returns an error, and prevents submission for an unknown plan.
event_handlers: onPlanLoadError: When the page controller reports planLoadState='error', render a message that the plan details are unavailable; ensure the subscription form is either hidden or disabled.
onRetryClick: If offered, trigger a retry by asking the page controller to call getMaintenancePlanDetails again with the current planId (useful when errors are transient).

components.functionality: Provides an input for the user to search across multiple entity types (machineModels, spareParts, documents, caseStudies) from the maintenance plan detail page.
event_handlers: onQueryChange: Update local search query state as the user types; optionally debounce input for live suggestions according to implementation choice.
onEntityTypesChange: Allow changing which entity types to search (if exposed in the UI) and update local entityTypes state.
onSubmit: When user triggers a search, emit an event to the page controller with the current query and entityTypes; the controller then calls globalSearch({ query, entityTypes }).
onClear: Clear the current query and notify the page controller to reset search results.

components.functionality: Displays grouped search results from globalSearch without navigating away from the maintenance plan detail page.
event_handlers: onSearchResultsChange: When the page controller provides updated search results from globalSearch, render the grouped lists for machineModels, spareParts, documents, and caseStudies; handle empty-result states.
onSearchLoadingChange: When search loading state changes, show or hide a loading indicator within this overlay or panel.
onSearchError: When the page controller indicates an error occurred during globalSearch, display an appropriate error message and optionally a retry action.

onResultClick: Handle user clicks on individual results by initiating navigation to the corresponding detail pages (e.g., machine detail, spare part detail, document download, case study detail) via appropriate URLs that may include their own parameters; the navigation itself is handled at the application level.
onCloseOverlay: Let the user close/dismiss the search results overlay, clearing local visibility state while preserving the last results if desired.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered fixed-width grid (approximately 1140–1200px max-width) on a full-bleed dark background, conceptually compatible with a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom structure anchored in a full-width dark shell with a centered content column. At the very top sits a fixed-height dark header with logo on the far left, navigation items running horizontally, and a language selector aligned to the right, mirroring the mockup. Below the header, the maintenance-plan-summary-panel takes the role of the hero section, occupying the top of the main content area as a wide, cinematic card. Directly beneath this hero summary, a multi-column band presents the maintenance-plan-features-and-exclusions on the left and the maintenance-plan-pricing-and-terms on the right, forming a balanced, data-rich stripe. The maintenance-subscription-form sits as its own full-width (within the centered column) section below the details, clearly framed as the primary action area. The maintenance-subscription-confirmation-panel and plan-load-error-panel replace or overlay the relevant sections (hero summary or form) when active, rather than adding more clutter. The global-search-bar exists as a compact floating utility in the bottom-right corner of the viewport, and when triggered, the global-search-results-overlay covers the content, presenting search-focused UI in a separate visual layer.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom structure anchored in a full-width dark shell with a centered content column. At the very top sits a fixed-height dark header with logo on the far left, navigation items running horizontally, and a language selector aligned to the right, mirroring the mockup. Below the header, the maintenance-plan-summary-panel takes the role of the hero section, occupying the top of the main content area as a wide, cinematic card. Directly beneath this hero summary, a multi-column band presents the maintenance-plan-features-and-exclusions on the left and the maintenance-plan-pricing-and-terms on the right, forming a balanced, data-rich stripe. The maintenance-subscription-form sits as its own full-width (within the centered column) section below the details, clearly framed as the primary action area. The maintenance-subscription-confirmation-panel and plan-load-error-panel replace or overlay the relevant sections (hero summary or form) when active, rather than adding more clutter. The global-search-bar exists as a compact floating utility in the bottom-right corner of the viewport, and when triggered, the global-search-results-overlay covers the content, presenting search-focused UI in a separate visual layer.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies roughly 10–15% of the viewport height, with enough vertical padding to feel substantial but not bulky. The summary hero panel below consumes the majority of the first-screen height on desktop—around 50–70% of the viewport height—while using about 70–80% of the centered content width, leaving 10–15% dark lateral margins on each side. The features/pricing band spans the full width of the content column but is divided into a 60/40 split between left and right panels, both inset from the page edges to maintain the cinematic frame. The subscription form panel also uses roughly 70–80% of the content width and is centered, with generous vertical spacing (48–64px) separating it from the band above and the footer below. Error and confirmation panels follow the same width and margin logic as the sections they replace, preserving the spacious layout. The global-search-bar floating control is small and unobtrusive, positioned about 24–32px from the bottom and right edges, and the search-results overlay temporarily consumes the full viewport while still centering the main search content in the top portion of the screen.
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall density is low and premium: each major section (hero, details band, form) is separated by 48–64px of vertical whitespace, and no panel runs flush to the viewport edges. Inside panels, content is arranged with 16–24px padding and clear typographic hierarchy, ensuring ample breathing room between headings, body text, and controls. The hero summary panel carries only the most essential plan details and key visual cues, avoiding dense tables or long paragraphs. The features and exclusions panel groups information into tidy lists with comfortable line spacing, while the pricing and terms panel focuses on distilled numbers and key terms rather than exhaustive legal copy. The subscription form avoids tight stacking of fields, keeping groups of inputs separated by 16–24px and showing only the necessary fields for the task. Error and confirmation panels use concise messaging with strong hierarchy. The search results overlay maintains plenty of negative space, especially around the search bar and between result groups, to keep the experience calm even with many items.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a classic F/Z-pattern inspired by the mockup. The eye first catches the saturated yellow logo in the top-left of the fixed header, then scans horizontally across the navigation and language selector along the top bar. From there, attention drops diagonally down to the center where the maintenance-plan-summary-panel hero sits, dominating the visual field. The user then follows the vertical flow downward into the two-column band of features/exclusions and pricing/terms, scanning left to right across key details. The eye continues to the centrally placed subscription form as the main call to action. Finally, the floating global-search control in the bottom-right corner offers a secondary attractor at the endpoint of this path, echoing the mockup’s bottom-right utility. When the search is invoked, the visual flow temporarily reorients to the top-center search bar in the overlay, then moves down through the results list.

layout.overall_description: The page sits inside a full-bleed, charcoal-dark background that frames a centered content column roughly 1140–1200px wide. At the top, a low-profile header bar stretches across the screen, with a bold yellow, industrial stencil-style logo on the far left and a clean row of navigation links running toward the center. A compact language selector anchors the top-right, maintaining the balanced, horizontal rhythm. Below this, the main view opens into a cinematic hero zone: a large, horizontally oriented maintenance-plan-summary-panel card occupies about three-quarters of the content width and stands slightly away from the top and side edges, floating on the dark background. This hero panel is the first major focal point, presenting the plan name, key machine association, and a distilled set of metrics in a minimal, typography-led layout that suggests the precision of CNC machinery. As the user scrolls slightly, a second section appears—a full-width band (within the centered column) where the left 60% houses the features-and-exclusions panel and the right 40% contains the pricing-and-terms panel, both appearing as separate but visually coordinated dark cards with subtle borders and equal top alignment. Below this, separated by generous vertical space, a standalone subscription form panel spans roughly 70–80% of the column and is centered, reinforcing it as the primary action area while still being surrounded by comfortable negative space. Conditionally, an error panel can occupy the hero zone when the plan fails to load, or a confirmation panel can replace the form once the subscription is submitted, each using the same centered, inset card aesthetic. Layered above all of this, a small, circular or pill-shaped search utility floats in the bottom-right corner of the viewport; when activated, a dimmed overlay and top-centered search panel take over, letting the user search globally without leaving the industrial, minimal shell of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the full-screen dark shell, the fixed-height header at the very top, and the centered content column beneath it. The header stretches full width, with a maximum inner width aligned to the underlying grid; it is anchored firmly to the top edge and sits about 80–96px tall (roughly 10–15% of a typical desktop viewport), leaving the rest of the viewport for scrolled content. Immediately below the header, the controller centers all main components within a content container roughly 1140–1200px wide, with 10–15% of the viewport width left as dark side margins. Vertically, it stacks sections in order: hero (summary or error), details band (features and pricing), and call-to-action area (subscription form or confirmation), each separated by 48–64px of vertical space. The controller also manages layering, ensuring that floating utilities such as the global search control and overlays like the search results panel sit above this scrollable column while not breaking the grid’s visual alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines a scrollable main container with a fixed-position header; manages z-index so that floating and overlay components (search utilities, error/confirmation overlays) can appear above the main vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The maintenance-plan-summary-panel occupies the hero position directly beneath the header as the first and most visually dominant content block. It is centered horizontally within the content column and spans about 70–80% of the column width, leaving equal margins of dark background on the left and right—approximately 10–15% of the total viewport width per side. Vertically, there is a generous 48–64px gap between the bottom edge of the header and the top edge of this panel, creating the feeling that the panel is framed within the dark shell rather than pressed against the header. The panel itself is a horizontally oriented, low-profile card with slightly lighter charcoal background and a subtle border or shadow to separate it from the backdrop. Its height is substantial—on desktop it can take up roughly half to two-thirds of the remaining viewport height so that, along with the header, the hero content nearly fills the first screen. Internally, content is aligned with a 12-column grid: primary plan information (name, machine model, plan tier) sits toward the left, roughly the first 7–8 columns, while key metrics or a small product image/graphic may occupy the right 4–5 columns, maintaining a clean visual hierarchy. There is 24–32px of internal padding on all sides and clear spacing between headline, subtext, and key stats to keep the hero area spacious and cinematic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll; acts as the top anchor of the content flow and is visually treated as the hero section. On smaller screens it can stretch closer to full width while maintaining top and side insets.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The maintenance-plan-features-and-exclusions panel sits below the hero summary as part of a two-column details band. This band is placed roughly 48–64px beneath the bottom edge of the summary panel to preserve the spacious vertical rhythm. Within the centered content column, the band spans the full width, but the features-and-exclusions panel occupies the left portion—about 60% of the available width. It is top-aligned with the pricing-and-terms panel on its right, creating a unified horizontal stripe. The panel is a rectangular card with a slightly lighter dark tone and subtle border, consistent with the hero panel styling but scaled down. Internally, content is arranged in a clean, list-like structure with headings for "Included" and "Excluded" near the top, each section having comfortable vertical spacing (16–24px) between bullets or rows. On taller viewports, this panel may be taller than the pricing panel; its bottom edge drops lower, but the spacing between this band and the next section (the subscription form) is still maintained at a minimum of 48px from the lowest card edge. On narrower screens, this panel stacks above the pricing-and-terms panel, both still centered and maintaining the same width logic (around 80–90% of the screen width).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll; on wider screens it forms the left column of a two-column band, and on smaller screens it reflows to stack above the pricing panel while maintaining generous vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The maintenance-plan-pricing-and-terms panel is positioned to the immediate right of the features-and-exclusions panel within the same horizontal band. It occupies roughly 40% of the content column width, aligned to the right edge of the band, with a small gutter (24–32px) separating it from the left panel to maintain visual clarity. The top edges of both panels are aligned so that the band reads as a single, cohesive section. This card has the same dark, minimal styling as the other panels, with 24–32px internal padding and a slight border or shadow. The layout inside the panel is vertically stacked: prominent pricing numbers or tiers at the top, followed by key contract terms (duration, visit frequency, response time) in short, well-spaced lines, and any compact legal note at the bottom in smaller type. The overall height is visually balanced with the features panel, but it can be slightly shorter; the difference is absorbed by the clear space separating this band from the subscription form below. On smaller viewports, this panel appears directly beneath the features-and-exclusions panel with 24–32px vertical spacing, still centered and using roughly 80–90% of the available width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll; participates in a responsive two-column-to-single-column transition with the features-and-exclusions panel, preserving the overall band structure across breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The maintenance-subscription-form sits in its own section below the details band, clearly separated from it by 48–64px of vertical space. It is centered horizontally within the content column and spans about 70–80% of the available width, leaving generous dark margins on both sides to echo the hero’s framing and emphasize the form as a deliberate step. The form is contained within a rectangular, dark card whose styling matches the other panels but may have a slightly stronger border or subtle highlight to signal its interactive nature. The form fields are arranged in a vertically stacked layout with logical grouping: customer details, machine details, and schedule/preferences, each grouping separated by 24–32px. Individual fields have 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside each control, consistent with the spacing system. The primary submit button is aligned to the right edge of the form content, near the bottom, creating a subtle Z-shaped path from the form title at the top-left through the fields and ending on the bottom-right button. There is at least 48px of clear dark space below the form card before any footer or page end, so the form never feels crowded against the bottom of the screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll; acts as the main call-to-action region. On smaller devices, it can expand closer to full width, but still retains some side padding from the viewport edges to preserve the minimal, premium feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The maintenance-subscription-confirmation-panel appears in the same vertical zone as the subscription form, effectively replacing it when a subscription is successfully submitted. It preserves the same width (about 70–80% of the content column) and centered alignment, as well as similar vertical spacing above and below, so the overall layout does not shift dramatically. The panel is a dark card with slightly more pronounced visual accents than the data panels—such as a bold yellow accent line or icon in the top-left—to signal success while staying within the industrial, minimal aesthetic. Inside, the message is concise and well-spaced: a prominent confirmation heading at the top-left, a short summary of the plan and start date beneath, and secondary actions (e.g., "Download confirmation", "View all plans") arranged horizontally or in a small vertical group near the bottom-right. The content has ample vertical padding (24–32px), and plenty of negative space so the state change feels calm and intentional rather than noisy. When visible, this panel takes the place of the form as the key focal point in the lower half of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll but conditionally rendered in place of the subscription form. It does not overlay the entire page; instead, it occupies the same structural slot to avoid disrupting the established flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-load-error-panel occupies the hero region when the maintenance plan fails to load, taking the place of the maintenance-plan-summary-panel. It appears directly beneath the header, centered within the content column, and spans roughly 60–70% of the column width—slightly narrower than the hero summary to subtly signal an exceptional state without breaking the layout. There is the same 48–64px gap between the header and the top of this panel, and its bottom edge sets the top boundary for any fallback content or guidance below. The panel is styled as a dark card with a more pronounced yellow warning accent (icon or left border) to differentiate it from normal data panels, while still respecting the minimal, industrial look. The error message is short and clearly legible, with a bold heading, a one- or two-line explanation, and recovery actions such as "Retry" or "Back to plans" aligned toward the bottom-right. If the error is critical and no plan data is available, other content panels (features, pricing, subscription form) are suppressed or visually de-emphasized, ensuring that this error card becomes the dominant element in the main viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll but conditionally replaces the hero summary region. It does not float; instead, it anchors the top of the content area to ensure the user encounters the error message immediately after the header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global-search-bar manifests as a compact floating control that sits above the main content in the bottom-right corner of the viewport, echoing the mockup’s small bottom-right utility. By default, it appears as a circular or pill-shaped dark button with a search icon, positioned approximately 24–32px away from both the right and bottom edges. Its size is modest—occupying only a small fraction of the screen—so it never competes with the hero or main content for attention but remains easily discoverable. When activated, this control can expand into a slightly larger pill or immediately trigger the global-search-results-overlay, where a full search input is shown; in both cases, it remains vertically and horizontally offset from the edges to avoid feeling cramped. The floating control is visible on top of all scrollable content, maintaining a consistent position as the user scrolls through the hero, details band, and form sections. On mobile, it may shrink slightly in size but retains the same relative bottom-right placement.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position floating element anchored to the bottom-right of the viewport, always visible above the main scrollable content. It serves both as an entry point to the search overlay and as a persistent utility, mirroring the mockup’s bottom-right button behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global-search-results-overlay sits on top of the entire page content whenever search is invoked, dimming the background while retaining the dark, industrial palette. It covers the full viewport, extending from the top edge of the header down to the bottom of the screen (or, if implemented above the header, it visually blends with the header to form a continuous dark layer). At the top center of this overlay, a wide search bar appears—spanning about 60% of the viewport width on desktop—with its left and right edges aligning roughly with the main content column below. The search bar is positioned 48–64px below the top edge of the viewport, giving it breathing room and mimicking the inset framing of the hero content. Below the search bar, search results are presented in a vertically scrolling list within the overlay, centered within the same width as the search bar and separated into clearly spaced groups or rows, each with 16–24px of vertical spacing.
